posts.summary: Luu argues that Verilog development desperately needs basic static analysis tools to catch common bugs early, despite the industry's lack of such tooling. He describes how a simple custom linter based on naming conventions (for pipeline stages, clock domains, and module boundaries) found 7 bugs in 30 minutes, demonstrating that you don't need a PhD to build useful static analysis for hardware design. The post challenges the notion that static analysis is too hard, showing that simple checks can prevent costly errors that take hours to debug in simulation or days to discover in timing reports.
key_points: Basic static analysis for Verilog is valuable but uncommon in the industry
Simple naming convention checks can catch bugs that take hours or days to discover through traditional methods
Cross-clock-domain accesses without synchronization can cause random errors, high power consumption, and physical damage
Referencing distant module signals without registering creates timing issues that take days to detect
A 30-minute custom linter found 7 bugs with only 2 false positives, proving static analysis doesn't require advanced expertise
money_quotes: Accessing a signal in a different clock domain without synchronization is like accessing a data structure from multiple threads without synchronization. Sort of. But worse. Much worse.
If you took a high-power chip from the late 90s and removed the heat sink, it would melt itself into the socket, even under normal operation.
I'd been feeling naked at my new gig, coding Verilog without any sort of static checking.

It turns out that don't need much of a parser, and all the things I've talked about are simple enough that half an hour after starting, I had a tool that found seven bugs, with only two false positives.
Huh. That wasn't so bad. I've now graduated to junior PL troll.
posts.key_insight: The hardware industry lacks basic static analysis tooling that software engineers take for granted, but simple custom linters can find critical bugs in minutes that would otherwise take days to discover through simulation or synthesis.

posts.summary: Verilog, the dominant hardware design language in the US, was originally designed as a simulation language and is fundamentally ill-suited for describing actual hardware. This mismatch means that the vast majority of syntactically valid Verilog code produces either nonsensical or unsynthesizable results, requiring engineers to mentally picture hardware first and then awkwardly translate it into Verilog's C-like constructs. Despite numerous attempts at better alternatives (Haskell-based languages like Lava and Bluespec, or Scala-based Chisel), adoption has been stymied by politics and industry conservatism rather than technical merit.
key_points: Verilog was designed as a simulation language, not a hardware description language, making most valid code untranslatable to actual hardware
Synthesis tools pattern-match Verilog to hardware constructs, often producing nothing or incorrect results without meaningful errors
Hardware engineers must first imagine the hardware they want, then figure out how to describe it in Verilog's counter-intuitive constructs
Alternative languages with better type systems and semantics exist (Bluespec, Chisel) but face political resistance rather than technical objections
The industry resists change due to language preferences (aversion to Haskell) and vendor track records of overpromising on high-level synthesis
money_quotes: As a software engineer, how would you feel if 99% of valid Java code ended up being translated to something that produced random results, even though tests pass on the untranslated Java code?

We hardware folks are so used to the vast majority of legal Verilog constructs producing unsynthesizable garbage that we don't find it the least bit surprising that you not only do you not get compile-time errors, you don't even get run-time errors, from writing naive Verilog code.

To write Verilog that will produce correct hardware, you have to first picture the hardware you want to produce. Then, you have to figure out how to describe that in this weird C-like simulation language.
Scala has managed to bring the modern world (in terms of type systems) to more programmers than ML, Ocaml, Haskell, Agda, etc., combined. Perhaps the same will be true in the hardware world.

It's not as high level, but it's the only hardware description language with a modern type system that I've been able to discuss with hardware folks without people objecting that Haskell is a bad idea.
posts.key_insight: Verilog's fundamental design as a simulation language rather than a hardware description language creates a massive impedance mismatch that forces engineers to work around the language rather than with it, yet better alternatives fail to gain adoption due to political and cultural resistance rather than technical deficiencies.

posts.summary: Luu argues that open source projects with large backlogs of ignored pull requests create a systemic barrier to new contributors, even when individual maintainer behavior is reasonable. He describes abandoning contributions to a project after seeing dozens of good patches languishing ignored on the mailing list. The post calls on maintainers with commit access to take ownership of the pull request process or stop soliciting contributions, emphasizing that response time matters more than perfection.
key_points: Many open source projects have massive backlogs of ignored pull requests (116+ in the example)
Individual maintainers may be reasonable, but collectively create a hostile environment for new contributors
New contributors often give up after a few ignored patches with no guidance on where to go next
Fast response time (hours, not weeks) makes a huge difference in encouraging contributions
Maintainers should own the PR process or stop asking for contributions in their repo description
money_quotes: This behavior, eminently reasonable on the part of any individual, results in a systemic failure, a tax on new open source contributors.
It doesn't take egregious individual behavior to create a hostile environment.
I saw a wasteland of good patches that were completely ignored, where the submitter would ping the list a couple times and then give up.
Did it seem worth spending a week to disentangle our IP from the project in order to submit a set of patches that would, in all likelihood, get ignored? No.
If you have commit access to a project that has this problem, please own the process for incoming pull requests (or don't ask for pull requests in your repo description).
posts.key_insight: Open source maintainers must either actively manage their pull request queue or stop soliciting contributions—ignoring contributions creates a systemic barrier to participation that discourages future contributors regardless of how reasonable individual maintainers' constraints are.

posts.summary: Social news sites like HN and Reddit have a fundamental threshold problem where articles that don't get early votes never reach the front page, while those that do get disproportionate exposure. This creates inefficiency where content quality doesn't correlate well with visibility - success depends heavily on random early voters. The proposed solution is counterintuitive: add random noise to article rankings to smooth out the discontinuity between front-page success and obscurity, similar to probabilistic counting algorithms in computer science.
key_points: Articles on HN face a critical threshold - those at N-1 votes have ~60% chance of reaching front page with 100+ votes, 40% chance of getting only 2 votes total
Low-traffic sites have the inverse problem - posting instantly to front page displaces quality content with uninteresting submissions
Current ad-hoc fixes (Reddit's rising page, HN's manual penalties) don't solve the root problem and create workaround wars
Adding random noise to rankings can smooth the discontinuity and give articles exposure proportional to their actual quality
HN eventually tried randomization but settled on human curation (second chance queue) which works better than pure algorithmic approaches
money_quotes: It's non-optimal that an article that will receive 50 votes in expectation has a 60% chance of getting 100+ votes, and a 40% chance of getting 2 votes.
By adding a small amount of random noise to the rank of an article, we can smooth out the discontinuity between making it onto the front page and languishing in obscurity.
That's the problem with 'simple' ad hoc solutions.
Adding noise to smooth out a discontinuity is a common trick when you can settle for an approximate result.
Most people seem to upvote and comment based on the title of the article and not the content
posts.key_insight: Social news aggregation suffers from a threshold effect where early randomness has outsized impact on visibility, and while adding algorithmic randomness seems counterintuitive, human curation ultimately proves more effective than pure algorithmic solutions.

posts.summary: Hardware development, particularly CPU design, requires decades of accumulated experience and cannot be successfully executed by inexperienced teams, no matter how smart. Unlike software startups where young founders often succeed, every CPU startup that relied heavily on inexperienced engineers has failed, burning through hundreds of millions in funding. Physical work involves tacit knowledge that cannot be derived from first principles or learned from books alone, as illustrated by South Korea's multi-decade journey to build a shipbuilding industry despite access to expert consultants and detailed plans.
key_points: Successful CPU teams typically have core members who have worked together for decades, often spanning multiple companies
CPU startups funded with inexperienced teams universally fail, despite raising tens to hundreds of millions of dollars
Software allows for rapid iteration and real-time fixes, while hardware mistakes are extremely costly (minor bugs cost months and millions, fundamental flaws cost years and hundreds of millions)

Physical manufacturing requires tacit knowledge that cannot be learned from books or derived from first principles, as demonstrated by South Korea's failed attempt to build ships using Scottish expertise
Age discrimination doesn't exist in hardware design because experience is so critical - teams are typically composed of people in their 40s-60s
money_quotes: Every single such startup I know of failed
Physical work isn't the kind of thing you can derive from first principles, no matter how smart you are.
When the unpracticed Koreans tried to build ships using Scottish plans and detailed step-by-step directions, the result was two ship halves that didn't quite fit together and sunk when assembled.
With a large ship, 'minor' fixes can take days or weeks, and a fundamental flaw means that your ship sinks and you've lost half a year of work and tens of millions of dollars.
You need enough wisdom and experience to avoid common mistakes entirely – especially the ones that can't be fixed.
posts.key_insight: The unforgiving nature of physical mistakes in hardware development makes experience indispensable, as there is no undo button and fundamental errors cannot be iteratively fixed like in software.

posts.summary: Luu recounts an interview where he disagreed with an interviewer who believed PCA (Principal Component Analysis) was a universal solution for all classification problems. Using empirical examples comparing LSA (a linear dimensionality reduction technique) to deep learning autoencoders for text classification, he demonstrates that linear methods have fundamental limitations when dealing with nuanced, complex patterns. The post critiques the broader trend of data scientists mechanically applying a small set of linear techniques to every problem, regardless of whether those methods are appropriate for the problem's inherent structure.
key_points: PCA and similar linear dimensionality reduction methods cannot effectively capture complex, non-linear patterns in data
Deep learning methods significantly outperform linear techniques like LSA for nuanced classification tasks
Many self-described data scientists overuse a small toolkit of linear methods inappropriately
Real-world system performance contains non-linear behavior that cannot be captured by simple regression models
Engineers should apply the same skepticism to generic data science approaches as they do to management consultants
money_quotes: When all you have are linear hammers, non-linear screws look a lot like nails.
But data science is new and hot enough that people get a pass when they call themselves data scientists instead of technology consultants.
I keep seeing people try to apply the same four linear methods to every problem in sight.
Real system performance is full of non-linear cliffs, the sort of thing where you can't just apply a queuing theory model, let alone a linear regression model.
posts.key_insight: The popularity of data science has led many practitioners to mechanically apply simple linear techniques to complex problems that fundamentally require non-linear approaches, producing poor results with misplaced confidence.

posts.summary: This post explains how cache line alignment can dramatically hurt performance through conflict misses. When data is page-aligned (or aligned to large powers of 2), addresses map to the same cache sets, artificially limiting cache capacity - for example, reducing a 32kB L1 cache to effectively only 8 useful locations. By misaligning data across different cache lines, the same hardware can utilize 512 locations in L1, yielding substantial performance improvements. The post uses empirical benchmarks and detailed cache organization analysis to demonstrate this counterintuitive result: 'nice' alignment can be disastrous.
key_points: Page-aligned or power-of-2 aligned data causes conflict misses that prevent full cache utilization
On Sandy Bridge, page-aligned accesses can only use 8 of 512 possible L1 cache lines, while misaligned accesses can use all 512
Cache sets are determined by the middle bits of addresses - alignment zeroes out these bits, forcing all accesses into the same set
The performance penalty persists across the cache hierarchy (L1, L2, L3), with different working set size thresholds
Misaligned data is noticeably faster than page-aligned data for working sets between 8-512 in the benchmark
money_quotes: If this benchmark seems contrived, it actually comes from a real world example of the disastrous performance implications of using nice power of 2 alignment, or page alignment in an actual system

If we use the cache optimally, we can store 32,768 items. But, since we're accessing things that are page (4k) aligned, we effectively lose the bottom log₂(4k) = 12 bits, which means that every access falls into the same set, and we can only loop through 8 things before our working set is too large to fit in the L1!
Not only will aligning to any large power of 2 cause a problem, generating addresses with a power of 2 offset from each other will cause the same problem

Nowadays, the importance of caches is well understood enough that, when I'm asked to look at a cache related performance bug, it's usually due to the kind of thing we just talked about: conflict misses that prevent us from using our full cache effectively
posts.key_insight: Counterintuitively, 'clean' power-of-2 memory alignment can cripple performance by forcing all accesses into the same cache sets, reducing effective cache capacity by orders of magnitude compared to deliberately misaligned data.

posts.summary: The author examines whether programmers actually need math by reviewing their nine undergraduate math courses and finding that seven of nine have proven directly useful in non-math-oriented programming work. While acknowledging some selection bias (math-heavy companies are more likely to hire them), they conclude that math has provided valuable tools even in low-level systems work through combinatorial proofs, numerical approximations, and algorithmic insights. The recommendation is nuanced: math is worthwhile if you enjoy it and it may bias your career toward interesting mathematical work, but it's not the most efficient path if your sole goal is transitioning from academia to programming.
key_points: Seven out of nine undergraduate math courses have had direct applications in non-math-oriented programming work
Calculus and linear algebra were used daily for months or years in real physical systems and algorithms
Even in low-level systems work, math concepts like combinatorial proofs and numerical approximations frequently prove useful
Selection bias exists - companies doing math-heavy work are more likely to hire math-oriented programmers
Math is valuable if you enjoy it, but not the most efficient use of time if your only goal is career transition to programming
money_quotes: I've often regretted not taking a class seriously because I've later found myself in a situation where I could have put the skills to good use, I've never said to myself 'what a waste of time it was to learn that fundamental mathematical concept and use it enough to that I truly understand it.'
It's not uncommon to be find a simple combinatorial proof to show that a solution that seems too stupid to be correct is actually optimal, or correct with high probability

I'm not sure how to reconcile my experience with the common sentiment that, outside of a handful of esoteric areas like computer graphics and machine learning, there is no need to understand textbook algorithms, let alone more abstract concepts like math

I've gotten more bang for the buck from understanding algorithms and computer architecture than from understanding math, but I really enjoy math and I'm glad that knowing a bit of it has biased my career towards more mathematical jobs
posts.key_insight: Math knowledge proves surprisingly useful across programming domains, not just in obviously mathematical work, though its value depends on whether you genuinely enjoy it rather than viewing it purely as career preparation.

posts.summary: Luu argues that engineering education systematically fails students by refusing to teach fundamental debugging skills, instead treating high failure rates as a badge of honor that weeds out those who "can't hack it." He recounts discovering systematic debugging through necessity during a divider project, realizing that many struggling students weren't lacking intelligence but simply had never been taught methodical problem-solving approaches. The education system's pride in its "leaky STEM pipeline" is a manufactured crisis—schools could easily teach these skills but choose not to, preferring hazing rituals over actual education.
key_points: Many engineering students fail not due to lack of ability, but because fundamental skills like systematic debugging are never taught
The "Feynman Method" (think really hard) works for simple problems but fails on complex real-world projects without systematic debugging techniques
Educators often rationalize high failure rates as evidence that "some students just can't hack it" rather than acknowledging instructional failures
A simple half-hour lesson on systematic debugging could save a significant fraction of students who wash out and switch majors
The more talented the engineer, the more likely they are to hit a debugging wall outside of school if they've never learned systematic approaches
money_quotes: Why do we leave material out of classes and then fail students who can't figure out that material for themselves?
The more talented the engineer, the more likely they are to hit a debugging wall outside of school.
For all the high-level talk about how we need to plug the leaks in our STEM education pipeline, not only are we not plugging the holes, we're proud of how fast the pipeline is leaking.
I was told in no uncertain terms that it would be a waste of time because some people just can't hack it in engineering.
It should be possible to systematically apply a mechanical technique repeatedly to find the source of our problems.
posts.key_insight: Engineering education's high failure rates are a manufactured crisis resulting from deliberate choices not to teach fundamental debugging skills, not evidence that struggling students lack aptitude.

posts.summary: In 1983, University of Wisconsin researchers published database benchmarks showing Oracle performed dramatically worse than competitors—sometimes 30-100x slower. Oracle's Larry Ellison reportedly tried to have the lead researcher fired, then banned benchmarking in Oracle's EULA. This spawned "DeWitt Clauses" that spread across the software industry, preventing customers from publicly comparing performance. The post argues this anti-competitive practice harms transparency and suggests creating anonymous benchmark markets to circumvent these restrictions.
key_points: 1983 Wisconsin benchmark showed Oracle was 30-100x slower than competitors on many operations
Larry Ellison allegedly tried to fire the researcher and added anti-benchmarking clauses to Oracle's EULA
Major commercial databases (Oracle, Microsoft SQL Server) now forbid publishing benchmarks; only IBM allows them
Anti-benchmarking "DeWitt Clauses" have spread from databases to compilers and cloud services
The practice prevents customers from making informed purchasing decisions based on actual performance data
money_quotes: Larry Ellison wasn't happy with the results and it's said that he tried to have DeWitt fired.
Over the years, many major commercial database vendors added a license clause that made benchmarking their database illegal.

This gives people ammunition for a meta-argument that IBM probably delivers better performance than either Oracle or Microsoft, since they're the only company that's not scared of people publishing benchmark results.
There's at least one cloud service that disallows not only publishing benchmarks, but even "competitive benchmarking", running benchmarks to see how well the competition does.
It would be worse to have public benchmarks than to have it be known that they're afraid of public benchmarks.
posts.key_insight: When vendors prohibit benchmarking in their licenses, it's effectively an admission that their performance is so poor that public comparison would be more damaging than the reputational cost of appearing afraid of transparency.

posts.summary: Binary editing is a practical technique for patching compiled programs when source code isn't available or recompiling isn't feasible. The author argues that while this seems shocking to developers who think exclusively in terms of source code, it's natural for those who understand that processors simply execute bits. Through concrete examples - from fixing bugs in closed-source software to handling emergency deployment situations - Luu demonstrates that binary editing is often the quickest path to solving real-world problems.
key_points: Many developers are shocked by binary editing because they think only in terms of source code transformations, not the underlying processor instructions
Binary editing is essential for workarounds with non-Intel/AMD x86 processors that incorrectly check CPUID
Emergency situations (like the auto-update versioning mishap) could be resolved in minutes with binary patching vs hours of rebuild/release cycles
Basic binary editing requires only simple tools like a hex editor and disassembler, not expensive software like IDA
Common techniques include changing comparison values, patching in NOPs (0x90), and redirecting jumps
money_quotes: If you have the first view, breaking out a hex editor to modify a program is the action of a deranged lunatic. If you have the second view, editing binaries is the most natural thing in the world.
You can try filing a bug, but it's much easier to just edit your binaries.
If it had occurred to anyone to edit the binary to increment the version number, they could have pushed out a good update in a minute instead of an hour
Editing binaries is a trick that comes in handy a few times a year. You don't often need to, but when you do, there's no alternative.
posts.key_insight: Understanding programs as processor instructions rather than just source code transformations unlocks powerful problem-solving capabilities for emergencies and workarounds that would otherwise require lengthy recompilation processes.

posts.summary: Dan Luu systematically debunks a viral Quartz article claiming there's no gender pay gap in tech by carefully analyzing the very sources it cites. He demonstrates that the AAUW study cited actually shows women earning 77-88% of what men earn in tech fields, with one-third of the gap unexplainable by any factor except gender. The Quartz article selectively cherry-picked data, misrepresented findings, and ignored contradictory evidence from its own sources to support a predetermined conclusion.
key_points: The AAUW study shows women in computer/information sciences earn 77% of what men earn, and 88% in engineering
One-third of the pay gap cannot be explained by hours worked, employment type, or school quality—it appears attributable to gender alone
The study explicitly refutes the idea that women simply prefer lower-paying jobs, citing legal cases showing discriminatory placement
The Quartz article selectively controlled for factors that reduce the gap while ignoring factors like grades that would increase it
Supporting evidence was cherry-picked, misrepresented, or cited the wrong sources entirely
money_quotes: The claims are awfully strong, considering that the main study cited only looked at people who graduated with a B.S. exactly one year ago, not to mention the fact that the study makes literally the opposite claim.
Women do not, in fact, just want lower paying jobs; this is, once again, diametrically opposed to the claims in the Quartz article.
It's clear that the author of the Quartz piece had an agenda in mind, picked out evidence that supported that agenda, and wrote a blog post.
If this is how 'digitally native news' works, I'm opting out.
To find a figure that supports the author's agenda, the author had to not only look at part time workers, but only look at part-time Asian women, and then round .3% up to 1%.
posts.key_insight: Viral tech journalism often works backwards from a desired conclusion, selectively cherry-picking and misrepresenting data from authoritative sources to support predetermined narratives, even when those sources explicitly contradict the article's claims.

posts.summary: Software companies should use the analytics data they already collect to automatically detect bugs, rather than relying solely on traditional testing. By analyzing user behavior patterns—like conversion rates through UI flows, unusual action sequences, or actions followed by disconnections—teams can identify bugs that users encounter in production. This approach mirrors how hardware engineers use performance counters to detect design flaws, and could catch a significant portion of bugs with minimal additional effort since the data is already being collected for other purposes.
key_points: A third of common bugs could be found automatically using analytics data that companies already collect
Hardware engineering has long used performance counters and analytics to detect design flaws by identifying unexpected patterns
User actions that don't convert (failed flows, immediate undos, disconnections) are strong signals of bugs
Software testing lags decades behind hardware testing in sophistication despite lower cost of software bugs
The same data infrastructure used to optimize clickthrough rates can be repurposed to detect bugs
money_quotes: Unexpected distributions in analytics are a sign of a misunderstanding, which is often a sign of a bug.
But in software land, I'm lucky if a random project I want to contribute to has tests at all.
Following directions at an intersection is a conversion, not deleting an image immediately after pasting it is a conversion, and using a modal dialogue box after opening it up is a conversion.
What do you think the future clickthrough rate is when a user encounters a show-stopping bug that prevents any forward progress on a modal dialogue box?
Everyone knows that adding 200ms of load time can cause 20% of users to close the window. What do you think the effect of exposing them to a bug that takes 5,000ms of user interaction to fix is?
posts.key_insight: Companies obsessively optimize conversion funnels for revenue but ignore that the same analytics infrastructure could automatically detect the bugs that destroy conversion rates far more dramatically than any UI tweak could improve them.

posts.summary: The post demonstrates that intrinsics, despite promises of being a portable and compiler-optimized alternative to hand-written assembly, remain unreliable and often produce suboptimal code. Through a detailed case study of the `popcnt` (population count) instruction, the author shows how naive use of intrinsics can be 3x slower than hand-written assembly due to compiler failures like not unrolling loops, generating backwards accumulation patterns, and not working around known CPU bugs. The analysis reveals that achieving optimal performance still requires manually checking assembly output across compilers and platforms, making intrinsics more work than just writing assembly directly.
key_points: Intrinsics require manual verification of generated assembly across all target compilers and platforms
Naive use of popcnt intrinsic was 3x slower than hand-written assembly on the same hardware
Compilers fail to optimize around known CPU bugs like the popcnt false dependency issue
Loop unrolling and working around CPU errata are necessary to achieve near-theoretical maximum performance
The SSSE3 pshufb implementation appeared faster than popcnt only because of poor intrinsic code generation
money_quotes: The problem is that intrinsics are so unreliable that you have to manually check the result on every platform and every compiler you expect your code to be run on, and then tweak the intrinsics until you get a reasonable result. That's more work than just writing the assembly by hand.
Despite decade old claims that intrinsics can make your life easier, it never seems to work out.

It's easy to see why someone might have benchmarked the same code and decided that popcnt isn't very fast. It's also easy to see why using intrinsics for performance critical code can be a huge time sink.
Clang seems to be calculating things manually rather than using popcnt.
In this case, the hand coded assembly version is about 3x faster than the original intrinsic loop.
posts.key_insight: Intrinsics promise compiler-optimized portable performance but in practice require the same manual verification and platform-specific tweaking as assembly, making them more work rather than less for high-performance code.

posts.summary: This post empirically tests cache eviction policies, particularly comparing LRU (least recently used) to a randomized approach of choosing the LRU between two random cache entries (2-random). Through extensive SPEC CPU benchmark simulations across different cache sizes and hierarchies, Luu demonstrates that while LRU performs better for smaller caches, 2-random actually outperforms LRU for larger caches (especially above 1MB). The counterintuitive finding is rooted in mathematical probability theory about load balancing—choosing between k random options dramatically improves performance with diminishing returns after k=2, making 2-random nearly as effective as full LRU while being cheaper to implement.
key_points: Random eviction degrades gracefully as cache size changes, while LRU can cause a miss every time if a tight loop doesn't fit in cache
2-random (choosing LRU between two random entries) performs within 5-10% of full LRU for small caches and actually beats it for larger caches
The advantage comes from probability theory: choosing the best of k random options gives O(log log n) maximum load versus O(log n) for pure random, with diminishing returns after k=2
Pseudo 3-random (80% accurate two-level tournament) approaches true 2-random performance while being as cheap to implement as pseudo-LRU
LRU works better when miss rates are low and cache sizes are small; 2-random excels when miss rates are high and caches are large
money_quotes: If you have a tight loop, LRU is going to be perfect as long as the loop fits in cache, but it's going to cause a miss every time if the loop doesn't fit. A random eviction policy degrades gracefully as the loop gets too big.

The mathematical intuition is that if we (randomly) throw n balls into n bins, the maximum number of balls in any bin is O(log n / log log n) with high probability, which is pretty much just O(log n). But if (instead of choosing randomly) we choose the least loaded of k random bins, the maximum is O(log log n / log k) with high probability.

Random and FIFO are both strictly worse than either LRU or 2-random. LRU and 2-random are pretty close, with LRU edging out 2-random for the smaller caches and 2-random edging out LRU for the larger caches.
A pseudo 2-random policy can be implemented that barely uses more resources than pseudo-LRU policies, which makes this very cheap compared to DIP.
posts.key_insight: Counterintuitively, making cache eviction decisions based on just two random samples performs nearly as well as tracking full LRU state, and actually beats LRU for large caches—a practical application of the 'power of two choices' theorem showing that a small amount of randomized choice can match expensive deterministic algorithms.

posts.summary: Dan Luu analyzes Travis CI data from 40 popular GitHub projects and finds that most open source projects have surprisingly unreliable builds compared to corporate environments. While 99% uptime (two 9s) would be considered poor in professional settings, only 8 of 40 open source projects achieve this baseline, with 15 projects failing to even reach 90% uptime. This is despite the fact that keeping builds working is technically trivial (the "not rocket science rule") and broken builds waste contributor time just as surely as they waste employee dollars.
key_points: Only 8 of 40 popular open source projects achieve 99% build reliability, which would be considered bad in corporate environments
15 of 40 projects don't even achieve 90% uptime (one 9 of reliability)
Broken builds waste contributor time and reduce conversion rates for potential contributors
The solution is technically trivial but culturally difficult - tools like bors exist but aren't widely adopted
Corporate teams prevent broken builds because managers understand the cost calculation of idle engineers
The sample is biased toward reliable projects (featured on GitHub and using CI), meaning the true state is likely worse
money_quotes: Web programmers are hyper-aware of how 100ms of extra latency on a web page load has a noticeable effect on conversion rate. Well, what's the effect on conversion rate when a potential contributor to your project spends 20 minutes installing dependencies and an hour building your project only to find the build is broken?

Graydon Hoare literally calls keeping a clean build the 'not rocket science rule', and wrote an open source tool (bors) anyone can use to do not-rocket-science. And yet, most open source projects still suffer through broken and failed builds.

At every place I've worked, two 9s of reliability (99% uptime) on the build would be considered bad. That would mean that the build is failing for over three and a half days a year, or seven hours per month.
Instead of wasting dollars, contributor's time is wasted.
This is one of my most maligned blog posts because no one wants to believe the build for their project is broken more often than the build for other projects.
posts.key_insight: Open source projects systematically tolerate levels of build unreliability that would be unacceptable in corporate environments, wasting contributor time despite having trivial technical solutions available, because they don't translate the cost of broken builds into wasted human effort the way businesses translate it to wasted money.

posts.summary: Intel's new CLWB and PCOMMIT instructions address performance bottlenecks that emerge when non-volatile RAM becomes fast enough that OS overhead and cache hierarchy impedance dominate transaction costs. The post explains how next-generation storage (PCM, 3D XPoint) creates an Amdahl's law problem where traditional memory caching policies (writeback, writethrough, etc.) impose orders-of-magnitude slowdowns when persistence guarantees are needed. CLWB allows cache writeback without invalidation, while PCOMMIT ensures stores are committed to persistent storage, potentially solving the impedance mismatch between x86's cache hierarchy and NVRAM without requiring cache flushes everywhere.
key_points: Next-gen NVRAM will be fast enough that syscall/OS overhead becomes more expensive than actual disk access
Traditional cache policies create massive overhead when persistence visibility is required - WB with visibility constraints can be within 2x of running without caches entirely
Research shows hardware+DMA costs are dwarfed by OS overhead even with significant optimizations (62% latency reduction still leaves majority as overhead)
CLWB writes back dirty cache lines without invalidating them (unlike CLFLUSH) and is ordered with SFENCE
PCOMMIT ensures accepted memory stores to persistent ranges become power-failure protected
money_quotes: next generation storage technologies (PCM, 3d XPoint, etc.), will be fast enough that syscall and other OS overhead can be more expensive than the actual cost of the disk access
Despite not being able to get rid of all of the overhead, they get pretty significant speedups, both on small microbenchmarks and real code.
WB with visibility constraints is significantly slower than the other alternatives. It's a multiple order of magnitude slowdown over WB when writes don't have to be ordered and flushed.
The performance of regular WB memory can be terribly slow: within a factor of 2 of the performance of running without caches.
How much CLWB and PCOMMIT actually improve performance will be up to their implementations. It will be interesting to benchmark these and see how they do.
posts.key_insight: Fast NVRAM creates an Amdahl's law bottleneck where cache coherency protocols and OS overhead become the dominant cost, requiring new CPU instructions like CLWB and PCOMMIT to maintain performance while guaranteeing persistence.

posts.summary: Dan Luu systematically reviews empirical studies on static versus dynamic typing, finding that most controlled experiments show very small effects, if any. The studies suffer from serious methodological flaws including trivial toy tasks, student populations, language familiarity bias, and flawed data classification. While some researchers make strong claims about type systems improving code quality, the empirical evidence is weak, limited in scope, and often contradictory, with programmer variance typically dwarfing any language effects.
key_points: Most controlled experiments find minimal differences between static and dynamic typing, with effects smaller than variance between individual programmers

The highly-cited GitHub study by Ray et al. has fundamental flaws including misclassified projects (Qt translation files as TypeScript, shell scripts as Perl) and confuses different language properties
Studies use trivial tasks (median completion times in hundreds of seconds, tasks avoiding loops/recursion as 'too complex') that don't reflect real-world development
Hanenberg's studies show some benefits for static types in specific narrow contexts but withStudent populations and artificially stripped documentation
Case studies on bug-finding (Python-to-Haskell, Erlang static analysis) show some bugs caught but don't establish comparative advantage over other techniques
No studies examine experienced programmers on non-trivial projects with modern tooling, maintenance tasks, or familiar codebases
money_quotes: I think programmers who doubt that type systems help are basically the tech equivalent of an anti-vaxxer.
These are C++ projects. So the intermediate result appears to not be that TypeScript is reliable, but that projects mis-identified as TypeScript are reliable.
The variance between programmers was still much greater than the variance between languages in all but two tasks.
If you look at the internet commentary on these studies, most of them are passed around to justify one viewpoint or another.
If the strongest statement you can make for your position is that there's no empirical evidence against the position, that's not much of a position.
posts.key_insight: Despite strong ideological claims on both sides of the static-versus-dynamic typing debate, the actual empirical evidence is weak, methodologically flawed, and shows effects that are typically smaller than individual programmer variation—yet researchers and practitioners continue to cite these studies as definitive proof of their preferred position.

posts.summary: Dan Luu documents encountering approximately 80 new bugs in a single week across various software platforms (Ubuntu, GitHub, Julia, Google products, games, etc.) to demonstrate how pervasive software quality issues are. He argues that while everyone knows they should write more tests, the conventional approach of manual unit testing is too labor-intensive to be practical, especially given that complexity doesn't scale linearly with code size. Instead, he advocates for property-based testing, fuzzing, and other automated testing approaches that can find dozens of bugs in hours rather than requiring years of manual test-writing effort.
key_points: Software is riddled with bugs - the author encountered 80 new bugs in one week across common platforms
Julia has only 18k lines of test code for 108k lines of source code, far below what would be expected for even a prototype
Manual unit testing is impractical - it would take 2-5 person-years to bring Julia's test coverage to prototype level
Automated testing approaches like fuzzing can find 20-30 bugs in an hour of development time
CPUs can execute 200 billion instructions per minute vs humans typing at 100 words per minute - automated testing is fundamentally more efficient
money_quotes: If I had to guess, I'd say I probably work around hundreds of bugs in an average week, and thousands in a bad week.
You can throw together an absurd hack in an hour and it will turn out to be pretty useful.
You might be able to hit 100 words a minute by typing, but your CPU can easily execute 200 billion instructions a minute. It's no contest.

There's a tragedy of the commons effect here. If there are only a few bugs, developers are likely to fix the bugs they run across. But if there are so many bugs that making a dent is hopeless, a lot of people won't bother.
Writing tests is like writing documentation. Everyone already knows you should do it. Telling people they should do it adds zero information.
posts.key_insight: Since developers won't invest the massive effort required for comprehensive manual testing, the path forward is automated testing approaches like fuzzing that can find orders of magnitude more bugs per hour of developer time invested.

posts.summary: Dan Luu applies TF-IDF (term frequency-inverse document frequency) analysis to Linux kernel commit messages from 2005 onwards to identify what different contributors work on. Simple word frequency counts are dominated by common words like 'the' and 'to', and manually filtering stop words still leaves too much noise. By applying TF-IDF to weight terms by how distinctive they are to each contributor, he successfully reveals clear patterns: for example, Takashi Iwai works on ALSA audio codecs, David Miller on SPARC64, and Ralf Baechle on MIPS.
key_points: Simple word frequency analysis of commit messages is dominated by common words and provides little useful information
Standard stop-word filtering doesn't work well without manual intervention for this dataset, as it misses domain-specific noise like 'Signed-off-by'
TF-IDF successfully identifies distinctive terms for each contributor, revealing clear specialization patterns (e.g., tiwai→ALSA, davem→sparc64, ralf→mips)
Very few Linux contributors work on POSIX compliance - you can count the individuals who mentioned it in commit messages
The post is an experiment in faster blogging (30-90 minute posts) to reinforce learning from a Mining Massive Data Sets course
money_quotes: Alright, so their most frequently used words are to, alsa, the, and the. Turns out, Takashi Iwai (tiwai) often works on audio (alsa), and by going down the list we can see that David Miller's (davem) fifth most frequently used term is sparc64, which is a pretty good indicator that he does a lot of sparc work. But the table is mostly noise.

I actually don't understand why it's standard to take the log here. Sometimes you want to take the log so you can work with smaller numbers, or so that you can convert a bunch of multiplies into a bunch of adds, but neither of those is true here.
Relatively, almost no one works on POSIX compliance. You can actually count the individual people who mentioned POSIX in commit messages.

TF-IDF is sometimes useful and incredibly easy to apply. You should use it when you should use it (when you want to see what words distinguish different documents/people from each other) and you shouldn't use it when you shouldn't use it (when you want to see what's common to documents/people).
posts.key_insight: TF-IDF provides a simple, effective technique for identifying what distinguishes individual contributors in large collaborative projects by weighting terms by their uniqueness rather than just their frequency.

posts.summary: Dan Luu optimizes his Octopress static blog hosted on GitHub Pages, achieving a 25-50x speedup by removing unnecessary JavaScript, fonts, and CSS. Starting with a 12-second load time, he systematically eliminates bloat like unused modernizr/jQuery dependencies, custom fonts, and unminified CSS to reach 400ms. The post demonstrates how someone with zero web development knowledge can achieve GPU-to-ASIC-level performance gains simply by removing the default cruft that comes with popular frameworks, raising questions about why widely-used platforms ship with such massive performance problems.
key_points: Moved JavaScript from head to footer, eliminating 4-second render-blocking delay
Removed unused Octopress.js features (flash videos, delicious integration) and all JavaScript since the only feature was an unused sidebar toggle
Eliminated custom web fonts which comprised 43.8% of page payload despite images being negligible
Minified and inlined CSS, removing support for obsolete browsers and unused features, cutting CSS by half
Switched to Cloudflare with 'Cache Everything' page rule since GitHub Pages doesn't cache HTML for bare domains without DNS alias records
money_quotes: How is it possible that someone with zero knowledge of web development can get that kind of speedup by watching one presentation and then futzing around for 25 minutes?
I associate speedups of that magnitude with porting plain Ruby code to optimized C++, optimized C++ to a GPU, or GPU to quick-and-dirty exploratory ASIC.
Despite my reference post being an unusually image heavy blog post, fonts are 43.8% and images are such a small percentage that webpagetest doesn't even list the number.

I haven't seen a study that looks at going from 400ms to 10900ms because the idea that a website would be that slow is so absurd that people don't even look into the possibility. But many websites are that slow!

Having no styling is surely not optimal, but there's something a bit funny about no styling beating the at-the-time most widely used programmer blog styling, which means it likely also beat wordpress, svtble, blogspot, medium, etc.
posts.key_insight: Popular web frameworks and platforms ship with such egregious performance problems that removing all their default features can yield hardware-optimization-level speedups, suggesting the industry has normalized absurdly slow websites.

posts.summary: Dan Luu challenges the common claim that 'informal reasoning' is superior to testing for ensuring software correctness, particularly critiquing arguments from 'Out of the Tar Pit.' He argues that many real-world systems—from modern CPUs to tax code implementations—contain irreducible complexity that cannot be reasoned about formally, and that humans inevitably make reasoning errors. Drawing from 7.5 years in CPU verification, he demonstrates that comprehensive testing can achieve extremely high reliability (one logical bug in 7.5 years), making it not just necessary but practically sufficient for most engineering purposes.
key_points: Many real systems have irreducible complexity that makes formal reasoning impossible
Modern high-performance CPUs cannot be understood through informal reasoning alone due to speculative execution and caching
Humans are inherently fallible at reasoning, even for 'obvious' cases (citing Euclid's Elements bug and Intel CPU bugs)
Testing with state space reduction and symbolic execution works well in practice despite theoretical limitations
Real-world evidence: CPU verification through testing achieved only one logical bug in 7.5 years
money_quotes: testing is hopelessly inadequate....(it) can be used very effectively to show the presence of bugs but never to show their absence.
no human can possibly hope to understand a modern high-performance CPU well enough to informally reason about its correctness
No matter how smart or knowledgeable, humans are incapable of reasoning correctly all of the time.
Testing may not be sufficient to find all bugs, but it can be sufficient to achieve better reliability than pretty much any software company cares to.
It's possible to make a simple CPU, but not one that's fast and simple.
posts.key_insight: While testing cannot prove the absence of bugs theoretically, practical evidence shows it can achieve reliability levels that exceed what most organizations require, making the 'informal reasoning over testing' argument actively harmful for real-world engineering.

posts.summary: Dan Luu analyzes the actual performance cost of integer overflow checking in C/C++ compilers, finding that the theoretical overhead should be around 2-5% for typical workloads based on instruction mix analysis. However, practical benchmarks reveal that current compiler implementations (clang and gcc circa 2014) produce dramatically worse code when overflow diagnostics are enabled - not due to the overflow checks themselves, but because of poor register allocation and mis-optimizations that add unnecessary pushes, pops, and moves. When using trap-on-error mode instead of diagnostic mode, the performance penalty essentially disappears, demonstrating that the problem is fixable compiler implementation rather than fundamental hardware limitations.
key_points: Theoretical analysis suggests integer overflow checking should add only 2-5% overhead based on instruction frequency and branch prediction
Benchmarks show 28% slowdown for bzip2 compression with diagnostics enabled, but nearly zero overhead with trap-on-error mode
Simple add function shows egregious compiler mis-optimization: unnecessary register saves/restores when diagnostics are enabled
The performance problem is entirely a compiler implementation issue, not a hardware limitation - the overflow flag already exists
Both clang 3.4 and gcc 4.8 produce suboptimal code for overflow checking with diagnostics, though later versions improved
money_quotes: That's awful (although not atypical) compiler generated code... Had the compiler just done the add without the additional mov, leaving the input in %edi/%esi as it was passed (and as done in the non-checked version), this wouldn't be necessary.

While a better C compiler could do better, in theory, gcc 4.82 doesn't do better than clang 3.4 here. For one thing, gcc's -ftrapv only checks signed overflow. Worse yet, it doesn't work, and this bug on ftrapv has been open since 2008.
Integer overflow checks ought to cost a few percent on typical integer-heavy workloads, and they do, as long as you don't want nice error messages.
But there's already hardware support to get good-enough performance for the vast majority of applications. It's just not taken advantage of because people don't actually care about this problem.
The current mechanism that produces nice error messages somehow causes optimizations to get screwed up in a lot of cases.
posts.key_insight: The performance penalty of integer overflow checking is a solved hardware problem (overflow flags exist), but poor compiler implementation creates artificial 4-6x slowdowns that disappear when diagnostics are disabled, revealing that the barrier to adoption is compiler quality rather than fundamental limitations.

posts.summary: Luu provides a detailed critique of Julia's engineering culture and practices, arguing that despite the language's impressive technical features and performance claims, it suffers from systematic quality issues stemming from inadequate testing, poor error handling, and a core team culture that dismisses legitimate concerns. The post catalogs numerous bugs encountered in basic usage, examines structural problems in the development process (lack of code review, poor documentation, weak test coverage), and critiques misleading performance benchmarks. A 2022 update demonstrates that many core issues persist years later despite claims they were fixed, with the community's reflexive defensiveness and PR-focused responses preventing meaningful improvement in correctness and reliability.
key_points: Julia has an unusually high rate of serious correctness bugs in both the language runtime and core libraries, with silent wrong results being common
The development culture prioritizes velocity over correctness but publicly claims world-class reliability and testing rigor
Performance benchmarks on the Julia website are unrepresentative microbenchmarks that avoid Julia's performance pitfalls while not giving other languages similar advantages
High barriers to contribution (broken builds, recursive make issues, undocumented code even core devs can't understand) limit community participation
Core team's pattern of dismissing legitimate criticism as 'illegitimate' or claiming issues are 'already fixed' continues for years while the same problems persist
money_quotes: I spent half an hour writing a throwaway script and ran into four bugs in the core language.
Julia folks don't care about error handling, so it's buggy and incomplete.

It's hard to say that a community is friendly when the response is mostly positive, but with a significant fraction of nasty responses, since it doesn't really take a lot of nastiness to make a group seem unfriendly.

There's a sleight of hand here where the reflexive response from a number of the co-creators as well as core developers of Julia is to brush off any particular issue with a comment that sounds plausible if read on HN or Twitter by someone who doesn't know people who've used Julia.
Julia and its packages have the highest rate of serious correctness bugs of any programming system I've used, and I started programming with Visual Basic 6 in the mid-2000s.
posts.key_insight: Julia's core problem isn't technical limitations but a development culture that systematically undervalues correctness, testing, and documentation while publicly claiming excellence in these areas, creating a gap between marketed reliability and actual user experience that persists because legitimate criticism is reflexively dismissed rather than addressed.

posts.summary: This tutorial walks through implementing a simple malloc/free memory allocator from scratch using sbrk to manipulate the heap. The implementation uses a linked list of memory blocks with metadata stored just below returned pointers, supports realloc and calloc, and can be dynamically linked to replace the system allocator in existing binaries. The post includes detailed debugging techniques using gdb and exercises to improve the basic implementation with proper alignment, block splitting, and block merging.
key_points: Uses sbrk to request heap memory from the OS and stores metadata in a hidden region below returned pointers
Implements a linked list of blocks with size, next pointer, and free flag to track and reuse freed memory
Can be compiled as a shared library and loaded via LD_PRELOAD to replace malloc in existing binaries without recompilation
Demonstrates debugging techniques for memory allocators using gdb, including setting breakpoints and inspecting memory at different offsets
Basic implementation has limitations around memory fragmentation, alignment, and block splitting that are left as exercises
money_quotes: A common trick to work around this is to store meta-information about a memory region in some space that we squirrel away just below the pointer that we return.
The heap region we get from the OS has to be contiguous, so we can't return a block of memory in the middle to the OS.

You never really need to assert anything, but it often makes debugging a lot easier. In fact, when I wrote this code, I had a bug that would have resulted in silent data corruption if these asserts weren't there.
What's happening here is that structs get padded, so that sizeof(struct block_meta) is 32, even though the last valid member is at 24.
I haven't tested this much, so I'm sure there are bugs, even if this basically kinda sorta works.
posts.key_insight: Memory allocators work by requesting contiguous heap space from the OS and managing it through metadata hidden just before returned pointers, creating a reusable pool of blocks that can't be returned to the OS piecemeal but must be tracked and recycled within the application's address space.

posts.summary: This post systematically dismantles the argument that market forces prevent discrimination in tech hiring. Using evidence from sports (baseball, basketball, hockey), finance (Alan Greenspan's economics firm), and empirical studies, Luu demonstrates that even in highly competitive markets with measurable performance metrics, discrimination persists for decades and actually creates competitive advantages for non-discriminating firms. The post argues that claims about 'lowering the bar' through diversity efforts are unfounded because current hiring practices are far from optimal, and that 'the market' is just collective human action that won't self-correct without intentional intervention.
key_points: Competitive markets don't automatically eliminate discrimination - Alan Greenspan's firm gained competitive advantage by hiring women economists at lower wages for 30+ years
Sports leagues with better performance data than tech still showed significant wage gaps and higher exit rates for minorities lasting decades after integration
The 'lowering the bar' argument assumes current hiring is already optimal, but evidence shows systemic bias at every stage from resume screening to promotion

Personal anecdotes illustrate how bias operates: highly qualified engineers face age discrimination, women with math degrees questioned as 'not technical enough', female bloggers regularly have credentials questioned while male counterparts don't
The market is just humans making decisions - it won't fix discrimination automatically without deliberate intervention
money_quotes: Hiring women . . . gave Townsend-Greenspan higher-quality work for the same money

In tech, some people are concerned that increasing diversity will 'lower the bar', but in sports, which has a more competitive hiring market than tech, we saw the opposite, increasing diversity raised the level instead of lowering it because it means hiring people on their qualifications instead of on what they look like.

I don't disagree with people who say that it would be absurd for tech companies to leave money on the table by not hiring qualified minorities. But this is exactly what we saw in the sports we looked at, where that's even more absurd due to the relative ease of quantifying performance. And yet, for decades, teams left huge amounts of money on the table
The market is just humans. It's humans all the way down.
We can fix this, if we stop assuming the market will fix it for us.
posts.key_insight: The appeal to 'market forces' as a defense against claims of tech industry discrimination is empirically false - even in more competitive markets with better performance metrics like professional sports, discrimination persisted for decades and created profit opportunities for non-discriminating firms, proving that markets alone don't eliminate bias without deliberate intervention.

posts.summary: Dan Luu analyzes whether running ads on his blog makes financial sense, ultimately concluding it probably doesn't despite potential revenue of $600-2,300/month. He examines the tradeoffs between modest ad revenue (which would never approach his tech salary) versus downsides like reduced traffic, privacy concerns, perverse incentives, and hassle. Through updates spanning two years, he discovers his traffic grew enough to theoretically support living expenses, yet still chooses not to run ads due to the hassle factor, comparing this to developers who rationally leave money on the table by choosing startups over higher-paying big tech jobs.
key_points: At typical CPM rates, even successful programming blogs generate modest income - Jeff Atwood's Coding Horror at peak traffic would earn only ~$1,700/month, far below tech salaries
Ads impose hidden costs: reduced traffic, privacy concerns for readers, unconscious bias toward optimizing for revenue rather than quality content
The ratio of traffic to Alexa rankings is wildly inaccurate - Coding Horror ranked 7x higher despite having 1/4 the traffic over the measured period
After two years, traffic grew to 2.3M hits/month (potentially $100k/year with optimization), yet running ads still felt like too much hassle
About 40% of readers use ad blockers, and top programmer crowdfunding campaigns earn an order of magnitude less than potential ad revenue
money_quotes: The wildly optimistic success rate is that you barely cover rent when the programming job market is hot enough that mid-level positions at big companies pay out total compensation that's 8x-9x the median income in the U.S. That's not good.
It seems totally irrational to leave $90k/yr on the table because 'it seems like a hassle', but here we are.

I regularly talk to people who leave $200k/yr or more on the table by working for startups instead of large companies, and that seems like a reasonable preference to me. They make 'enough' money and like things the way they are. So why can't not running ads be a reasonable preference?

My impression is that people are mostly concerned with really big companies that have enough information that they could deanonymize people if they were so inclined, like Google and Facebook, but some people are probably also concerned about smaller ad networks like Carbon.

People are influenced by money, even when they don't notice it. I'm people. I might do something to get more revenue, even though the dollar amount is small and I wouldn't consciously spend a lot of effort of optimizing things to get an extra $5/month.
posts.key_insight: Even when blog monetization becomes theoretically lucrative enough to support living expenses, the rational choice for someone with tech industry earning power may be to leave that money on the table if the hassle and tradeoffs don't align with personal preferences - just as developers rationally choose lower-paying jobs they prefer.

posts.summary: Modern CPUs have evolved far beyond simple arithmetic and logic operations, adding complex memory hierarchies with caches and TLBs, out-of-order execution, SIMD instructions, and sophisticated concurrency primitives. While many optimizations like branch prediction and cache management happen transparently, programmers can achieve significant performance gains by understanding memory access patterns, cache locality, and avoiding expensive syscalls. The gap between memory and CPU speed has grown to 100x+, making memory hierarchy understanding critical, though clever prefetching and caching often mask this latency automatically.
key_points: Memory access has become the primary bottleneck - memory can take 400+ cycles while CPU operations take a few cycles, but prefetching and caching can recover most of this performance loss
Context switches and syscalls are extremely expensive due to cache/TLB evictions, making batched syscalls and user-space I/O stacks necessary for high performance
SIMD instructions provide 2x-4x speedups for parallel operations, and modern compilers can often auto-vectorize simple code
Most 'old school' optimizations (branch prediction tricks, alignment padding, avoiding branches) are now largely irrelevant as CPUs have gotten much better at executing 'bad' code
Future trends point toward hardware acceleration (FPGAs/ASICs), user-mode I/O stacks for low-latency networking/storage, and dark silicon enabling specialized on-chip accelerators
money_quotes: A few cycles vs. 400+ cycles sounds really bad; that's well over 100x slower. But if I write a dumb loop that reads and operates on a large block of 64-bit values, the CPU is smart enough to prefetch the correct data before I need it, which lets me process at about 22 GB/s on my 3GHz processor.

The real cost of not locking also often ends up being the inevitable bugs. Doing clever things with memory barriers is almost always a bug waiting to happen... The fact is, any time anybody makes up a new locking mechanism, THEY ALWAYS GET IT WRONG.
14,000 cycles after a syscall, code is still not quite running at full speed.

CPUs have gotten a lot better at executing poorly optimized code over the past decade, and compilers are getting better at optimizing code, which makes optimizing branches a poor use of time unless you're trying to squeeze out the absolute best possible performance.

It's important to note that x86 has one of the strongest memory models of any architecture you're likely to encounter nowadays. If you write code that just works without thinking it through and port it to architectures that have weaker guarantees (PPC, ARM, or Alpha), you'll almost certainly have bugs.
posts.key_insight: Modern CPU performance is dominated by memory hierarchy effects rather than instruction execution speed, and while many optimizations happen transparently, understanding cache behavior and avoiding syscalls matters far more than traditional micro-optimizations like branch prediction or alignment that CPUs now handle well automatically.

posts.summary: While backdoors in software are well-documented, the possibility of CPU-level hardware backdoors remains dismissed as impossible despite being technically straightforward to implement. The post details three components of a CPU backdoor: what it would do (privilege escalation from user to kernel mode), how it would be triggered (through carefully chosen instruction sequences with specific input values), and how it would be inserted (either through compromising the design process, manufacturing, or the microcode patch mechanism that all modern CPUs use for bug fixes). The technical barriers are minimal compared to the organizational challenges of gaining the necessary access.
key_points: CPU backdoors could enable privilege escalation that bypasses both hardware and software virtualization
Backdoors could be triggered through specific floating-point instruction inputs, providing 160+ bits of state space to hide the trigger
Modern CPUs have built-in patch mechanisms that accept signed updates, which could be compromised to insert backdoors
Chips are designed to allow easy edits to top metal layers during manufacturing, making last-minute modifications feasible
AMD's K7 and K8 microcode patch mechanisms were actually compromised, demonstrating this is not just theoretical
money_quotes: I'm not going to claim that CPU backdoors exist, but I will claim that the implementation is easy, if you've got the right access.
Since it's the CPU that's doing it, this can punch through both hardware and software virtualization.
It would certainly be easier to compromise a present-day corporation than it was to compromise government agencies during the cold war, and that was eminently doable.
Since chips are designed to allow easy edits, someone with access to the design before the chip is manufactured (such as the manufacturer) can make major changes with relatively small edits.
Is this actually happening? I have no idea. Could it be happening? Absolutely.
posts.key_insight: CPU backdoors are not only technically feasible but relatively simple to implement through existing patch mechanisms and manufacturing processes—the real barrier is organizational access, not technical difficulty.

posts.summary: The common debate over AI replacing humans focuses on whether AI can match ideal human performance, but this misses two key points: many customer service jobs are already filled by apathetic humans in poorly-designed systems who perform terribly, and companies will replace humans with AI even if AI is significantly worse, as long as it's much cheaper. Through detailed examples of frustrating bureaucratic interactions with healthcare scheduling and auto service, the post demonstrates that we're already at the point where computers can match apathetic humans in poorly-designed systems, which describes a significant fraction of service jobs. The 2023 update confirms this prediction, noting that LLM-based customer service is indeed replacing human workers despite being imperfect, because human customer service wasn't improving while AI continues to get better.
key_points: Jobs at risk of AI replacement are often filled by apathetic humans in poorly-designed systems, not ideal helpful humans
Companies replace humans with AI even when AI is significantly worse, if the cost savings are substantial (e.g., phone trees)
Many bureaucratic customer service interactions already feel like dealing with a bad 1980s compiler - mechanical, error-prone, unable to handle exceptions
Good computer-based systems can already outperform average humans at bureaucratic institutions for tasks like scheduling
The bar for AI isn't matching expert humans in well-designed systems, but matching apathetic humans in broken systems
money_quotes: I think this misses two very important factors. One, is that jobs that are on the potential chopping block, such as first-line customer service, customer service for industries that are either low margin or don't care about the customer, etc., tend to be filled by apathetic humans in a poorly designed system, and humans aren't even very good at simple tasks they care a lot about.
Phone trees are absolutely terrible compared to the humans they replaced, but they're also orders of magnitude cheaper.

The case against using computers instead of humans is that computers are bad at handling error conditions, can't adapt to unusual situations, and behave according to mechanical rules, which can often generate ridiculous outcomes, but that's precisely the situation we're in right now with humans.

I'm not sure if, in my lifetime, technology will advance to the point where computers can be as good as helpful humans in a well designed system. But we're already at the point where computers can be as helpful as apathetic humans in a poorly designed system, which describes a significant fraction of service jobs.
AI customer service seems quite poor, just like human customer service. But human customer service isn't improving, while AI customer service is.
posts.key_insight: AI doesn't need to match the performance of ideal humans to displace workers—it only needs to match the performance of apathetic humans in broken systems while being dramatically cheaper, a bar that has already been cleared in many industries.

posts.summary: The post systematically debunks several widely-cited scientific findings by actually reading the original papers, showing that popular interpretations of studies on Dunning-Kruger effect, income and happiness, hedonic adaptation, and chess expertise are often opposite to what the research actually found. Luu argues that reading academic papers is much easier than people think - often requiring just reading the abstract, conclusion, and skimming graphs to verify claims. The post emphasizes that gatekeeping around scientific literacy is often self-serving, as the knowledge required to interpret most studies can be acquired in far less time than a full degree program.
key_points: Dunning-Kruger is commonly misinterpreted - the study shows perceived ability is compressed (not inverted), with people at both low and high skill levels misestimating their abilities
Income consistently correlates with happiness across all countries studied, contrary to popular claims about happiness caps at $75k or lower
Hedonic adaptation theory is wrong - people don't fully return to baseline happiness after negative events like unemployment, disability, or divorce
Chess expertise advantage persists even with randomized positions (1.6x vs 2.2x for real positions), contradicting the widely-cited claim that the advantage completely disappears
Reading empirical papers to verify claims is usually straightforward - reading abstracts, conclusions, and skimming graphs is often sufficient to catch misrepresentations
money_quotes: It's really common to see claims that some meme is backed by 'studies' or 'science'. But when I look at the actual studies, it usually turns out that the data are opposed to the claim.
Reading papers sounds like a lot of work. It sometimes is. But it's often not.

Gatekeeping reading and interpreting a study on whether or not someone has a credential like a degree is absurd when someone can spend a week's worth of time gaining the knowledge that a degree offers.
It's in the best interests of non-altruistic people who do read studies to make it seem like reading studies is difficult.
People love clever, contrarian, ideas and don't care very much if they're wrong, so clever, contrarian [ideas] relatively frequently become viral relative to their correctness.
posts.key_insight: Most popular interpretations of scientific studies are wrong because people don't actually read the papers, but verifying claims by reading the original research is far easier than the gatekeepers of scientific literacy would have you believe.

posts.summary: This post catalogs the enormous depth of technical knowledge gaps exposed by the deceptively simple question 'what happens when you load a URL?' Through 48 progressively deeper questions spanning keyboards, networks, fiber optics, browsers, and displays, Luu demonstrates how much complexity lies beneath everyday computing tasks. Rather than providing answers, the post serves as a humbling inventory of how little even experienced engineers truly understand about the full stack. The post concludes with curated resources for learning about these topics and a note that this open-ended exploration represents the opposite of effective technical interviews.
key_points: The question 'what happens when you load a URL' reveals enormous technical knowledge gaps across the entire computing stack
Even experts lack deep understanding of most layers - from keyboard hardware to fiber optic transmission to browser rendering
The complexity spans physical engineering (connectors, fiber), protocols (TCP, DNS, HTTP), and systems (OS, browser, GPU)
Open-ended questions like this make poor interview filters compared to more focused technical assessments
The post provides curated resources for learning about different layers of the stack
money_quotes: I've been hearing this question a lot lately, and when I do, it reminds me how much I don't know.

You have this connection that can handle 10Gb/s. That surely won't work if there's any gap at all between the physical doodads that are being connected. How do people design connectors that can withstand tens of thousands of insertions and still maintain their tolerances?
Where I can get answers to this stuff? That's not a rhetorical question!

As an interview question, this is pretty much the antithesis of the tptacek strategy. From what I've seen, my guess is that tptacek-style interviews are much better filters than open ended questions like this.
posts.key_insight: The staggering complexity hidden beneath simple computing tasks reveals how specialized modern engineering has become - no single person can deeply understand the full stack from keyboard physics to fiber optics to browser rendering.

posts.summary: This post examines how optimizing for intermediate metrics instead of true goals leads to ineffective or counterproductive outcomes across domains. Using three case studies—IQ scores in early childhood education, cholesterol levels in heart disease prevention, and percentile latency in software systems—Luu demonstrates that when proxy metrics become targets, they can be improved without (or even at the expense of) the actual desired outcome. The fundamental problem is Goodhart's Law in action: when a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure.
key_points: Early childhood education programs like Head Start showed IQ improvements that faded by age 10, yet produced significant long-term benefits in graduation rates, crime reduction, and health outcomes
Pharmaceutical companies spent billions developing drugs that improved cholesterol levels but failed to reduce (or even increased) heart attack risk
Optimizing only specific percentiles (99th, 99.9th) in latency measurements creates distributions with artificial kinks that meet targets while degrading overall user experience
Common benchmarking frameworks measure latency under unrealistic sequential-access patterns, producing metrics that differ by an order of magnitude from real concurrent-user scenarios
The solution isn't a simple trick but constantly validating that proxy metrics remain connected to actual goals
money_quotes: If you have very narrow optimization goals, and your measurements don't give you any visibility into anything else, everything but your optimization goals is going to get thrown out the window.
It's like the video game solving AI that presses pause before its character is about to get killed, because pausing the game prevents its health from decreasing.
But the only trick is that there is no trick, that you have to constantly check that your map is somehow connected to the territory.

If you optimize the 99%-ile of that measurement, you're optimizing the 99%-ile for when all of your users get together and decide to use your app sequentially, coordinating so that no one requests anything until the previous user is finished.

Since the end goal is usually to improve the user experience and not just optimize three specific points on the distribution, targeting a few points instead of using some kind of weighted integral can easily cause anti-optimizations that degrade the actual user experience, while producing great slideware.
