posts.key_insight: Optimizing intermediate proxy metrics without validating their continued connection to ultimate goals is a universal failure mode across domains—from education to medicine to software performance—requiring constant vigilance to ensure the map matches the territory.

posts.summary: Luu argues that software testing relies too heavily on manual test generation compared to hardware testing, where automated random and coverage-based testing dominate. He demonstrates that simple random test frameworks (like QuickCheck) and coverage-guided fuzzers (like AFL) can find the vast majority of production bugs with minimal effort, yet software projects underutilize these methods due to cultural path dependence rather than rational cost-benefit analysis. The hardware industry's assumption that all critical bugs must be caught before release drives investment in sophisticated automated testing, while software's acceptance of bugs escaping to production perpetuates inefficient manual testing practices.
key_points: Manual test generation consumes 1-25% of hardware test effort but most software test effort, despite being less scalable and efficient
Random test generation using frameworks like QuickCheck is trivially easy to implement and can find thousands of bugs with minimal developer time
58% of critical production failures in distributed systems could be caught with very simple tests
Coverage-based fuzzing tools like AFL find deep bugs by instrumenting code to track edge coverage and generating mutations of interesting test cases
Hardware teams expect QA to catch all bad bugs before release; software teams accept that most important bugs will escape, leading to different resource allocation and attitudes toward testing
money_quotes: It is very common for bugs that have complex real-world manifestations to be reproducible with really simple test cases, if you know where to look.
You can spend an hour implementing a random test generator and find tens of bugs, or you can spend more time and find thousands of bugs.
58% could be caught with very simple tests. Turns out, generating 'shallow' random tests is enough to catch most production bugs.
If a test generator takes a month to catch a bug in an area, there are probably other subtle bugs in the same area, and more work needs to be done on the generator to flush them out.
Considering the relative cost of a single machine compared to the cost of a developer, it's almost certainly worth dedicating at least one machine to generating and running tests.
posts.key_insight: The software industry's reliance on manual testing over automated random and coverage-based approaches is a cultural artifact rather than a rational choice, leaving easily-findable bugs in production systems despite cheap, proven alternatives.

posts.summary: Dan Luu argues that "boring" mainstream languages like C, C++, and Java are underrated and actually more valuable for serious work than "interesting" fancy languages. While interesting languages can reduce boilerplate for routine tasks, when tackling genuinely difficult problems, domain knowledge matters far more than language features. The most impressive software systems - from operating systems to distributed databases to major web services - are overwhelmingly built in these supposedly boring languages, and the best engineers typically have deep domain expertise rather than expertise in exotic languages.
key_points: Boring languages are ideal for hard problems where boilerplate is a rounding error compared to domain complexity
Learning diverse languages helps with boilerplate efficiency but doesn't directly solve hard problems - domain knowledge does
The vast majority of impressive software systems are built in C, C++, and Java
Online discussions favor fancy languages while experienced developers in practice largely prefer mainstream ones
Greenfield projects still choose boring languages even when teams could use alternatives
money_quotes: When I'm doing boring work that's basically bottlenecked on the speed at which I can write boilerplate, it feels much nicer to use an interesting language (like F#), which lets me cut down on the amount of time spent writing boilerplate. But when I'm doing interesting work, the boilerplate is a rounding error and I don't mind using a boring language like Java.
Why do I work in boring languages? Because that's what the people I want to work with use, and what the systems I want to work on are written in.

The vast majority of experienced developers I know think that most mainstream languages are basically fine, modulo performance constraints, and this is even more true among people who've built systems that are really impressive to me.

When I have a hard problem, I spend so little time on that kind of stuff that the skills I learned from writing a wide variety of languages don't really help me; instead, what helps me is having domain knowledge that gives me a good lever with which I can solve the hard problem.

I don't think about things in a programming language. I think of an abstract solution and then translate the solution to a language, so having another language in my toolbox can, at most, help me think of better translations and save on translation.
posts.key_insight: The value of a programming language for difficult work lies not in its features for reducing boilerplate, but in the quality of engineers and systems it gives you access to - and empirically, the world's most impressive software is built in "boring" languages by people with deep domain expertise.

posts.summary: While power efficiency in computing is often discussed in the context of mobile devices and chip scaling limits, datacenter power consumption represents a massive and underappreciated cost driver. Power costs compound through infrastructure losses (UPS, voltage conversion, PSUs) and cooling requirements, with every watt of server power requiring 1-2 additional watts for support systems. The total cost of power and cooling infrastructure plus operational power use accounts for 33% of lifetime datacenter costs, rivaling the cost of servers themselves and fundamentally reshaping where and how we build computing infrastructure.
key_points: Datacenters lose power multiple times through the delivery chain: at substations, UPS storage (~6-12%), voltage stepdown (~10%), server power supplies (~10%)
Cooling and air circulation consume 40-70% additional power on top of what servers draw
Power and cooling infrastructure represents 82% of datacenter facility costs ($74M of $90M in the example)
Over the datacenter lifetime, power/cooling totals 33% of all costs when amortized (20% infra + 13% usage)
Datacenters are now built near cheap power sources, just like steel mills used to be
money_quotes: We used to build steel mills near cheap sources of power, but now that's where we build datacenters.

Patrick Gelsinger observed that if scaling continued at then-current rates, chips would have the power density of a nuclear reactor by 2005, a rocket nozzle by 2010, and the surface of the sun by 2015
For every watt of power used in the server, we pay for another 1-2 watts of support power.
82% is associated with power and cooling
Best practices for writing power efficient code still aren't well understood.
posts.key_insight: Datacenter power consumption has become so significant that power costs (infrastructure plus usage) rival server hardware costs, fundamentally shifting where computing infrastructure gets built and making power efficiency a first-order business concern rather than just a technical optimization.

posts.summary: Many websites accidentally create a Google search monopoly by blocking all web crawlers except Googlebot in their robots.txt files. Bell Labs exemplifies this by blocking archive.org and all search engines except Google and the outdated msnbot, making their content inaccessible to alternative search engines like Yandex and preventing historical archiving. This widespread practice, often unintentional, makes it nearly impossible for competing search engines to operate and has led to some crawlers simply ignoring robots.txt entirely.
key_points: Bell Labs and many other sites block all crawlers except Googlebot in robots.txt
This prevents both alternative search engines and Internet Archive from accessing content
The practice is often accidental rather than intentional policy
Competing search engines face barriers beyond technical challenges due to these blocks
Misuse of robots.txt has led crawlers to start ignoring it, making it effectively useless
money_quotes: Running a competing search engine and preventing a Google monopoly is hard enough without having sites ban non-Google bots.
I don't think that the common default many web devs have, of blocking everything but googlebot, is really intended to block competing search engines as well as archive.org.
This mis-use of robots.txt, where sites ban everything but googlebot, is slowly making robots.txt effectively useless, much like browsers identify themselves as every browser in user-agent strings.
Google has a turnaround time of minutes to hours on updates to this page, which I find pretty amazing... they must also be crawling millions or tens of millions of other sites on an hourly basis.
posts.key_insight: Web developers are inadvertently reinforcing Google's search monopoly through careless robots.txt configurations that block all crawlers except Googlebot, a practice so widespread it's rendering the robots.txt standard itself obsolete.

posts.summary: Dan Luu argues that monorepos (single large repositories containing all of a company's code) offer significant practical advantages over managing multiple smaller repositories. The primary benefits include simplified organization and navigation, easier dependency management with universal versioning, better tooling due to unified structure, and the ability to make atomic cross-project changes and refactorings. While acknowledging downsides exist, Luu contends that companies like Google, Facebook, and Twitter have adopted monorepos for sound engineering reasons, not ignorance of alternatives.
key_points: Monorepos allow logical organization of code without being constrained by version control system limitations
Dependencies are simplified through universal version numbers and atomic cross-project commits
Tooling becomes much easier to build when operating on a single unified codebase
Cross-project API refactoring and changes can be done atomically in a single commit
The benefits are significant enough that companies have patched git and Mercurial to support giant monorepos
money_quotes: With a monorepo, you just refactor the API and all of its callers in one commit. That's not always trivial, but it's much easier than it would be with lots of small repos.
SVN, hg, git, etc solve the problem of atomic cross-file changes; monorepos solve the same problem across projects.
This sort of Lego-like development process does not happen as cleanly in the open source world.
Most people now consider it absurd to use a version control system like CVS, RCS, or ClearCase, where it's impossible to do a single atomic commit across multiple files
My point isn't that you should definitely switch to a monorepo; it's merely that using a monorepo isn't totally unreasonable
posts.key_insight: Monorepos aren't an ignorant mistake by large tech companies but a deliberate engineering choice that trades different constraints for substantial gains in code navigation, dependency management, tooling simplicity, and the ability to make atomic cross-project changes.

posts.summary: SourceForge destroyed sixteen years of user trust by taking over project admin accounts and forcibly injecting adware into installers like GIMP, transitioning from optional to mandatory malware bundling. Slashdot, owned by the same parent company, compounded the damage by censoring all stories about the SourceForge scandal for a week while it spread across every other tech publication. This represents a textbook case of how quickly companies can destroy brand value through short-term revenue grabs and ineffective damage control that only makes things worse.
key_points: SourceForge eliminated opt-in adware bundling and began forcibly taking over project accounts to inject malware
Slashdot censored the SourceForge story for a week while it appeared on all other major tech publications
The parent company's financial reports show declining revenue despite aggressive monetization tactics
The censorship creates substantial downside risk of a 'Digg moment' with minimal upside since the story was already everywhere
Management appears to have fallen into 'we must do something' thinking rather than strategic decision-making
money_quotes: No clueful person is going to ever download something from SourceForge again.
I'm amazed at how quickly it's possible to destroy user trust, and how much easier it is to destroy a brand than to create one.
How is it possible that someone, somewhere, thinks that censoring SourceForge's adware bundling on Slashdot is a net positive for Slashdot Media?
As far as I can tell, they've fallen back to this classic syllogism: 'We must do something. This is something. We must do this.'
There's basically no upside here and a substantial downside risk.
posts.key_insight: Corporate panic over declining revenue can lead to catastrophically bad decision-making where management destroys long-term brand value through both aggressive short-term monetization and ineffective cover-ups that make the situation worse.

posts.summary: Dan Luu examines common patterns in postmortems from major tech companies, identifying that most critical failures stem from a few recurring categories: error handling bugs (92% of critical failures involve incorrectly handled errors), configuration changes (responsible for ~50% of global outages despite being seen as "obviously" risky), hardware failures including silent data corruption, and process bugs that enable catastrophic human errors. Despite these failure modes being "obvious" to engineers, companies repeatedly fail to invest adequately in testing error paths, staging config changes, and automating risky manual operations, leading to the same types of outages over and over.
key_points: Error handling bugs cause 92% of critical failures in distributed systems, with 25% from simply ignoring errors
Configuration changes cause about 50% of global outages, yet most companies don't test/stage config changes like code changes
Hardware fails more often than advertised (DRAM errors >10x worse than spec), and failover systems also fail
Process bugs that require humans to execute flawless manual procedures repeatedly cause catastrophic failures
Monitoring/alerting gaps often turn minor issues into major outages, but are underrepresented in public postmortems
money_quotes: If you care about building robust systems, the error checking code is the main code!
Configuration bugs, not code bugs, are the most common cause I've seen of really bad outages.
If you repeatedly put a human in a position where they can cause a catastrophic failure, you'll eventually get a catastrophe.
Just because something is obvious doesn't mean it's being done.

In one case, someone told me that what I was telling them was obvious at pretty much the same time their company was having a global outage of a multi-billion dollar service, caused by the exact thing we were talking about.
posts.key_insight: The gap between what's "obvious" about system reliability and what actually gets implemented is enormous—companies repeatedly suffer outages from well-known failure modes because they don't invest proportionally in testing error paths, staging config changes, and automating manual procedures.

posts.summary: Dan Luu reviews Steve Yegge's 2004 tech predictions, finding them remarkably accurate despite seeming obvious in hindsight. Most predictions were directionally correct, including the rise of JavaScript, cloud hosting, NoSQL databases, and mobile computing, with only the Lisp prediction missing significantly. The analysis demonstrates how good predictions suffer from hindsight bias—they seem obvious after the fact but were controversial when made—and shows how Yegge underestimated market inertia in his timing but correctly identified major industry trends.
key_points: Steve Yegge made 10 tech predictions in 2004 that were largely accurate in direction if not exact timing
Most misses were due to underestimating market inertia and the effect of 'dark matter' programmers
Good predictions appear obvious in hindsight due to hindsight bias, making them seem less impressive than they were
Yegge correctly predicted JavaScript's rise, cloud hosting dominance, NoSQL adoption, and mobile's importance years before they happened
The only major miss was predicting Lisp would break into top 10 languages by 2010
money_quotes: It's a no-win proposition: if you're right, hindsight bias makes it look like you're pointing out the obvious. And most predictions are wrong.
The misses are mostly caused by underestimating the sheer about of inertia it takes for a young/new solution to take over.
There's a massive amount of dark matter -- programmers who do business logic and don't blog or give talks -- that makes this prediction unlikely to come true in the near future.
Of course these all these things seem obvious in retrospect, but that's just part of Steve's plan to pass as a normal human being.
posts.key_insight: Accurate predictions about technology trends suffer from a paradox: hindsight bias makes them seem obvious after the fact, obscuring how controversial and non-obvious they were when originally made, while the main failure mode is underestimating how long market inertia delays even correct directional insights.

posts.summary: Luu examines how partially-degraded hardware ('limping' nodes) can catastrophically impact distributed systems performance far more than completely failed nodes. Through analysis of research by Thanh Do et al., he shows that a single slow disk or network card can cause 100x+ performance degradation across entire clusters in systems like Hadoop, HDFS, and HBase. While these failures are relatively rare (3% of hardware issues), they expose fundamental design flaws in tail-tolerance mechanisms and cascading failure prevention that have persisted since MapReduce's original 2004 paper.
key_points: Partially degraded hardware causes worse performance than complete failures in most distributed systems
Three failure modes: operation limplock (slow component), node limplock (slow component affects unrelated operations), and cluster limplock (single slow node cascades)
Hadoop's speculative execution fails against limping nodes because slow map nodes drag down all reducers, preventing straggler detection
Cassandra handles limping hardware better due to queue overflow prevention and SEDA architecture that decouples operations
Limping hardware accounts for ~3% of failures—rare but significant enough to warrant design consideration
money_quotes: The job completion rate slowed down from 172 jobs per hour to 1 job per hour, effectively killing the entire cluster.
In most (but not all) cases, having degraded hardware affected performance a lot more than having failed hardware.

This exact cause of failures was described in the original MapReduce paper, published in 2004. Five years later, when the paper we're reading was published, its built-in mechanism for straggler detection not only failed to prevent multiple types of stragglers, it also failed to prevent stragglers from effectively deadlocking the entire cluster.
Of course we should have monitoring, but we should also have systems that are robust when our monitoring fails, as it inevitably will.
posts.key_insight: Distributed systems' tail-tolerance mechanisms often assume binary failure states (working/dead) and catastrophically fail against the more insidious case of degraded-but-functioning hardware, a design oversight that has persisted for over a decade despite being documented in foundational papers.

posts.summary: Intel added cache partitioning (Cache Allocation Technology/CAT) to enable higher server utilization by allowing latency-sensitive and batch workloads to coexist on the same machines. Research shows datacenter workloads only need 4-6MB of LLC despite chips having 12-30MB caches, and spend much of their time stalled on memory despite low bandwidth utilization. By partitioning cache alongside other resource isolation mechanisms (CPU pinning, network quotas, power limiting), systems like Google's Heracles achieve 90% utilization compared to typical 10-50% utilization, translating to hundreds of millions in cost savings.
key_points: Typical server utilization is 10-50%, but Google demonstrates 90% utilization without impacting latency SLAs
Datacenter workloads only benefit from 4-6MB of LLC despite modern server chips having much larger caches
Cores spend 46-61% of time stalled on cache/memory, yet bandwidth utilization is surprisingly low
Cache partitioning (CAT) combined with CPU pinning, network quotas, and power limiting enables safe workload co-location
Heracles achieves 90% average utilization, worth $600M+ annually for large companies
money_quotes: Typical server utilization is between 10% and 50%. Google has demonstrated 90% utilization without impacting latency SLAs.
Datacenter workloads operate on such large data sets that it's often impossible to fit the dataset in RAM on a single machine, let alone in cache, making a larger LLC not particularly useful.

It's curious that we have low cache hit rates, a lot of time stalled on cache/memory, and low bandwidth utilization. This is suggestive of workloads spending a lot of time waiting on memory accesses that have some kind of dependencies that prevent them from being executed independently.
With Heracles, the worst case utilization is 80%, and the average is 90%. This is amazing.

SPEC is basically irrelevant as a benchmark now. It's somewhat dated as a workstation benchmark, and simply completely inappropriate as a benchmark for servers, office machines, gaming machines, dumb terminals, laptops, and mobile devices.
posts.key_insight: Modern datacenter economics makes even small utilization improvements worth massive engineering effort, and empirical measurement reveals that datacenter workloads have fundamentally different resource usage patterns than traditional benchmarks assume, enabling novel hardware features like cache partitioning to unlock dramatic efficiency gains.

posts.summary: Luu revisits Butler Lampson's 1999 assessment of computer science research to evaluate what's changed 16 years later. He methodically analyzes each category (Yes/Maybe/No) from Lampson's original talk, finding that nearly all the 1999 'Yes' items remain fundamental today, while seven formerly uncertain or failed technologies (like garbage collection, distributed systems, and RPC) became mainstream successes. Only RISC moved from 'Maybe' to 'No' as x86 dominated despite predictions of its demise. The analysis demonstrates that the landscape has changed substantially within a single career, making long-term predictions about research impact fundamentally unreliable.
key_points: All technologies rated 'Yes' in 1999 remain fundamental today (virtual memory, packet nets, RDB/SQL, transactions, GUIs, web, algorithms)
Seven technologies upgraded from 'Maybe' or 'No' to mainstream success: garbage collection, reuse, capabilities, RPC, and distributed computing most notably
RISC processors failed to dominate despite 1980s-90s predictions, with x86 winning through engineering rather than ISA elegance
Parallelism remains frustratingly difficult - Lampson admitted threading/locks were a failed solution and parallel programming still requires 'wizards'
Fancy type systems and pure functional programming remain niche despite their appeal to enthusiasts
money_quotes: Lampson's comment was that when they came up with threading, locks, and conditional variables at PARC, they thought they were creating something that programmers could use to take advantage of parallelism, but that they now have decades of evidence that they were wrong.

A clean RISC ISA is a beautiful thing. I sometimes re-read Dick Sites's explanation of the Alpha design just to admire it, but it turns out beauty isn't really critical for the commercial success of an ISA.
I take Lampson's view, which is that if the vast majority of programmers are literally incapable of using a certain class of technologies, that class of technologies has probably not succeeded.

After reviewing Lampson's 1999 talk, I'm much more optimistic about research areas that haven't yielded much real-world impact (yet), like capability based computing and fancy type systems. It seems basically impossible to predict what areas will become valuable over the next thirty years.
Distributed systems are so ubiquitous that startups with zero distributed systems expertise regularly use distributed systems provided by Amazon or Microsoft, and it's totally fine.
posts.key_insight: The rapid transformation from research concepts to mainstream adoption within a single career span makes long-term technological predictions essentially impossible, suggesting humility about dismissing today's academic research areas as impractical.

posts.summary: Hardware latency improvements, particularly in networking and storage, fundamentally change optimal system architecture. As network round-trip times have dropped from 500μs to 10s of μs and NVRAM access times have decreased to 15μs, it's now feasible to disaggregate storage across datacenters without performance penalties—providing applications with effectively "infinite disk." This shift means systems designed for 2005-era latencies are no longer optimal; applications that relied on local caching for 1ms latencies now carry counter-productive overhead when network latencies drop below 1μs. The same economics that enable datacenter disaggregation will inevitably extend to consumer cloud storage as bandwidth costs continue their exponential decline.
key_points: Network latencies have decreased enough (500μs to 10s of μs) that storage can be disaggregated across datacenters without noticeable performance impact
RDMA over Ethernet enables network latencies below NVRAM access times, allowing even high-performance storage to be disaggregated
Systems designed for higher-latency eras contain assumptions and optimizations (like local caching) that become overhead as latencies decrease
Cloud providers' cost of replicated storage is becoming lower than retail cost of local unreplicated storage for consumers
Bandwidth costs have declined exponentially (10Mb to 40Gb+ NICs) faster than applications have been re-architected to exploit it
money_quotes: The round-trip latency is so much lower than the seek time of a disk that we can dis-aggregate storage and distribute it anywhere in the datacenter without noticeable performance degradation, giving applications the appearance of having infinite disk space.
Applications written in a higher-latency era are often full of 'careless' operations that burn hundreds of nanoseconds at a time.
When latency drops below 1us, the caching that was a huge win in 2005 is often not just pointless, but actually counter-productive overhead.

If you look at what major cloud providers are paying for storage, their COGS of providing safely replicated storage is or will become lower than the retail cost to me of un-backed-up unreplicated local disk on my home machine.
There's literally more bandwidth than people know what to do with.
posts.key_insight: Hardware performance ratios—not just absolute speeds—determine optimal system architectures, and as network/storage latency ratios shift by orders of magnitude, yesterday's performance optimizations become tomorrow's bottlenecks.

posts.summary: Dan Luu systematically refutes Jeff Atwood's arguments against using ECC memory in servers, demonstrating that Atwood's reasoning is based on outdated information (Google's 1999 decision, which Google itself reversed), misunderstandings of hard vs. soft errors, and flawed market logic. Luu provides empirical evidence that memory errors are common enough to cause significant silent data corruption, with failure rates of 0.5+ faults per server per year even with conservative estimates, and that ECC provides crucial error detection capabilities that prevent catastrophic data corruption. He argues that the case for ECC is actually stronger for consumers than servers, since consumers have fewer sophisticated error detection mechanisms, though the primary reason consumers don't use ECC is lack of awareness rather than lack of benefit. The post demonstrates through multiple real-world examples (Google's search index corruption, Sun's cache reliability issues) that silent memory corruption is a persistent problem that ECC effectively mitigates at reasonable cost.
key_points: Google abandoned non-ECC memory after 1999 when they discovered it caused serious issues including search index corruption that returned random results

The study Atwood cites actually supports ECC (chipkill reduced failure rates 36x), and hard errors don't justify skipping ECC since they can develop over time and ECC helps detect them for hardware replacement
With modern server RAM sizes (128GB+), expected failure rate is ~0.5 faults per server per year, with each fault causing hundreds to thousands of errors
Real-world data shows 10-20% of Firefox crashes are due to memory corruption, and game companies report significant crashes from data corruption
The argument 'if ECC were important it would be everywhere' ignores price discrimination - Intel charges extra for server features because they can, not because consumers don't need them
Silent data corruption is harder to detect for consumers than datacenters, making the case for consumer ECC arguably stronger than for servers
money_quotes: Making mistakes and then fixing them is common in every successful engineering organization. If you're going to cargo cult an engineering practice, you should at least cargo cult current engineering practices, not something that was done in 1999.
A major reason consumers don't generally use ECC isn't that it isn't worth it for them, it's that they just have no idea how to attribute crashes and data corruption when they happen.

It's extremely hard to do so and basically every heavily tested filesystem can have a massive failure from a single error... They're better at that kind of analysis than 99.9% of programmers. It's just that this problem has been repeatedly shown to be hard enough that humans cannot effectively reason about it.
One of the key uses of ECC is not to correct errors, but to signal errors so that hardware can be replaced before silent corruption occurs.

For anyone who's used to dealing with reliability issues and just wants to know the FIT rate, the study finds a FIT rate of between 0.057 and 0.071 faults per Mbit (which, contra Atwood's assertion, is not a shockingly low number).
posts.key_insight: ECC memory is a cost-effective necessity for preventing silent data corruption in both servers and consumer systems, and the primary barrier to consumer adoption is lack of awareness rather than lack of benefit, as empirical evidence shows memory errors cause 10-20% of real-world application crashes even with modern hardware.

posts.summary: Desktop applications routinely fail to handle file operations correctly, leading to data corruption that even Gmail avoids despite being distributed. The post systematically demonstrates that writing files safely requires navigating a minefield of filesystem-specific behaviors, undocumented semantics, poorly-handled disk errors, and reordering issues that mirror the challenges of concurrent programming. Most developers underestimate this complexity, resulting in widespread bugs even in critical applications like version control systems, databases, and email clients.
key_points: Writing a simple file update atomically requires multiple fsync calls, careful ordering, checksums, and filesystem-specific knowledge to avoid corruption
Filesystem semantics are poorly documented (manpages literally cite 'rumor'), vary wildly between ext3/ext4/btrfs/xfs, and change between versions
Most filesystems ignore write errors and handle disk failures incorrectly - ext3 had comments like 'I really hope a write error doesn't happen here'
Studies found 5-20% of disks experience errors over two years, and 0.5% suffer silent data corruption annually
Even sophisticated applications (LevelDB, git, Mercurial, HDFS, Zookeeper) had file corruption bugs from incorrect assumptions about atomicity and ordering
money_quotes: Files are hard.

Correctly reasoning about re-ordering behavior and inserting barriers correctly is hard. But even though shared memory concurrency is considered a hard problem that requires great care, writing to files isn't treated the same way, even though it's actually harder in a number of ways.
The manpage literally refers to rumor. This is the level of documentation we have.
Comments they found next to ignored errors include: 'Should we pass any errors back?', 'Error, skip block and hope for the best.', 'We can't do anything about an error here.'

The fact that so many people thought that this was a simple solution to the problem demonstrates that this problem is one that people are prone to underestimating, even they're explicitly warned that people tend to underestimate this problem!
posts.key_insight: File I/O is fundamentally a concurrent programming problem with filesystem-specific reordering and atomicity semantics, but unlike multithreading it's treated casually by developers, leading to pervasive data corruption bugs that could be caught with basic testing and static analysis.

posts.summary: Dan Luu systematically dismantles the common startup mythology by analyzing compensation, work quality, and career development at big tech companies versus startups. Using concrete numbers, he demonstrates that senior engineers at major tech companies can earn a lifetime's median US income in just 6-7 years ($250k+/year), while startup equity has roughly a 0.5% chance of meaningful payoff for early employees. He argues that big companies now offer both superior compensation and access to technically interesting problems at unprecedented scale, though acknowledges the trade-offs vary by individual interests and circumstances.
key_points: Big tech companies offer dramatically higher compensation than commonly believed - $250k+/year for senior engineers with high probability versus lottery-ticket odds at startups
The startup compensation advantage has eroded significantly since 2004; equity typically becomes worthless or requires staying past desired departure due to ISO expiration and tax implications
Interesting technical work at scale is increasingly concentrated at big companies, which have resources for speculative 9-figure research projects that startups cannot match
The real differences are between individual companies, teams, and managers rather than between company types - picking the right specific opportunity matters more than the category
You can get startup upside while working at a big company by investing $25k/year in seed rounds, getting better terms than employee equity without vesting requirements
money_quotes: Roll d100... 0~70: Your equity grant is worth nothing. 71~94: Your equity grant is worth a lump sum of money which makes you about as much money as you gave up working for the startup. 95~99: Your equity grant is a life changing amount of money. 100: You worked at the next Google, and are rich beyond the dreams of avarice. Perceptive readers will note that 100 does not actually show up on a d100.

The only difference between then and now is that I took a job at a big company. When I took that job, the common story I heard at orientation was basically 'I never thought I'd be able to get a job at Google, but a recruiter emailed me and I figured I might as well respond'.

Since the company is a $X billion dollar a year company, the project only needed to move the needle by a small percentage to be worth it. And so a research project whose goal was to speed up the progress of another research project was approved.

The differences between types of companies is smaller than the differences between companies of the same type. This is recursive. The differences between different managers and teams at a company can easily be larger than the differences between companies.

A lot of people are really invested in the idea that they're making about as much as possible. If you've decided that making less money is the right trade-off for you, that's fine and I don't have any problem with that. But if you really think that you can't make that much money and you don't believe me, I recommend talking to one of the hundreds of thousands of engineers at one of the many large companies that pays well.
posts.key_insight: The conventional wisdom that startups offer better compensation and more interesting work than big tech companies is outdated - the 2010s tech boom has inverted the risk-reward calculation, with big companies now offering both higher expected value compensation and access to technically challenging problems at unprecedented scale.

posts.summary: Luu explores the phenomenon of "normalization of deviance" in software engineering, where dangerous practices become accepted as normal over time despite their risks. Drawing parallels from healthcare, aviation, and other fields, he argues that tech companies routinely normalize practices that would be considered shocking elsewhere—from broken builds to non-existent security to rewarding heroism over prevention. The industry lacks the systematic approach to learning from failures that other fields have developed, instead relying on cargo-culting practices from successful companies without understanding the context.
key_points: Tech companies routinely normalize deviant practices like broken builds, flaky tests marked to pass on any success, and firing people for minor leaks while ignoring major security issues
The normalization of deviance is well-studied in healthcare, aviation, and engineering but rarely discussed in software despite clear parallels in postmortems

Common justifications for breaking rules mirror those in other fields: rules seem inefficient, breaking them seems justified for immediate benefit, people trust themselves, and weak signals from newcomers get ignored
Companies that eventually developed good practices (Google, Microsoft) only did so after being embarrassed by major failures, not through essays or speeches
Strong engineering culture is a much larger productivity multiplier than trendy languages or methodologies, but companies spend far more effort on external metrics than internal processes
money_quotes: The data are clear that humans are really bad at taking the time to do things that are well understood to incontrovertibly reduce the risk of rare but catastrophic events. We will rationalize that taking shortcuts is the right, reasonable thing to do.
new person joins / new person: WTF WTF WTF WTF WTF / old hands: yeah we know we're concerned about it / new person: WTF WTF wTF wtf wtf w... / new person gets used to it

Google didn't go from adding z to the end of names to having the world's best security because someone gave a rousing speech or wrote a convincing essay. They did it after getting embarrassed a few times.

People write a lot about how things like using fancier languages or techniques like TDD or agile will make your teams more productive, but having a strong engineering culture is much larger force multiplier.
It's cargo cults all the way down.
posts.key_insight: Software engineering lacks systematic mechanisms to prevent the normalization of deviance that other fields have developed, relying instead on cargo-culting practices and only learning from catastrophic failures rather than near-misses or chronic dysfunction.

posts.summary: Intel experienced numerous severe CPU bugs in 2015 despite strong financial performance, including bugs that allowed VMs to crash host systems and widespread system hangs from common benchmarking tools. The author argues these bugs represent a systemic shift at Intel away from rigorous validation toward faster development cycles, driven by competitive pressure from ARM in the mobile market. Evidence suggests Intel's validation teams were explicitly told to move faster and cut validation times around 2013-2014, coinciding with an exodus of verification engineers and a measurable increase in serious bugs with security implications.
key_points: At least two severe Intel CPU bugs were discovered in late 2015, including a microcode hang exploitable by any VM to DoS its host
Intel's errata lists reveal numerous bugs causing 'unpredictable system behavior,' which can mean complete system crashes or security vulnerabilities
Anonymous sources claim Intel validation leadership around 2013 pushed to move faster and reduce validation times to compete with ARM
The shift from rare CPU bugs (once per year or decade) to frequent bugs is driven by both increased complexity and apparently reduced validation effort
Security implications have changed: bugs that once meant restarting computations now provide attack vectors for anyone with cloud access
money_quotes: If this CPU microcode hang had been found by one of these black hats, there would have been major carnage for most cloud hosted services at the most inconvenient possible time.

We need to move faster. Validation at Intel is taking much longer than it does for our competition. We need to do whatever we can to reduce those times... we can't live forever in the shadow of the early 90's FDIV bug, we need to move on.
As someone fresh out of school who'd previously assumed that CPUs basically worked, I was surprised by how many bugs we were able to find.
We should expect to see a lot more of these when people realize that it's much easier than it seems to find these bugs.

There's always going to be a trade off between development speed and bug rate and the optimal point probably isn't zero bugs. But we're now regularly seeing severe bugs with security implications, which changes the tradeoff a lot.
posts.key_insight: Intel's competitive pressure from ARM led to a documented cultural shift around 2013 that prioritized development velocity over validation rigor, transforming CPU bugs from rare annoyances into regular security vulnerabilities with catastrophic potential for cloud infrastructure.

posts.summary: Traditional sampling profilers like perf are widely used but fundamentally inadequate for debugging tail latency and rare performance problems because they aggregate events into averages and sample too infrequently (typically 1kHz). The post demonstrates through several Google case studies how tracing frameworks that capture every event at much higher resolution (with ~1% overhead) can reveal critical performance bugs that sampling profilers miss—including lock contention causing 50us waits, batched reindexing causing 1800ms email delays, and CPU quota throttling causing 250ms periodic stalls across entire machines. While Google's internal tracing tools can debug these problems effectively, the public tooling ecosystem remains fragmented and high-overhead, leaving most engineers stuck with profilers that can't see the performance problems that increasingly matter.
key_points: Sampling profilers aggregate to averages and can't effectively debug tail latency problems, which are increasingly important for user experience and revenue
Perf samples at ~1kHz due to interrupt overhead, which violates Nyquist theorem for observing high-frequency performance phenomena like p-state changes
Google's tracing framework runs at ~1% overhead by injecting tracing code into execution stream rather than using periodic interrupts

Critical bugs found by tracing include: RPC slowdowns from scheduler affinity policies, Gmail 1800ms delays from batched reindexing, and 9-minute disk stalls from CPU quota throttling affecting 25% of servers
Building a minimal tracing system requires only ~32MB/s bandwidth and 1-4GB RAM to capture 200k events/sec/core with sub-100ns resolution
money_quotes: Sampling profilers, the most common performance debugging tool, are notoriously bad at debugging problems caused by tail latency because they aggregate events into averages. But tail latency is, by definition, not average.
Dick Sites says that fixing this bug paid for his salary for a decade.

We know from the Nyquist theorem that, to observe a signal with some frequency, X, we have to sample with a rate at least 2X. There are a lot of factors of performance that have a frequency higher than 1kHz (e.g., CPU p-state changes), so we should expect that we're unable to directly observe a lot of things that affect performance with perf or other traditional sampling profilers.
It's really common to hear stories of bugs that can take an unbounded amount of time to debug if the proper tools aren't available.
Tracing tools are cumbersome enough that [Brendan Gregg's] top recommendation on his page about tracing tools is to learn a profiling tool!
posts.key_insight: The dominant performance debugging paradigm—sampling profilers—is fundamentally mismatched to the problems that matter most (tail latency), yet superior tracing approaches remain inaccessible outside companies like Google, creating a tooling gap that causes critical performance bugs to go undetected for years.

posts.summary: Tech companies claim to hire 'only the best' but systematically reject talented engineers for superficial reasons like using the 'wrong' tech stack, working as contractors, or lacking brand-name credentials. This strategy forces them to compete for the same tiny pool of candidates that Google and Facebook are willing to pay $200k+ for new grads, while passing over equally capable engineers who are undervalued by the market. The author argues this is both economically irrational and creates path-dependent career outcomes where random early-career events follow people for decades, similar to how graduating during a recession permanently impacts lifetime earnings.
key_points: Companies reject experienced candidates for trivial reasons (wrong tech stack, 'too random' experience, contractor status) while claiming to hire 'only the best'
Hyperfocusing on trendy candidates means competing with Google/Facebook offering $200k+ packages to new grads, making it nearly impossible to hire at non-competitive salaries
The same person can be 'unhireable' or highly sought-after based purely on superficial credentials like school or previous employer, despite unchanged actual abilities
Companies spend enormous resources on hiring 'the best' but typically invest almost nothing in training, tooling, or processes that would make average engineers highly productive
External shocks and random events create path-dependent career outcomes that compound over decades, similar to how graduating during a recession permanently reduces lifetime earnings
money_quotes: You are competing for talent with the wealthiest (or most overfunded) tech companies in the market.

We interview people whose only prior work experience is 'Line of Business .NET Developer', and they end up showing us how to write exploits for elliptic curve partial nonce bias attacks that involve Fourier transforms and BKZ lattice reduction steps that take 6 hours to run.
What's the point of hiring 'the best' and then crippling them? You can get better results by hiring undistinguished folks and setting them up for success, and it's a lot cheaper.
If you wouldn't hire someone before they worked at Google and would after, the main thing you know is that the person is above average at whiteboard algorithms quizzes (or got lucky one day).

We like to think that we're different from all those industries that judge people based on appearance, but we do the same thing, only instead of saying that people are a bad fit because they don't wear ties, we say they're a bad fit because they do.
posts.key_insight: Tech companies irrationally compete for the same overpriced 'trendy' candidates while rejecting equally talented engineers for superficial reasons, when they could achieve better results more cheaply by hiring undervalued people and investing in training, tools, and processes.

posts.summary: Dan Luu's detailed notes on Google's SRE book reveal how Google approaches reliability through software engineering principles rather than traditional operations. Key insights include error budgets that align incentive between SRE and product teams (allowing teams to 'spend' unreliability to move faster), the 50% cap on operational work to force automation, and blameless postmortems. The notes emphasize that achieving reliability requires treating it as a first-class engineering problem with concrete metrics, automation, and thoughtful tradeoffs rather than pursuing perfect uptime.
key_points: Error budgets (e.g., 99.99% uptime = 13 min downtime/quarter) create shared incentives between SRE and product teams to balance reliability and feature velocity
SREs must spend ≤50% time on ops work, forcing automation and redirecting overflow to product teams as feedback
Monitoring should require zero human interpretation for alerts; only three valid outputs: alerts (act now), tickets (act eventually), and logging (no action)
Toil (manual, repetitive, automatable work) should be minimized; teams with >50% toil indicate poor load distribution
Cascading failures often stem from queue buildup, retry storms, and systems that don't fail fast with deadlines instead of arbitrary timeouts
Load balancing and capacity planning should be intent-based (specify 'need 5 nines reliability') rather than implementation-based (specify 'need 50 cores')
money_quotes: If a human operator needs to touch your system during normal operations, you have a bug. The definition of normal changes as your systems grow.
Automation is a force multiplier, not a panacea
Going from 5 9s to 100% reliability isn't noticeable to most users and requires tremendous effort
Code is a liability
In practice, observe 2x difference between most loaded and least loaded... most expensive request can be 1000x more expensive than cheapest request
Queues do nothing in steady state. Queued requests consume memory and increase latency.
posts.key_insight: Reliability is fundamentally an engineering problem requiring quantifiable metrics, automation, and explicit tradeoffs rather than heroic effort or perfect uptime - teams should purposefully consume error budget to maximize velocity while maintaining acceptable reliability.

posts.summary: Luu examines the academic literature on concurrency bugs in both single-machine and distributed systems, finding that while most bugs are simple by count, non-deterministic concurrency bugs consume disproportionate debugging time (weeks to months per bug). He surveys numerous academic tools for detecting these bugs and notes a frustrating pattern: most published research tools are unavailable or unmaintained, while the most impactful real-world tools (TSan, Jepsen, AFL) use simpler, older techniques prioritizing practicality over novelty. The gap between academic research and usable tooling represents a major missed opportunity for industry impact.
key_points: Non-deterministic bugs are rare but extremely costly - individual bugs can take weeks or months to debug, far exceeding time spent on all other bug types combined
Most concurrency bugs are surprisingly simple: 97% of non-deadlock bugs are atomicity or ordering violations, 96% can be reproduced by fixing order of just 2 threads
Distributed systems have different bug profiles than single-machine code: 63% relate to hardware faults, 47% cause latent failures, and 70% have simple fixes
Academic tools demonstrate novel techniques but are almost universally unavailable or unmaintained, while practical impact comes from tools like TSan and Jepsen that prioritize usability
Sample bias in studies is significant - bugs that take months to debug rarely make it into academic studies because they're too time-consuming to fully characterize
money_quotes: I've spent more time debugging some individual non-deterministic bugs (weeks or months) than on all other bug types combined.

One interesting incident occurred in the open source Chrome browser. Up to 15% of known crashes were attributed to just one bug which proved difficult to understand - the Chrome engineers spent over 6 months tracking this bug without success. On the other hand, the TSAN V1 team found the reason for this bug in a 30 minute run.

Almost all of the papers for the academic tools claim to do something novel that lets them find bugs not found by other tools... But since almost no one goes and runs the older tools on any codebase, you'd never know if one of the newer tools only found a subset of the bugs that one of the older tools could catch.

Individuals like Kyle Kingsbury or Michal Zalewski have had an outsized impact on industry... you too can probably pick an underserved area in testing and have a curiously large impact on an entire industry.

Bugs like that mostly don't make it into studies like this because it's rare that a job allows someone the time to chase bugs that elusive. How many bugs like that are out there is still an open question.
posts.key_insight: The most impactful bug-finding tools succeed not through algorithmic novelty but through relentless focus on usability and availability, revealing a systemic failure in academic computer science to translate research into practical tools that practitioners can actually use.

posts.summary: This post examines whether developer compensation is becoming bimodal like it has in law and consulting, where income distributions show two distinct peaks rather than a smooth distribution. While fields like law have clear bimodal patterns driven by tournament-like up-or-out structures and lockstep pay scales, programmer compensation appears to have high dispersion but lacks the sharp separation into distinct modes. The author struggles to find a convincing explanation for why programmers are paid so well compared to other engineering fields that require similar skills, noting that common explanations like 'software is eating the world' or 'programming is hard' apply equally to fields that pay much less.
key_points: Law and consulting have developed bimodal compensation distributions over time, with a hollowing out of the middle
Limited data on programmer total compensation (including stock/bonus) makes it hard to determine if the field is truly bimodal
Programming lacks the typical barriers to high pay seen in other fields: no prestige filter, no licensing cartel, no tournament structure, and doesn't require specific credentials
High programmer pay appears driven by specific market dynamics (FB breaking wage-fixing agreements) rather than inherent properties of the field

Common explanations for high programmer pay (importance of software, difficulty, productivity multipliers) fail to explain why programming pays more than other engineering fields where the same logic applies
money_quotes: Because the mechanism for compensation increases has been arbitrary (FB could not exist, or the person who's in total control of FB, Zuckerberg, could decide on different compensation policy), it's quite arbitrary that programmer pay is as good as it is.

If their filters were perfect (as opposed to the complete joke that they're widely regarded to be), they couldn't be better than 90%-ile. Realistically, it's more likely that the median programmer at a high-paying tech company is a bit above 50%-ile.
The thing all of these theories have in common is that they apply to other fields as well, so they cannot be, as stated, the reason programmers are better paid than people in these other fields.

Any argument of the form, 'the price for X is high because X is important' implicitly assumes that there's some force constraining the supply of X. The claim that 'X is important' or 'we need a lot of X' is missing half the story.
Google alone employs 30k engineers. If 20k are programmers in the U.S. and the estimate that there are 3 million programmers in the U.S., Google alone employs 0.6% of programmers in the U.S.
posts.key_insight: High programmer compensation appears to be driven by contingent historical factors like the collapse of wage-fixing agreements rather than inherent properties of the field, and common explanations fail because they apply equally to lower-paid engineering disciplines.

posts.summary: Dan Luu recounts his circuitous 15+ year journey into programming, starting with failed childhood attempts, studying electrical engineering and math instead of CS, and working as a hardware engineer for a decade before transitioning to software. He challenges the 'you either have it or you don't' narrative about programming talent, arguing that meta-skills like debugging persistence, bug tracking, learning how to approach hard problems, and working with mentors were more valuable than any specific technical knowledge. The post emphasizes that successful programming careers rarely follow the stereotypical 'whiz kid' path and that luck, serendipity, and supportive environments play major roles that are difficult to replicate as career advice.
key_points: Spent years failing to learn programming through BASIC, high school CS classes, and college courses before eventually succeeding
Worked as an electrical engineer at Centaur for 8 years, learning debugging, automation, and problem-solving from experienced mentors
Meta-skills (debugging persistence, bug tracking, approaching hard problems, communication) proved more valuable than specific technical knowledge
Most colleagues had similarly non-traditional paths, contradicting the 'talented whiz kid' stereotype
Career success involves so much luck and serendipity that extracting generalizable advice is nearly impossible
Reading broadly had mixed results - some books transformative (Design of Everyday Things), others wasteful (too many language books)
Functional programming advocacy and language fragmentation (Perl→Ruby→Python) were well-intentioned mistakes
money_quotes: If you believe in the common 'you either have it or you don't' narrative about programmers, I definitely don't have it. And yet, I now make a living programming, and people seem to be pretty happy with the work I do.

I find that I have a meta-skill set that I use all the time. There's nothing profound about the meta-skill set, but because I often work in new (to me) problem domains, I find my meta skillset to be more valuable than my actual skills.
The most common sort of career advice I see is 'you should do what I did because I'm successful'. It's usually phrased differently, but that's the gist of it. That basically never works.
Non-deterministic hardware bugs are so much worse than non-deterministic software bugs that I always believe I can track down software bugs.

It's just that writing seems undervalued in a way that makes it relatively easy to have outsized impact through writing. But the same forces that make it easy to have outsized impact also make it difficult to get paid well!
posts.key_insight: The path to becoming a successful programmer is far more varied and luck-dependent than the industry's 'natural talent' narrative suggests, and the meta-skills of persistence, learning how to learn, and working with great mentors matter more than any specific technical knowledge or educational pedigree.

posts.summary: Dan Luu challenges Joel Spolsky's famous claim that great developers are nearly impossible to hire because they're never on the market. Luu argues this model contradicts itself—if great developers are easy to identify, companies would simply poach them. Through empirical observations, he demonstrates that the hiring market isn't a 'market for lemons' as commonly believed, but rather that hiring difficulties stem from four fixable problems: paying too little, filtering out good candidates through arbitrary criteria, seeking rare skill combinations, or being more dysfunctional than management realizes. The real scarcity isn't great developers, but rather great teams and environments worth joining.
key_points: Joel Spolsky's model assumes companies can easily identify great developers and retain them, which creates a logical contradiction—if identification is easy, poaching should be common
Information asymmetries often favor external employers over internal management, as demonstrated by engineers doing critical work that goes unrecognized internally
Good teams are harder to find than good developers—great teams fill up quickly through networks while dysfunctional teams constantly hire
Most hiring difficulties stem from four solvable problems: low compensation, arbitrary filtering, rare skill requirements, or unrecognized dysfunction
Companies systematically filter out qualified candidates through irrelevant criteria (pedigree, traditional interview performance) while fighting over the same small pool of 'trendy' candidates
money_quotes: It's actually quite common for prospective employers to have better information than current employers!
If you join a random team, you're overwhelmingly likely to join a team that has a lot of churn. Additionally, if you know of a good team, it's likely to be full.
Companies always seem to think that they pay well, even when they're not even close to being in the right range.
I've never worked at a place that doesn't filter out a lot of people who end up doing great work elsewhere.

There's often a long time period where a 'great' dev has an extremely hard time getting hired, even through their network who knows that they're great, because they don't look like what people think 'great' developers look like.
posts.key_insight: The hiring crisis in tech is largely self-inflicted—companies systematically underpay and filter out qualified candidates using arbitrary criteria, then complain about talent scarcity while great developers struggle to find good teams worth joining.

posts.summary: Dan Luu argues that while HN comments are mostly wrong and often mean-spirited, it remains the best public technical forum available because well-informed comments, though rare, tend to rise to the top. He curates dozens of excellent HN comments across topics like software engineering, business operations, and tech industry dynamics to demonstrate the hidden value buried in comment threads. The post explores what makes HN's comment quality superior to other forums, attributing it to careful ranking algorithms that penalize flamebait and heavily weight user flags, though the ultimate cause may simply be network effects and concentration of expertise.
key_points: HN comments are often wrong and mean-spirited, but well-informed comments typically float to top when they exist
By volume, there are more interesting technical insights in HN comments than in posted links
HN's ranking system penalizes high-comment-count flamebait and weights user flags heavily, unlike Reddit's simple vote cancellation
Great HN comments tend to disappear over time unlike blog posts, representing lost valuable content
HN's success may be due to network effects and concentration of expertise rather than replicable design choices
money_quotes: On topics I'm familiar with, while it's rare that a thread will have even a single comment that's well-informed, when those comments appear, they usually float to the top.

Most people realize by their 30s that prestige is a sucker's game; it's a way of inducing people to do things that aren't much fun and they wouldn't really want to do on their own, by lauding them with accolades from people they don't really care about.
Even when the site is civil, it's still committed to grind away any joy you take either in accomplishing something near or even in just sharing something interesting you learned.

Blockchain is the world's worst database, created entirely to maintain the reputations of venture capital firms who injected hundreds of millions of dollars into a technology whose core defining insight was 'You can improve on a Ponzi scam by making it self-organizing and distributed.'
My favorite developers ever all do badly in your typical stringent Silicon Valley interview. They work slower, do more thinking, and consider every line of code they write technical debt.
posts.key_insight: HN's value lies not in its average comment quality but in its unique ability to surface rare expert insights to the top, though this valuable knowledge gets lost over time unlike blog posts.

posts.summary: Luu critiques the common "must-read programming books" lists as nonsense given the field's breadth and diverse learning styles, instead offering nuanced recommendations based on actual reading experience. He systematically covers algorithms, operating systems, computer architecture, game theory, and hardware fundamentals, providing clear guidance on when to read comprehensive textbooks versus practical introductions. Throughout, he debunks the myth that certain "classic" books like CLRS or Peopleware are universally appropriate, arguing that many widely-recommended texts are impractical for self-study or mismatched to their purported audience.
key_points: Generic "must-read" lists fail because programming is too broad for universal requirements and learning preferences vary drastically
Many widely-recommended "classic" textbooks (CLRS, Kleinberg & Tardos, Peopleware) are impractical for self-study despite appearing on must-read lists
Understanding lower-level systems (OS internals, computer architecture) provides debugging superpowers and prevents massive performance waste in production systems
Textbook quality often inversely correlates with edition churn designed to extract money from students
Real learning value comes from understanding tradeoffs and developing intuition, not from memorizing formulas or achieving comprehensive coverage
money_quotes: The field is too broad for almost any topic to be required reading for all programmers, and even if a topic is that important, people's learning preferences differ too much for any book on that topic to be the best book on the topic for all people.

This book somehow manages to make it into half of these 'N books all programmers must read' lists despite being so comprehensive and rigorous that almost no practitioners actually read the entire thing.

I can think of multiple production systems at large companies that are giving up 10x to 100x in performance which are operating at a scale where even a 2x difference in performance could pay a VP's salary -- all because people didn't think through the performance implications of their design.

Modern computing is too complicated for any human to have a real full-stack understanding of what's going on. In fact, one reason modern computing can accomplish what it does is that it's possible to be productive without having a deep understanding of much of the stack that sits below the level you're operating at.
From a pure ROI perspective, I doubt learning math is 'worth it' for 99% of jobs out there.
posts.key_insight: The best technical books teach you how to reason about tradeoffs and develop intuition rather than achieve encyclopedic coverage, and the most widely-recommended "classics" are often terrible choices for self-study despite their prestige.

posts.summary: The post challenges the common misconception that large tech companies are bloated and wasteful by explaining why products like Google Search require thousands of engineers despite seeming simple on the surface. Luu demonstrates that building an MVP in a weekend is vastly different from building a production system at scale, which requires extensive work on performance optimization, internationalization, security, error handling, and countless revenue-driving features that users don't even notice. He argues that companies rationally keep hiring engineers as long as each additional engineer generates more value through cost savings, performance improvements, or revenue-increasing features than they cost to employ.
key_points: Building a simple MVP versus a production system at scale are entirely different problems - the "real" work is in all the aspects people don't think about
Performance optimization alone justifies many engineers because even small latency improvements significantly impact revenue and reduce infrastructure costs
Internationalization is far more complex than people realize - each language has unique technical challenges that require specialized knowledge
Organizational inefficiencies are much harder than technical problems - message loss and corruption rates in human organizations far exceed those in distributed systems
Companies rationally hire until marginal cost equals marginal benefit - if an engineer can add percentage points to revenue or reduce costs, they're worth hiring
money_quotes: When people imagine how long it should take to build something, they're often imagining a team that works perfectly and spends 100% of its time coding. But that's impossible to scale up.

Distributed systems are considered hard because real systems might drop something like 0.1% of messages, corrupt an even smaller percentage of messages, and see latencies in the microsecond to millisecond range. When I talk to higher-ups and compare what they think they're saying to what my coworkers think they're saying, I find that the rate of lost messages is well over 50%, every message gets corrupted, and latency can be months or years.

For reliable systems, error handling is more work than the happy path. The same thing is true for large services -- all of this stuff that people don't think of as 'real' work is more work than the core service.
A company that could eliminate organizational inefficiency would be a larger innovation than any tech startup, ever.
It's fine to ignore this stuff for a weekend-project MVP, but ignoring it in a real business means ignoring the majority of the market!
posts.key_insight: The vast majority of engineering work at large tech companies goes into optimization, internationalization, reliability, and incremental features that each add small revenue gains - work that's invisible to outsiders but essential for profitability at scale.

posts.summary: Dan Luu documents how modern web bloat makes the internet largely unusable for people with slow connections, demonstrating through extensive testing that most popular websites fail to load on dialup-speed connections despite being text-based. He tests various sites across connection speeds from fiber to 16kbps satellite, finding that even Google takes 90+ seconds on dialup while many commercial sites fail entirely. The post argues this isn't just about developing countries—millions of Americans still lack broadband, and the problem is often caused by easily fixable anti-optimizations in popular themes and frameworks.
key_points: Most modern websites are unusable on slow connections despite being fundamentally text-based, with commercial sites frequently timing out entirely on dialup-speed connections
Half of Americans don't have broadband speed according to Microsoft's measurements, and AOL alone had 2 million dialup subscribers in 2015
Simple optimizations can yield 10-20x speedup—Luu improved his own blog by that much just by moving JavaScript to the footer and removing unused dependencies
The 'page weight doesn't matter' meme is flawed because it relies on average speeds that mask the reality of users on terrible connections
Both page authors and browser developers share blame—browsers make poor decisions like opening 6 parallel connections on slow satellites, guaranteeing all timeout
money_quotes: When I was at Google, someone told me a story about a time that 'they' completed a big optimization push only to find that measured page load times increased. When they dug into the data, they found that the reason load times had increased was that they got a lot more traffic from Africa after doing the optimizations.

The flaw in the 'page weight doesn't matter because average speed is fast' is that if you average the connection of someone in my apartment building (which is wired for 1Gbps internet) and someone on 56k dialup, you get an average speed of 500 Mbps. That doesn't mean the person on dialup is actually going to be able to load a 5MB website.

It's particularly galling that airbnb doesn't test this stuff, because traveling is pretty much the whole point of the site so they can't even claim that there's no money in servicing people with poor connections.

I was able to get a 20x speedup on my blog in a few hours. You might argue that I've now gone too far and removed too much CSS, but I got a 20x speedup for people on fast connections before making changes that affected the site's appearance.

Google's AMP currently has > 100kB of blocking javascript that has to load before the page loads! There's no reason for me to use AMP pages because AMP is slower than my current setup of pure HTML with a few lines of embedded CSS.
posts.key_insight: Web performance optimization is treated as optional nicety for nerds, but the reality is that poor optimization excludes millions of users with slow connections from accessing fundamentally text-based content, and most sites could achieve 10-20x improvements with minimal effort by just avoiding blatant anti-optimizations.

posts.summary: Widely cited claims from Apple researcher Bruce Tognazzini that mice are faster than keyboards for all tasks are based on unreproducible studies and one bogus microbenchmark. When the author actually tests various tasks (search-and-replace, text selection, scrolling), results are mixed and predictable: keyboards excel at some tasks, mice at others. The literature on keyboard vs. mouse speed is plagued by issues including outdated studies from the 1980s, artificial microbenchmarks, vague descriptions, and tests on unskilled users that don't generalize to experienced programmers.
key_points: AskTog claims based on $50M Apple R&D are unreproducible - no experimental details or data are provided, just blanket assertions
The one cited experiment (replacing 'e' with '|' using cursor keys vs mouse) is bizarrely artificial and disallows basic editor features like search-and-replace
Author's own tests show mixed results: keyboard is 2x faster for search-and-replace (1.26s vs 5.39s), mouse is 2.5x faster for selecting visible text blocks (0.7s vs 1.8s), scrolling is roughly equal

Published literature is largely irrelevant due to age (1980s studies on inferior hardware), narrow microbenchmarks, vague descriptions, or testing unskilled users who take 2 seconds to move a mouse across screen
Programmers can accurately predict which input method will be faster for specific tasks, contrary to Tog's claim that users are deluded about keyboard speed
money_quotes: I'm deeply skeptical of broad claims that come from microbenchmarks unless the author has a demonstrated, deep, understanding of benchmarking their particular domain, and even then I'll ask why they believe their result generalizes.
The e -> | replacement task described is not only a microbenchmark, it's a bizarrely artificial microbenchmark.

There's nothing wrong with studying populations who aren't relatively expert at using computer input devices, but there is something wrong with generalizing those results to people who are relatively expert.

It's entirely possible that the mysterious studies Tog's org spent $50M on prove that the mouse is faster than the keyboard for all tasks other than raw text input, but there doesn't appear to be enough information to tell what the actual studies were.
This result is so boring that it would barely be worth mentioning if not for the large groups of people who believe that either the keyboard is always faster than the mouse or vice versa.
posts.key_insight: The influential Apple studies claiming mice are universally faster than keyboards are based on unreproducible research and artificial microbenchmarks that ignore how skilled users actually work, while empirical testing shows the obvious truth that each input method excels at different tasks.

posts.summary: Dan Luu challenges the common startup pitch that equity compensation has higher expected value than cash packages from large tech companies. He argues that if startup options were truly as valuable as claimed, companies could sell more equity to VCs and pay employees in cash instead. Through detailed analysis of how employee stock options differ from investor preferred stock (liquidation preferences, dilution, exercise windows), tax implications, and historical data showing VC funds don't outperform the market, he demonstrates that most startup equity packages are worth significantly less than advertised. The piece systematically dismantles both cynical and non-cynical justifications for option-heavy compensation, concluding that the arrangement primarily benefits founders and investors at employees' expense.
key_points: Employee common stock options are fundamentally different from VC preferred stock due to liquidation preferences, anti-dilution clauses, and dilution effects
VC funds don't outperform other investment classes on average, suggesting startup equity doesn't carry a justified risk premium
Most startup options are significantly overvalued when presented to candidates through misleading metrics like strike price or last valuation
Common justifications like retention and incentive alignment are contradicted by evidence from cash-paying companies like Netflix
Over 50% of options are returned to the pool when employees leave, making them effectively free or heavily discounted for companies
money_quotes: If you wanted to get rich, how would you do it? I think your best bet would be to start or join a startup. That's been a reliable way to get rich for hundreds of years" and "Risk and reward are always proportionate." This risk-reward assertion is used to back the claim that people can make more money, in expectation, by joining startups and taking risky equity packages than they can by taking jobs that pay cash or cash plus public equity. However, the premise — that risk and reward are always proportionate — isn't true in the general case.

If you look at companies that have made a lot of people rich, like Microsoft, Google, Apple, and Facebook, almost none of the employees who became rich had an instrumental role in the company's success.

As an employee who gets startup options, you don't (directly) pay VC fees, which means you can beat the market by keeping the VC fees for yourself. Another argument is that, some investors (like YC) seem to consistently do pretty well. If you join a startup that's funded by a savvy investors, you too can do pretty well. For this to make sense, you have to realize that the company is worth more than "expected" while the company doesn't have the same realization because you need the company to give you an option package without properly accounting for its value.

At rates big companies are paying today (mid-band of perhaps $380k/yr for senior engineer, $600k/yr for staff engineer), working at a big company and spending $25k/yr investing in startups is strictly superior to working at a startup from the standpoint of financial return.
