Maybe so, but that's not a counter-argument — it's an argument that the most startups don't have options that are valuable enough to be exchanged for the equivalent sum of money, i.e., that the options simply aren't as valuable as claimed.
posts.key_insight: The persistent use of stock options over cash compensation in startups is better explained by information asymmetry and the ability to obscure true compensation value than by any legitimate financial or incentive-alignment benefit to employees.

posts.summary: Dan Luu systematically measures terminal emulator latency and finds that most terminals add 30-100ms of delay from keypress to display, enough to be perceptible to users. He argues the terminal ecosystem is optimized for the wrong benchmarks—stdout throughput and memory usage—when latency and responsiveness matter far more for user experience. His measurements show significant variance across terminals and demonstrate that tail latency under load becomes even more problematic, with 99.9th percentile latencies reaching levels comparable to cross-country network roundtrips.
key_points: Measured latency from keypress to screen update across major terminals ranges from 5-45ms at median, with tail latencies exceeding 100ms under load
Terminal benchmarks focus on stdout throughput and memory usage, which have minimal impact on user experience compared to latency
Research shows humans notice latency differences in the 10-100ms range, yet terminal tail latencies routinely exceed perceptible thresholds
The terminal ecosystem lacks awareness of latency issues because unlike tablets (vs pen-and-paper) or VR (vs real vision), users have no low-latency reference point for keyboard input
Common terminals can sink stdout orders of magnitude faster than humans can read, making further throughput optimization largely pointless
money_quotes: The idea that computers respond quickly to input, so quickly that humans can't notice the latency, is the most common performance-related fallacy I hear from professional programmers.
For reference, the internally generated keypress to GPU memory trip for some terminals is slower than the time it takes to send a packet from Boston to Seattle and back, about 70ms.
Say I type at 120wpm and that results in 600 characters per minute, or 10 characters per second of input. Then I'd expect to see the 99.9% tail (1 in 1000) every 100 seconds!
The relationship between the rate that a terminal can sink stdout and its latency is non-obvious.
Most terminals have enough latency that the user experience could be improved if the terminals concentrated more on latency and less on other features or other aspects of performance.
posts.key_insight: Terminal developers optimize for easily measured but largely irrelevant metrics like stdout throughput while ignoring the latency that actually degrades user experience, demonstrating how misaligned benchmarks lead to misguided optimization efforts.

posts.summary: This post provides a technical deep-dive into CPU branch prediction, explaining why modern processors predict branch outcomes and how various prediction algorithms evolved from simple schemes to sophisticated multi-level predictors. Luu methodically walks through increasingly complex prediction strategies—from always predicting 'taken' (70% accuracy) to hybrid predictors combining local and global state (96% accuracy)—demonstrating how each improvement addresses specific weaknesses in prior approaches. The central argument is that CPUs are actually simpler and more understandable than most programmers assume, because hardware complexity is constrained by physical costs, performance overhead, and verification requirements that don't limit software in the same way.
key_points: Branch prediction is essential because modern pipelined CPUs need to start executing subsequent instructions before knowing which path a branch will take
Without branch prediction, a 20-stage pipeline executing typical code (20% branches) would run at 4.8 cycles per instruction versus 1 cycle with perfect prediction—nearly a 5x penalty
Branch prediction evolved from simple static schemes (always predict taken, backward-taken-forward-not-taken) to sophisticated dynamic predictors using local/global history and hybrid approaches
Modern predictors like gshare, agree, and TAGE achieve 93-96% accuracy by reducing interference between aliased branches and adapting predictions based on pattern history
Hardware is conceptually simpler than software because physical constraints (cost, power, speed, verification requirements) push back against complexity in ways that don't limit software development
money_quotes: Since having one bit for every possible branch is too many bits to feasibly store, we'll keep a table of some number of branches we've seen and their last results.
At 5 GHz, one unit of work is one-fifth of one nanosecond. For reference, light travels roughly one foot in one nanosecond.
If you think about the complexity of software, the main limiting factor on complexity is your imagination. If you can imagine something in enough detail that you can write it down, you can make it.

A side effect of these factors that push back against chip complexity is that, for any particular 'high-level' general purpose CPU feature, it is generally conceptually simple enough that it can be described in a half-hour or hour-long talk.

Every chunk of hardware you implement costs money, so you want to implement as little hardware as possible. Additionally, performance matters for most hardware, and adding complexity makes hardware slower.
posts.key_insight: Modern CPUs are more conceptually accessible than programmers assume because physical and economic constraints force hardware designs toward elegant simplicity, whereas software complexity is limited only by human imagination and organizational capability.

posts.summary: The post explains Sattolo's algorithm, which generates random permutations with exactly one cycle, contrasting it with the more common Fisher-Yates shuffle. Luu provides an accessible proof of why the algorithm works without requiring advanced mathematical machinery like Stirling numbers, motivated by finding that existing explanations were unnecessarily complex and that many people were misusing the algorithm. The post includes detailed proofs of correctness, discussion of derangements, and critiques common implementation mistakes in shuffling algorithms.
key_points: Sattolo's algorithm differs from Fisher-Yates by just two characters but produces fundamentally different results: permutations with exactly one cycle rather than uniformly random permutations
The algorithm maintains the invariant that each cycle has exactly one swappable element, which guarantees merging two cycles with each swap
There are (n-1)! permutations with exactly one cycle, matching the number of outputs Sattolo's generates with uniform probability
Many people incorrectly use Sattolo's to generate random derangements, but it only produces derangements with one cycle, missing valid multi-cycle derangements
Incorrect shuffling implementations have appeared in production systems including games, gambling sites, and Microsoft's browser choice poll
money_quotes: I find that this is common for explanations of concepts that could, but don't have to, use a lot of mathematical machinery...for people who are only interested in a single result, I think it's unfortunate that it's hard to find a relatively simple explanation that doesn't require any background.
The key invariant that's maintained is that each cycle has exactly1swappable element.
Although I said that you probably don't need to know this stuff, you do actually need to know it if you're going to implement a custom shuffling algorithm!
At the time, the top Google hit for javascript random array sort was the incorrect algorithm that Microsoft ended up using.
I really enjoy the naive rejection based algorithm because I find it delightful when basic randomized algorithms that consist of 'keep trying again' work well.
posts.key_insight: Simple algorithms can have subtle correctness requirements that are often misunderstood in practice, and accessible explanations without heavy mathematical machinery are valuable for practitioners who need to understand a single concept rather than master an entire field.

posts.summary: This post reproduces decade-old filesystem robustness studies to test how modern filesystems (ext4, btrfs, etc.) handle errors at the block device level. While write error propagation has improved since 2005, most filesystems still don't detect silent data corruption (except btrfs/zfs which use checksums). The investigation also examines internal error handling code and finds that many function return values that indicate write failures are still frequently ignored in the Linux kernel, though less so than in 2008.
key_points: Modern filesystems now correctly propagate write errors when block devices report failures, unlike in 2005 when ext3 and jfs would ignore them
Only btrfs and zfs checksum data to detect silent corruption; most filesystems (including Apple's apfs) rely entirely on hardware error detection
Internal kernel code still frequently ignores error return values from critical write functions, though the rate has improved from ~30% checking in 2008 to ~30-71% checking in 2017
C's error handling mechanisms make it easy to accidentally drop errors even with annotations like __must_check, as errors can propagate through many layers before being ignored
Funding work on correctness is difficult because consumers don't strongly incentivize it and open source contributors face perverse incentives (paid in complaints rather than cash)
money_quotes: More than 20% of flash drives develop uncorrectable errors in a four year period, 30-80% develop bad blocks and 2-7% of them develop bad chips.
Who drives their car around banging against the guardrail saying, 'Whoa! I'm glad I've got these guardrails because I'd never make it to the show on time.'
doing open source work is like doing normal paid work, except that you get paid in complaints instead of cash

A backup solution that works 90% of the time is quite bad, but if you have one that works 90% of the time, it will still have staunch defenders who drop into discussions to say things like 'I've restored from backup three times and it's never failed!'
If Kyle hadn't come along, would most of the bugs that he and his tool found still plague open source distributed systems today? That's a scary thought.
posts.key_insight: Despite a decade of development, most filesystems still lack basic data integrity checks and kernel code continues to ignore error conditions at concerning rates, suggesting that correctness work remains chronically underfunded and undervalued in both industry and open source.

posts.summary: Dan Luu empirically tests keyboard latency across 20+ keyboards and finds that most modern keyboards add 30-60ms of latency between keypress and USB output—more than the entire end-to-end pipeline of fast computers from the 1970s. Surprisingly, gaming keyboards marketed on speed claims like "1000Hz polling" and "8x faster response" perform no better than regular keyboards, while Apple's minimalist Magic keyboard (15ms) is the fastest tested. The research demonstrates that keyboard latency is both significant enough to be human-perceptible (studies show people detect latencies down to 2ms) and contributes meaningfully to the latency bloat that makes modern systems feel less responsive than decades-old machines despite vastly superior hardware.
key_points: Median keyboard latency (30-55ms) exceeds the entire keypress-to-screen pipeline of 1970s computers like the Apple 2
Gaming keyboards tested were not faster than non-gaming keyboards despite marketing claims about 1000Hz polling and faster response times
Apple Magic keyboard had the lowest latency at 15ms, attributed to minimal key travel distance
Research shows humans can perceive latencies as low as 2ms and that increased latency causes users to execute tasks less accurately
Major latency sources are key travel time, slow matrix scanning rates, and debouncing—all of which could be engineered to be much faster
money_quotes: In general, my belief is that if someone makes performance claims without benchmarks, the claims probably aren't true, just like how code that isn't tested (or otherwise verified) should be assumed broken.
The median keyboard I tested has more latency than the entire end-to-end pipeline of the Apple 2.
Keyboards are often more powerful than entire computers from the 70s and 80s! And yet, the median keyboard today adds as much latency as the entire end-to-end pipeline as a fast machine from the 70s.
This line of argument is like saying that you wouldn't notice a flight being delayed by an hour because the duration of the flight is six hours.
As a human, you don't activate the switch, you press the key. A measurement that starts from switch activation time misses this large component to latency.
posts.key_insight: Marketing claims about performance are worthless without empirical measurement, and modern keyboard latency is a measurable, human-perceptible contributor to system responsiveness that could be easily fixed but isn't because manufacturers prioritize cost savings over user experience.

posts.summary: Using Android marketshare data from 2010-2017, this analysis demonstrates that Android devices are becoming increasingly outdated over time, with nearly half of the two billion active devices being at least two years behind on updates as of 2017. The data shows that new Android version adoption is slowing down, creating over one billion devices stuck on old versions that will likely never be updated. This contrasts sharply with iOS, where new versions achieve 50% adoption within months, and compares unfavorably even to Windows XP, which received twelve years of security updates.
key_points: Nearly 50% of Android's 2+ billion active devices are two years out of date, with approximately zero chance of ever updating
Android version adoption is slowing over time - recent releases show shallower uptake curves than previous versions
The data understates the problem by measuring API versions (not point releases), showing only Play Store users, and using marketshare rather than absolute device counts
iOS 11 achieved ~50% marketshare within two months vs <1% for the latest Android version released three months prior
Even Windows XP received 12+ years of updates, while typical Android devices from the same era got 1.5-2 years
money_quotes: At this point, we should expect that there are more than one billion devices that are two years out of date! Given Android's update model, we should expect approximately 0% of those devices to ever get updated to a modern version of Android.
This is tremendously unfair to Windows XP, which was released on 10/2001 and got security updates until 4/2014, twelve and a half years later.

iOS 11 was released two months ago and it now has just under 50% iOS marketshare despite November's numbers coming before the release of the iPhone X (this is compared to < 1% marketshare for the latest Android version, which was released in August).

It looks like Android is still likely to stay behind iOS in terms of updates for a while. Google has promised that its latest phone (Pixel 2, 10/2017) will get updates for three years... But if you have an iPhone, the latest version of iOS (released 9/2017) works on devices back to the iPhone 5S (released 9/2013).
Android's scale is unprecedented in the desktop world. The were roughly 200 million PCs sold in 2017. Samsung alone has been selling that many mobile devices per year since 2008.
posts.key_insight: Data-driven analysis reveals that Android's update problem is worse than commonly understood and getting progressively worse over time, with the unprecedented scale of Android deployment creating billions of permanently outdated, potentially insecure devices that will persist for decades.

posts.summary: Luu challenges the efficient market hypothesis by examining decision-making in baseball and board games, where outcomes are easily measurable and data is abundant. He demonstrates that even with clear statistical evidence showing decisions cost teams multiple wins per season (worth millions of dollars), managers persisted with demonstrably inferior strategies like excessive sac bunts, stolen base attempts, and intentional walks for decades. Despite financial incentives, accessible data, and rigorous analysis available since the 1970s, cultural resistance and tolerance for high-variance outcomes delayed adoption of evidence-based decision-making until the 2000s-2010s, suggesting similar or worse decision quality in less measurable business and engineering domains.
key_points: Statistical analysis shows conventional baseball decisions (sac bunts, steals, intentional walks, batting order) cost teams 3-5 wins per season, worth tens of millions in player value
Despite clear evidence since the 1970s and massive financial incentives, teams took decades to adopt data-driven strategies, with some decisions only improving after 2012
Board game players confidently advocate losing strategies despite having publicly visible Elo ratings proving their poor performance
High variance in outcomes makes it easy to justify bad decisions and penalizes managers who make statistically correct but unconventional choices
If decision-making is this poor in easily measurable domains with clear incentives, it's likely worse in business and engineering where outcomes are far more ambiguous
money_quotes: A statement I commonly hear in tech-utopian circles is that some seeming inefficiency can't actually be inefficient because the market is efficient and inefficiencies will quickly be eliminated.

Stats nerds were talking about how you could do this at least since 1971 and no team really took it seriously until the 90s and the ideas didn't really become mainstream until the mid 2000s, after a bestseller had been published.

The thing that's odd about this is, how come the low-ranked players so confidently argue that their position is correct? Not only do they get per-game information indicating that they're wrong (because they often lose), they have a rating that aggregates all of their gameplay and tells them, roughly, how good they are.

You can't even tell if this is happening by asking people if they're making rigorous, data-driven, decisions. If you'd ask people in baseball they were using data in their decisions, they would've said yes throughout the 70s and 80s.

It's too simplistic to say that we expect a firm to make good decisions because they're exposed to markets and there's economic value in making good decisions and people within the firm will probably be rewarded greatly if they make good decisions.
posts.key_insight: Strong financial incentives and readily available data are insufficient to drive good decision-making when cultural inertia, high variance outcomes, and resistance to unconventional ideas allow decision-makers to dismiss clear statistical evidence for decades.

posts.summary: Dan Luu argues that software companies regularly break UI backwards compatibility in ways that exploit users' muscle memory, causing them to perform unintended actions. He contrasts this with the strict API backwards compatibility policies of systems like Linux and Windows, noting that UI changes are arguably even more problematic since users can't mechanically update their habits like code can be mechanically refactored. While maintaining UI compatibility creates constraints, Luu suggests that UI stability may be undervalued, pointing to successful products with frozen or dated interfaces, and proposes deprecation warnings for UI changes as a potential middle ground.
key_points: Apps regularly change UIs in ways that trick users into doing the opposite of what they intend (hiding vs. saving posts, sending messages to wrong recipients)
Software has strong traditions of API backwards compatibility (Linux, Windows) but almost no equivalent culture for UI compatibility
UI backwards compatibility is arguably MORE important than API compatibility because habits can't be mechanically updated like code
Many successful products (Craigslist, LinkedIn during growth phase) succeeded with frozen or dated UIs, suggesting UI churn may be overvalued
UI deprecation warnings (like no-op placeholders pointing to new locations) could help users retrain muscle memory without causing harmful accidents
money_quotes: The really 'great' thing about a change like this is that it A/B tests incredibly well if you measure new feature 'engagement' by number of clicks because many users will accidentally save a story when they meant to hide it.
There's no equivalent way to reach into people's brains and change user habits, so a breaking UI change inevitably results in pain for some users.
It sure would be nice if shortcuts didn't break so often that I spend as much time consciously stopping myself from using shortcuts as I do actually using the app.
We simply do not break user space. (Linus Torvalds quote on API backwards compatibility)
Programmers seem especially prone to coming into an area they're not familiar with and telling experts how they should solve their problems.
posts.key_insight: Software companies systematically undervalue UI stability and backwards compatibility compared to API compatibility, despite UI changes being harder for users to adapt to and potentially more damaging to user experience and retention.

posts.summary: Dan Luu empirically measured input-to-display latency across 40 years of computing devices (1977-2017) and found that most modern computers are significantly slower than machines from the 1970s and 80s. An Apple 2e from 1983 responds in 30ms while typical modern laptops range from 100-200ms. The culprit isn't raw processing power but architectural complexity: modern systems involve multiple process handoffs, buffering layers, and context switches that ancient machines simply didn't have, and the industry optimizes for throughput benchmarks rather than end-to-end latency.
key_points: Empirical measurements show computers from the late 1970s/early 1980s (Apple 2e, Commodore PET) had 30-60ms latency versus 100-200ms for most modern computers
Only expensive gaming PCs with high refresh rate displays (165Hz) and iPad Pros can match the responsiveness of 40-year-old machines
The latency bloat comes from complexity at every layer: slower keyboard scanning (100-200Hz vs 556Hz), display buffering, OS handoffs between processes, and cross-process locks
Modern systems are optimized for throughput benchmarks (SPEC2006, Dhrystone) rather than end-to-end user-perceived latency, leading to 'you get what you measure' problem
Reducing latency typically requires adding more complexity (high refresh displays, careful buffer management) rather than simplification
money_quotes: It's a bit absurd that a modern gaming machine running at 4,000x the speed of an apple 2, with a CPU that has 500,000x as many transistors (with a GPU that has 2,000,000x as many transistors) can maybe manage the same latency as an apple 2 in very carefully coded applications if we have a monitor with nearly 3x the refresh rate.
In practice, the solution to poor performance caused by 'excess' complexity is often to add more complexity.
You get what you measure.
A lot of the complexity might be called accidental complexity, but most of that accidental complexity is there because it's so convenient.

It's perhaps even more absurd that the default configuration of the powerspec g405, which had the fastest single-threaded performance you could get until October 2017, had more latency from keyboard-to-screen (approximately 3 feet, maybe 10 feet of actual cabling) than sending a packet around the world.
posts.key_insight: Despite 40 years of exponential hardware improvements, most modern computers feel slower than 1970s machines because the industry optimizes for measurable throughput benchmarks rather than unmeasured end-to-end user latency, demonstrating that you literally get what you measure.

posts.summary: Dan Luu conducted a 339-game randomized experiment in Overwatch to test whether players receive different treatment based on perceived gender. Playing with masculine versus feminine usernames, he found that players with feminine names were told how to play or asked to switch characters at roughly 3x the rate (19% vs 6% in competitive mode), with qualitatively harsher criticism. Sexual comments directed at players were rare in both conditions, contradicting common assumptions but consistent with prior survey research. The study highlights how empirical testing can resolve longstanding online debates more efficiently than thousands of comment threads.
key_points: Ran 339 games with masculine vs feminine usernames, tracking sexual comments, being told how to play, insults, and compliments
Feminine names received significantly more unsolicited gameplay advice (19% vs 6% in competitive, 4% vs 1% in quick play)
Sexual comments directed at players were similarly rare in both conditions, contrary to expectations
Qualitative differences were stark: masculine condition received polite requests, feminine condition often involved sustained harassment
Study demonstrates that empirical experiments can settle debates more effectively than endless online discussions
money_quotes: In less time than has been spent on a single large discussion, we could just run the experiment, so here it is.

For example, the one time someone asked me to switch characters in the masculine condition, the request was a one sentence, polite, request. When using the feminine name, a typical case would involve 1-4 people calling me human garbage for most of the game and consoling themselves with the idea that the entire reason our team is losing is that I won't change characters.
My median blog post is more rigorous than my median paper or, for that matter, the median paper that I read.
A lot of demands to switch and general vitriol came during my best performances with the feminine account.

Most of the analysis uses the number of games in which a type of comment occured and not tone to avoid having to code comments as having a certain tone in order to avoid possibly injecting bias into the process.
posts.key_insight: Empirical experimentation with proper controls reveals that gendered harassment in online gaming manifests primarily through disproportionate unsolicited advice and qualitatively harsher criticism rather than overt sexual comments, demonstrating how thousands of hours of debate could be replaced by actually running the experiment.

posts.summary: This talk systematically dismantles the myth that file I/O is simple by examining failures at every layer of the storage stack. Luu demonstrates that writing a single file safely requires navigating filesystem-specific quirks, non-atomic operations, and ordering guarantees that even expert developers routinely get wrong. Through empirical evidence from academic research, he shows that filesystems drop errors at alarming rates, disks corrupt data millions of times more frequently than datasheets claim, and even 'power loss protection' features often fail in practice. The underlying issue isn't technical ignorance but economic misalignment: tech giants invest heavily in data integrity because they bear the costs of corruption, while consumer software vendors can externalize those costs to users.
key_points: Writing a single file atomically requires careful use of fsync, checksums, directory syncing, and handling filesystem-specific modes - complexity that causes bugs even in databases and version control systems
Filesystem error handling is fundamentally broken: errors are routinely dropped or reported to wrong processes, and fsync failures are essentially unrecoverable on Linux
Actual disk error rates are 500,000 to 5 million times worse than vendor datasheets claim, with SSDs showing uncorrectable bit error rates of 2e-9 instead of promised 1e-16
Common 'simple tricks' like atomic rename or append-only writes don't actually work safely across different filesystems and crash scenarios
The core problem is economic rather than technical: companies bear costs of data loss proportional to their incentive to prevent it, leaving consumer data poorly protected
money_quotes: Many of the same things that make concurrent programming hard are the same things that make writing to files safely hard, so of course we should expect that writing to files is hard!
Depending on the type of drive, 2e-9 is 2 gigabits, or 250 MB, 500 thousand to 5 million times worse than stated on datasheets depending on the class of drive.

When a drive advertises that it has power loss protection, this appears to mean that someone spent some amount of effort implementing something that will, under some circumstances, prevent data loss or data corruption under power loss.
User data gets corrupted regardless of who's 'fault' the bug is, and as we've seen, bugs can persist in the filesystem layer for many years.
We know how to make this stuff pretty reliable. It's hard, and it takes a lot of time and effort, i.e., a lot of money, but it can be done.
posts.key_insight: File I/O safety is not a solved problem but an economic one: the technical solutions exist and are deployed by companies who bear the cost of data loss, while consumer software remains unsafe because vendors can externalize corruption costs to users who lack the sophistication to assign blame.

posts.summary: Dan Luu argues that algorithms interviews fail to achieve their stated purpose of preventing inefficient code in production. Despite companies rigorously testing candidates on algorithms problems, he routinely finds trivial, interview-level inefficiencies in production code at major tech companies—bugs worth millions of dollars annually that persist because teams aren't incentivized to fix them. The real issue isn't hiring people who can't solve algorithms problems, but rather creating misaligned incentive structures that discourage engineers from applying that knowledge to make systems more efficient.
key_points: Major tech companies have interview-level algorithms bugs in production worth millions annually in wasted compute
Examples include O(n) resizable array implementations and unnecessary allocations that each cost more than the author's lifetime earnings to fix
Engineers can pass algorithms interviews but aren't incentivized to apply that knowledge on the job
Companies that don't use algorithms interviews but do incentivize efficiency have fewer of these problems
Current interview practices are likely another fad, similar to past fads of brainteasers and trivia questions
money_quotes: even though a decent fraction of the value I've provided for companies has been solving phone-screen level algorithms problems on the job, I can't pass algorithms interviews!
Trying to get people to solve algorithms problems on the job by asking algorithms questions in interviews doesn't work.
I'm asking teams to do some work and take on some risk to do something that's worthless to them.

I think it would be pretty surprising if this wasn't a fad. I would be less surprised to discover that current techniques were not a fad if people were doing or referring to empirical research or had independently discovered what works.
companies get what they incentivize. If they expect training to come from developers but don't value it as much as they value dev work, then there's going to be a shortage of training.
posts.key_insight: The disconnect between what companies test for in interviews and what they incentivize on the job creates a situation where multi-million dollar inefficiencies persist despite hiring engineers who could easily fix them if given the right incentives.

posts.summary: This post examines suspicious discontinuities across diverse domains - from tax policy to sports to drug prosecutions - where sharp thresholds create perverse incentives and non-smooth distributions. The author demonstrates how discontinuities lead to predictable gaming behavior: people losing money to qualify for health insurance subsidies, teachers inflating borderline test scores, prosecutors charging exactly at mandatory minimum thresholds, and birth month determining athletic careers. The common thread is that artificial bright-line rules create step functions in outcomes where smooth gradients would be more rational, and basic visualization techniques (histograms, scatterplots) reliably expose these patterns.
key_points: U.S. tax policy contains sharp income thresholds that can incentivize people to deliberately lose money to qualify for subsidies
Discontinuities appear across diverse domains: queues, college admissions, election fraud, drug charges, test scores, sports selection, and procurement auctions
Basic visualization tools (histograms, scatterplots, CDFs) are effective at detecting suspicious patterns caused by threshold effects
Randomization and gradual phase-outs are common solutions to smooth out problematic discontinuities
money_quotes: That means if an individual buying ACA insurance was going to earn $55k, they'd be better off reducing their income by $6440 and getting under the $48,560 subsidy ceiling than they are earning $55k.
A tax system that encourages people to lose money, perhaps by funneling it to (on average) much wealthier options traders by buying put options, seems sub-optimal.

Among people who don't qualify for a Pell Grant, it's those with the lowest income who are the most severely impacted and have the most severely reduced probability of admission. Among people who do qualify, it's those with the highest income who are mostly likely to benefit, again the opposite of what you'd probably want.

I've gotten a lot of mileage out in my career both out of being suspicious of discontinuities and figuring out where they come from and also out of applying standard techniques to smooth out discontinuities.
Basic tools like 'drawing a scatterplot', 'drawing a histogram', 'drawing the CDF' often come in handy.
posts.key_insight: Bright-line thresholds in policy, systems, and measurement create predictable discontinuities that incentivize gaming and produce outcomes opposite to their intended purpose, but simple visualization techniques can reliably expose these patterns.

posts.summary: The author argues that reaching 95th percentile performance in most activities is surprisingly achievable because most participants don't engage in deliberate practice or seek feedback. Using Overwatch as a detailed example, he demonstrates that even highly-ranked players constantly make basic, easily-correctable mistakes. The key insight is that the gap between casual participation and focused improvement is so large that modest effort—like reviewing your own performance, getting feedback, or practicing fundamentals—can yield dramatic percentile gains without requiring exceptional talent.
key_points: 95th percentile performers in games like Overwatch regularly make simple, game-losing mistakes that could be easily fixed with basic observation and feedback
Most people who want to improve don't utilize available tools for feedback, such as recording and reviewing their own performance
Real-world activities are even easier to improve at percentile-wise because almost no one practices deliberately, unlike in competitive games
Simple interventions like practicing talks 30 times or recording yourself coding can produce dramatic improvements relative to peers
The main barrier to improvement isn't talent or time invested, but rather not understanding how to identify and fix mistakes through feedback
money_quotes: The 'one weird trick' is that, for a lot of activities, being something like 10%-ile among people who practice can make you something like 90%-ile or 99%-ile among people who participate.
At 90%-ile and 95%-ile ranks in Overwatch, the vast majority of players will pretty much constantly make basic game losing mistakes.
It's generally not unreasonable to think that people are generally trying to win when they're severely angered by losing.

It would generally be considered absurd to operate a complex software system without metrics or tracing, but it's normal to operate yourself without metrics or tracing, even though you're much more complex and harder to understand than the software you work on.
In most real life activities, there's almost no one who puts in a level of deliberate practice equivalent to someone who goes down to their local table tennis club and practices two hours a week.
posts.key_insight: Most people dramatically underestimate how achievable high percentile performance is because they conflate effort with deliberate practice—simply recording yourself, getting feedback, and fixing obvious mistakes can move you from average to top 5% in most activities.

posts.summary: Dan Luu examines how Unix command-line tools have dramatically increased in complexity since 1979, with commands like 'ls' growing from 11 options to 58 options. He argues this proliferation contradicts the Unix philosophy of 'do one thing well' but is actually inevitable given Unix's text-based design philosophy and the lack of structured data passing. While Unix pioneers like McIlroy decry this bloat, Luu contends the complexity was always going to exist somewhere—either in the tools themselves or pushed onto users who must manually parse and format text streams. The Unix philosophy worked fine for a small team in one room but fundamentally cannot scale to modern collaborative software development without creating inconsistency and accidental complexity.
key_points: Common Unix commands have grown dramatically in options count—ls went from 11 options (1979) to 58 options (2017)
The Unix philosophy of text streams ironically drove complexity because formatting and content are intermingled in plain text
Structured data passing (like PowerShell) would reduce the need for formatting options and user-held metadata
The 'same room' design philosophy doesn't scale to large numbers of contributors or long time periods
Modern shells/terminals are inconsistent across systems in ways that dwarf the incoherence of mocked languages like PHP
money_quotes: Saying that 'text' is a universal format is like saying that 'binary' is a universal format.

At least with tools that bundle up more functionality and options than is UNIX-y users can replace a gigantic set of wildly inconsistent tools with a merely large set of tools that, while inconsistent with each other, may have some internal consistency.

The sleight of hand that's happening when someone says that we can keep software simple and compatible by making everything handle text is the pretense that text data doesn't have a structure that needs to be parsed.

As a language and a standard library, any commonly used shell plus the collection of widely used *nix tools taken together is much worse [than PHP] and contains much more accidental complexity due to inconsistency.

It's not a matter of not thinking longer or harder; it's a matter of having a philosophy that cannot scale unless you have a relatively small team with a shared cultural understanding, able to sit down in the same room.
posts.key_insight: The Unix philosophy's design decisions that worked for a small colocated team in the 1970s fundamentally cannot scale to modern collaborative development, and the resulting complexity burden was always inevitable—the only question was whether it would be borne by tool maintainers or users.

posts.summary: Dan Luu examines why his personal blog often gets more traffic than corporate engineering blogs from well-funded tech companies, despite those companies having more resources and greater incentive to succeed. Through interviews with companies that have compelling blogs (Cloudflare, Heap, Segment) and those that don't, he finds that successful corporate blogs share a lightweight approval process with direct executive support, while unsuccessful ones get bogged down in months-long approval cycles with multiple stakeholders who perceive only risk and no benefit from streamlining. The key difference is high-level executives willing to take responsibility and cut through bureaucratic risk aversion, enabling engineers to publish interesting technical content quickly.
key_points: Successful corporate engineering blogs have fast approval processes (hours to weeks) with few approvers, mainly from engineering
Unsuccessful blogs have slow, multi-stakeholder approval processes (3-6 months typical, up to a year) that make posts vaguer and less interesting
High-level executive support (CTO, CEO, co-founder level) is critical to maintaining a lightweight process and overriding bureaucratic risk aversion
The natural state of engineers writing about their work is interesting; corporations have to actively suppress this to create boring blogs
Individual stakeholders in large companies perceive risk from streamlining but no personal benefit, creating a structural barrier to improvement
money_quotes: In order to have a boring blog, the corporation has to actively stop engineers from putting interesting content out there.
My opinion is that the natural state of a corp eng blog where people get a bit of feedback is a pretty interesting blog.

None of the fourteen stakeholders who'd have to sign off on approving a streamlined process care about streamlining the process since that would be good for the company in a way that doesn't really impact them, not when that would mean seemingly taking responsibility for the risk a streamlined process would add, however small.
ICs in large companies don't have the authority or don't feel like they have the authority to do something just because it makes sense.
One comment I've often heard from people at more bureaucratic companies is something like 'every company our size is like this', but that's not true.
posts.key_insight: Corporate engineering blogs fail not from lack of talent or material, but because bureaucratic risk aversion without executive intervention transforms potentially interesting technical content into months-delayed, watered-down fluff that engineers hate writing and nobody wants to read.

posts.summary: Luu describes building LongTermMetrics (LTM), a system that stores and enables SQL queries over years of infrastructure metrics data, which took one day to build and immediately found millions in cost savings. The key insight is that storing just 0.01-0.1% of relevant metrics data indefinitely in a columnar format enables interactive analysis across all services and hosts, making it trivial to find misconfigurations and optimization opportunities. Rather than using sophisticated ML or real-time systems, LTM deliberately uses boring, proven technologies (Scalding, Presto, Parquet, HDFS) that required minimal effort to integrate with existing infrastructure. The post argues that simple, boring solutions often deliver better ROI than complex cutting-edge systems, but such work is underreported because it doesn't make good conference talks or resume items.
key_points: Built LTM in one day by storing 0.01-0.1% of relevant metrics in columnar format on HDFS, enabling SQL queries across all services/hosts over any time period
Immediately found mid-7-figure optimization; shipped mid-8-figures in cost savings in first year by finding misconfigured JVM parameters, over-provisioned memory, and network bottlenecks

Deliberately chose boring, proven technologies (Scalding, Presto, Parquet) over sophisticated alternatives (ML, real-time systems, specialized databases) to minimize implementation and operational cost
Storage cost is trivial compared to value - individual optimizations found easily pay for decades of data storage
Industry bias toward complex solutions (deep learning, novel architectures) causes people to turn day-long projects into person-year projects with worse ROI
money_quotes: I think writing about systems like this, that are just boring work is really underrated. A disproportionate number of posts and talks I read are about systems using hot technologies.

If you do the math on the cost of metrics data storage for a project like this in a company in our size class, the storage cost is basically a rounding error. We've shipped individual diffs that easily pay for the storage cost for decades.
They turned a project that could return significant value to the company after a couple of person-days into a project that took person-years.

I would guess that at least 90% of metrics are things that almost no one will want to look at in almost any circumstance, and of the metrics anyone really cares about, the vast majority aren't performance related.
But the add a batch job took one day, you don't need to have roadmap and planning meetings for a day of work, you can just do it and then do follow-on work incrementally.
posts.key_insight: Boring technology choices that prioritize minimal implementation effort and operational burden often deliver vastly better ROI than sophisticated systems, but industry incentives (resume building, promotions, conference talks) bias engineers toward complex solutions that turn simple projects into multi-year endeavors.

posts.summary: Luu argues that distributed tracing can deliver high value without massive effort, contrary to common industry belief that it only works at Facebook/Google scale. Using Twitter's experience, he demonstrates how a small team (<2 person-years) transformed an underutilized trace-view system into a high-ROI analytics platform by restructuring trace data into queryable tables and building analysis tools on top. The key insight is that tracing data becomes valuable when you can run aggregations and queries across traces, not just view individual trace timelines.
key_points: Twitter's tracing infrastructure was initially low-value due to poor schema design, inability to aggregate data, biased sampling, and clock skew issues
Simple engineering fixes (GC tuning, proper queuing, fixing sampling logic) addressed immediate problems
Creating queryable tables (trace_index, span_index, anno_index, etc.) that support SQL queries unlocked immediate high ROI
Tools like Service Dependency Explorer (SDE) built on structured trace data help users understand load amplification and service dependencies without writing queries
Tracing and metrics are complementary - having only one leaves significant blind spots in understanding system behavior
money_quotes: I think this overestimates how much work it is to get a lot of value from tracing.
Just having this set of tables, queryable with SQL queries... is enough for tracing to pay for itself, to go from being difficult to justify to being something that's obviously high value.
I'd estimate that there's been less than 2 person-years worth of work on everything discussed in this post.
This difference between the complexity of metrics data and tracing data makes the value add for higher-level views of tracing larger than it is for metrics.
You're going to pay a high cost for that in a variety of ways: unnecessary incidents, extra time spent debugging incidents, generally higher monetary costs due to running infra inefficiently, etc.
posts.key_insight: Distributed tracing becomes high-ROI infrastructure when trace data is structured for aggregation and querying rather than just individual trace viewing - a transformation that requires modest engineering effort, not Facebook-scale resources.

posts.summary: Car manufacturers optimize their vehicles for specific crash test benchmarks rather than overall safety, as revealed when IIHS added small overlap crash tests in 2012 and 2018. When these new tests were introduced, only Volvo scored well without modifications—nearly all other manufacturers had poor initial scores but quickly improved them once the tests became public benchmarks. This pattern mirrors benchmark gaming in other industries and suggests most carmakers prioritize cost savings over safety improvements that aren't measured by official tests.
key_points: When IIHS added driver-side small overlap tests in 2012, only Volvo scored well without modifications; most manufacturers had poor scores despite small overlap crashes causing ~25% of vehicle fatalities
After driver-side tests were added, manufacturers improved driver-side safety but neglected passenger-side until IIHS added passenger-side tests in 2018
Most crash test dummies are based on 1970s male proportions, leading manufacturers to optimize for male safety; systems like whiplash protection often work for men but not women
Real-world fatality data is noisy and difficult to interpret due to confounding factors, making crash test scores valuable despite their limitations
Tesla's safety reputation in tech circles contrasts with their mediocre test scores and defensive PR responses to criticism
money_quotes: Any time you have a benchmark that gets taken seriously, some people will start gaming the benchmark.

Despite the importance of small overlap crashes, from looking at the results when the IIHS added the driver-side and passenger-side small overlap tests in 2012 and 2018, it looks like almost all car manufacturers were optimizing for benchmark and not overall safety.

Although automotive companies can, in some cases, be held liable for unsafe designs, just optimizing for a small set of benchmarks, which must've resulted in extra deaths over optimizing for safety instead of benchmark scores, isn't something that engineers or corporations were, in general, held liable for.
Improving benchmark scores by cheating on benchmarks is much cheaper (and therefore higher ROI) than improving benchmark scores by actually improving the product.
We should have pretty strong priors that there was no 2014 model vehicle that was so safe that the probability of being killed in a car accident was zero.
posts.key_insight: Manufacturers systematically optimize for known benchmarks rather than actual safety, revealing that without continuous introduction of new tests covering previously unmeasured scenarios, safety improvements stagnate in areas that don't affect public scores.

posts.summary: Brooks's 1986 'No Silver Bullet' essay claimed that at least half of programming complexity is 'essential' (inherent to the problem) rather than 'accidental' (tool/technology-related), limiting possible productivity gains to at most 2x. This argument fails because it requires accurately predicting all future innovations—something Brooks demonstrably failed at, dismissing languages, AI, testing tools, and hardware improvements that later yielded massive gains. The essential/accidental complexity distinction is meaningless because problems with too much accidental complexity wouldn't even be attempted, making the 'essential' fraction effectively unbounded as tools improve.
key_points: Brooks underestimated every category of improvement he analyzed—languages, AI, testing, version control, and hardware all delivered far more than the 2x total improvement he claimed was possible

The concept of 'essential complexity' is flawed because it only accounts for problems people can imagine solving with current tools—as accidental complexity decreases, entirely new problem spaces become tractable
Modern development tasks (like querying 100TB of metrics data or parallel log processing) are still 90%+ accidental complexity despite orders-of-magnitude improvement since 1986
Brooks's predictions mirror historical patterns of elders claiming 'everything that can be done has been done'—from assembly programmers in 1955 to Cliff Stoll's 1995 internet dismissal
Hardware speed improvements enable higher-level tools (like ggplot) that would be impossible on slower machines, creating a virtuous cycle of reduced accidental complexity
money_quotes: Brooks couldn't conceive of anyone building a project as complex as Win2k, which could really utilize faster hardware. Of course, using the tools and practices of Brooks's time, it was practically impossible to build as project as complex as Win2k

I've personally never worked on a non-trivial problem that isn't completely dominated by accidental complexity, making the concept of essential complexity meaningless on any problem I've worked on that's worth discussing
The limit on how much accidental complexity Brooks sees is really a limit of his imagination, not something fundamental
Long before computers were invented, elders have been telling the next generation that they've done everything that there is to be done and that the next generation won't be able to achieve more

Faster computers both reduce the amount of accidental complexity tool users run into as well as the amount of accidental complexity that tool creators need to deal with, allowing more productive tools to come into existence
posts.key_insight: The distinction between essential and accidental complexity is fundamentally flawed because it can only account for problems imaginable with current technology—as tools improve, problems once deemed impossible become routine, revealing that nearly all complexity in practical programming work is accidental.

posts.summary: The post argues that measurement and benchmarking projects are systematically undervalued compared to building software, despite often having higher ROI and broader impact. Luu demonstrates this through numerous examples where systematic measurement revealed widespread flaws—from Kyle Kingsbury's Jepsen work exposing catastrophic data loss in distributed databases to Consumer Reports' testing forcing improvements in car safety features. The root cause is that without rigorous third-party measurement, markets fail to select for quality because consumers can't distinguish good products from bad ones, and manufacturers optimize for appearance and marketing rather than actual performance.
key_points: Measurement projects often have higher ROI than building projects because measurement is undervalued and neglected
Kyle Kingsbury's Jepsen work found critical data loss bugs in nearly every distributed system tested, forcing the industry to take correctness seriously
Systematic measurement has driven improvements across many domains: keyboard latency, vehicle safety, tire performance, and computer UI responsiveness
Most product reviews are compromised either by dependence on manufacturer review copies or by SEO/affiliate incentives, making independent measurement crucial
Markets don't naturally select for quality products because consumers can't distinguish good from bad without reliable measurements
money_quotes: If anything, because measurement is, like writing, not generally valued, it's much easier to find high ROI measurement projects than high ROI building projects.

I find that it's generally true that, in areas where no one is publishing measurements/benchmarks of products, the products are generally sub-optimal, often in ways that are relatively straightforward to fix once measured.
Trusting vendors is not a strategy. We know that vendors will lie and cheat to look better at benchmarks.
I find the juxtaposition of people arguing that the market will generally result in products being good while they themselves buy overpriced garbage to be deliciously ironic.
The typical response that I've seen when a catastrophic bug is reported is that the project maintainers will assume that the bug report is incorrect.
posts.key_insight: Markets systematically fail to produce quality products without rigorous independent measurement because consumers lack the information to distinguish good from bad, creating massive opportunities for high-impact work in unglamorous measurement and benchmarking projects.

posts.summary: Companies at scale should invest in specialized in-house expertise even in areas not traditionally considered 'core' to their business, because these investments pay for themselves many times over through incident mitigation, performance optimizations, and long-term cost savings. Twitter's kernel team and JVM experts are cited as examples where single optimizations (like a 5-15% performance improvement from virtualizing compare-and-swap operations) generated returns far exceeding team costs in perpetuity. The common advice to 'buy vs build' and focus only on 'core competencies' is oversimplified—companies can strategically choose their comparative advantages and develop expertise that becomes core, as Apple did with CPU design.
key_points: Twitter's kernel team helped quickly debug a critical firewall/kernel bug that caused the company's worst outage since 2013
A JVM optimization by Twitter's Scala team reduced service costs by 5-15%, paying for the entire team in perpetuity
At sufficient scale, tiny percentage improvements (0.5% TCO reduction) justify specialized teams indefinitely
'Buy vs build' advice often fails because buying doesn't eliminate the need for in-house expertise to extract value
Companies can arbitrarily choose comparative advantages—Apple's CPU design wasn't inevitable before acquiring PA Semi for $278M
money_quotes: If, in the lifetime of the specialist team like the kernel team, a single person found something that persistently reduced TCO by 0.5%, that would pay for the team in perpetuity, and Twitter's kernel team has found many such changes.

The two patches combined reduced the cost of typical major Twitter services using idiomatic Scala by 5% to 15%, paying for the JVM team in perpetuity many times over and that wasn't even the biggest win Flavio found that year.
While it's technically possible to hire yet another firm to be the expert, that's more expensive than developing or hiring in-house expertise and, in the long run, also more risky.

There was nothing about Apple that made the purchase inevitable, that made CPU design an inherent comparative advantage of Apple. But if a firm can pick an area and make it an area of comparative advantage, saying that the firm should choose to concentrate on its comparative advantage(s) isn't very helpful advice.

Companies don't have to get all that large before it starts making sense to have in-house expertise in their operating system, language runtime, and other components that people often think of as being fairly specialized.
posts.key_insight: The conventional wisdom to outsource non-core functions and focus on comparative advantage ignores that at scale, companies can profitably develop deep expertise in 'specialized' areas like kernels and JVMs, with single optimizations often paying for entire teams in perpetuity while also choosing what becomes their comparative advantage.

posts.summary: Luu argues against generic "learn what I learned" career advice, advocating instead for identifying a few skills that match your personal aptitudes and learning them in environments with world-class experts. Rather than being a broad generalist or following popular recommendations (like learning Haskell or compilers), he suggests focusing on amplifying strengths through deliberate practice in conducive environments. He uses his own experience with skills like "having an adversarial mindset" and "looking at data" to illustrate how specialization and good learning environments trump breadth or fashionable technical choices.
key_points: Most career advice amounts to "learn what I learned" without considering individual aptitudes or contexts, making it generally useless
Specialization and amplifying strengths (the "few tricks" approach) is often more effective than being a broad generalist
The best skills to learn are those you have a relative aptitude for and can't help but apply, not what's trendy or what worked for others
Learning environments matter enormously - working with world-class experts accelerates skill development far more than books or internet advice
Finding suitable skills often requires trial and error rather than having a predetermined long-term career strategy
money_quotes: When people said things like 'Forth is very productive', what I suspect they really meant was 'Forth makes me very productive and I have not considered how well this generalizes to people with different aptitudes or who are operating in different contexts'.

I think that people who are really good at something have too many bits of information in their head about how to do it for that information to really be compressible into a book, let alone a blog post.

A random walk will only move you sqrt(n) in some direction whereas a directed walk will move you n in some direction... but I think that, for many people, that advice implicitly underestimates the difficulty of finding an area that's suited to you.
Look for things you can't help but do that most other people don't seem to do.
The environment, how you spend your day-to-day, is inherently local.
posts.key_insight: Effective skill development comes from identifying your natural aptitudes through trial and error, then deliberately practicing those specific skills in environments surrounded by world-class experts—not from following generic advice about what technologies or fields to learn.

posts.summary: Dan Luu argues that willingness to look stupid is a significant advantage in learning and professional development. By not filtering thoughts or questions based on social acceptability, he's able to explore unconventional ideas that others dismiss, ask clarifying questions that deepen understanding, and pursue high-ROI opportunities that seem too foolish for others to attempt. While this approach causes regular social friction—from condescending interviewers to dismissive medical professionals—the compounding benefits of faster learning and access to overlooked opportunities far outweigh the costs of damaged reputation in individual interactions.
key_points: Asking "stupid" questions that challenge seemingly obvious but incorrect explanations leads to much deeper understanding over time
People who avoid looking stupid often blame external factors for failures rather than learning from mistakes, which severely limits their improvement
Pursuing ideas that sound too stupid for others to try often yields high-ROI opportunities with little competition
Most social costs of looking stupid are actually benefits in disguise—filtering out incompatible relationships and revealing who values substance over appearances
The pattern of avoiding stupid-sounding thoughts often operates unconsciously, preventing people from even considering unconventional ideas that might be correct
money_quotes: If I try to solve some a problem by doing what everyone else is doing and go looking for problems where everyone else is looking, if I want to do something valuable, I'll have to do better than a lot of people, maybe even better than everybody else if the problem is really hard.

Often, the dumb solution is something any idiot could've come up with and the reason the problem hasn't been solved is because no one was willing to think the dumb thought until an idiot like me looked at the problem.

I would regularly figure out that the seemingly obvious reason was wrong and then ask a question to try to understand the subtler reason, which sounded stupid to someone who thought the seemingly obvious reason was correct.

Most people seemed more interested in making an excuse to avoid looking stupid (or maybe feeling stupid) in the moment than actually improving, which, of course, resulted in them having many more moments where they looked stupid.
Most people I talk to have a real aversion evaluating stupid thoughts and (I'm guessing) also to having stupid thoughts.
posts.key_insight: Willingness to look stupid is a competitive advantage because most high-value opportunities require exploring ideas that sound too foolish for status-conscious people to consider, making these opportunities both valuable and accessible to anyone willing to ignore social proof.

posts.summary: Dan Luu argues that improving productivity and velocity is valuable despite common objections that working on the right thing matters more than speed, or that specific tasks don't consume enough time to optimize. He demonstrates through concrete examples how faster execution creates compound benefits: it enables more experimentation to find the right problems, allows long-shot ideas to become feasible, and creates phase transitions where entire workflows change. Contrary to the moral arguments against productivity, Luu shows that being more productive actually enables more leisure time and personal fulfillment.
key_points: Velocity and picking the right problem are force multipliers for each other, not competing priorities
Speed improvements create non-linear phase transitions that fundamentally change what's possible (e.g., multi-quarter team projects become single-day MVPs)
Common 'X doesn't matter' arguments (typing speed, coding speed) don't hold up when you actually measure where time goes
Faster execution allows experimentation with long-shot ideas that would be infeasible at slower speeds
Productivity gains can fund more leisure time rather than just more work
money_quotes: I find this a bit funny since I'm not a naturally quick programmer. Learning to program was a real struggle for me and I was pretty slow at it for a long time. My 'one weird trick' is that I've explicitly worked on speeding up things that I do frequently and most people have not.

It is commonly accepted, verging on a cliche, that you have no idea where your program spends time until you actually profile it, but the corollary that you also don't know where you spend your time until you've measured it is not nearly as accepted.

There are 'phase changes' as you cross certain thresholds where your entire way of working changes... if you can do an experiment in an interactive UI by dragging on a slider and see the result in a fraction of a second, at that point it's very 'no filter', if you want to try something you just do it.
If someone thinks it's important to spend time with friends and family, an easy way to do that is to be more productive at work and spend less time working.

I spend roughly half my writing time typing. If I typed at what some people say median typing speed is (40 WPM) instead of the rate some random typing test clocked me at (110 WPM), this would be a 0.5 + 0.5 * 110/40 = 1.875x slowdown.
posts.key_insight: Velocity improvements create non-linear returns by enabling phase transitions that fundamentally change what work is feasible—turning multi-quarter team projects into day-long experiments and making long-shot ideas economically viable to pursue.

posts.summary: Dan Luu catalogs significant errors from his blog posts between 2013-2024, classifying them into categories like insufficient thought, naivete, insufficient verification, blunders, and confusing explanations. He draws a subjective line between 'serious' errors that materially affected his arguments versus minor typos, noting that many major errors came from his first three months of blogging. The post exemplifies his commitment to transparency and intellectual honesty, treating corrections as an ongoing process rather than a one-time exercise.
key_points: Many major errors occurred during the first three months of blogging in 2013
Errors range from insufficient thought and naivete about software to arithmetic blunders and misreading sources
The classification system is subjective and categories aren't mutually exclusive
Some errors stemmed from rushing last-minute edits before publication
The list is expected to grow over time as new errors are discovered or made
money_quotes: the difference isn't that those companies don't produce bad (unreliable, buggy, slow, etc.) software, it's that product/market fit and network effects are important enough that it frequently doesn't matter that software is bad
A list that included everything, including typos would both be uninteresting for other people to read as well as high overhead for me

I distinctly remember not being sure what nostaglebraist was saying and originally agreed with the correct interpretation. After re-reading it, I came away with my mistaken reading, which I then wrote into my post. That I had uncertainty about the reading should've caused me to just reproduce his analysis
I view it as a good sign that a large fraction of the list is from my first three months of blogging, in 2013, but that's no reason to get complacent
posts.key_insight: Maintaining a public corrections log demonstrates intellectual honesty while revealing how many errors stem not from lack of knowledge but from rushing under time pressure or failing to verify when uncertainty exists.

posts.summary: Luu argues that while process and incentives are important management tools, company culture has a measurably large impact on outcomes like productivity, quality, and individual skill development. He draws on his experience at Centaur, a high-velocity CPU company with minimal process but exceptional results, to show how cultural values around ownership, debugging rigor, and caring about quality can produce dramatically better outcomes than heavyweight processes at big tech companies. Culture is transmitted both to employees (who absorb the values of their environment) and carried forward when they leave, making it an underrated factor in career choices.
key_points: Companies have three major tools to influence behavior: incentives, process, and culture, with culture being systematically underrated despite measurable impact
Cultural attitudes determine what people believe is possible, affecting velocity, debugging skills, and willingness to own problems outside their mandate
Centaur achieved under one serious production bug per year with ~100 engineers and minimal process, primarily through a culture of ownership and caring about quality
Big tech companies have countless high-impact problems going unsolved because cultural norms discourage cross-team ownership and problem-solving
People absorb the cultural values of their environment more than they expect, making workplace culture an important but underrated career consideration
money_quotes: Almost none of us are operating at anywhere near our capacity and what we think we can achieve is often a major limiting factor.
You can't shake a stick without hitting a problem that's worth more to the company than my expected lifetime earnings and it's easy to knock off multiple such problems per year.
In eight years at Centaur, I only found one trivial problem whose fix was worth more than I'll earn in my life because, in general, problems would get solved before they got to that point.
Very few people are ok with everyone around them looking at them like they're an idiot for having the wrong values.
The 'one weird trick' was onboarding, not hiring.
posts.key_insight: Culture shapes what people believe is possible and what they care about, producing measurable differences in velocity and quality that often dwarf the impact of formal processes and incentive structures.

posts.summary: Organizations systematically fail by treating individuals as fungible units rather than recognizing that individual effectiveness varies by orders of magnitude. This manifests in roadmap planning where teams privately know projects will fail based on who's assigned, in HR systems that force headcount-based budgeting preventing hiring of exceptional talent, and in attrition management where 'normalizing' turnover disproportionately drives out the most effective employees. The cost of this legibility-driven approach is enormous in heavy-tailed distributions of talent, where the right individual can single-handedly increase a giant company's revenue by 0.7% or solve decades-old problems in foreign aid work.
key_points: Roadmap planning treats people as interchangeable, but teams privately predict project success/failure based on who's actually assigned
HR systems convert budgets into fixed 'headcount' at standardized compensation levels, preventing hiring of highly effective or specialized talent
Companies that artificially increase attrition to 'healthy' levels lose their most effective engineers at 2x+ the average rate
High-impact interventions (in both tech and development work) are often not amenable to simple quantitative analysis and depend heavily on team composition
Prioritizing legibility and scalable processes over recognizing individual variance is extremely costly in heavy-tailed distributions of talent
money_quotes: I've never worked in an organization where this actually worked, where this actually enabled teams to effectively execute with dependencies on other teams.

People, as a whole, cannot be treated as an abstraction where the actions company leadership takes impacts everyone in the same way. The people who are most effective will be disproportionately likely to leave if you turn a knob that leads to increased attrition.
Unfortunately for those people, as well as for the world, the world is not simple.

When ROI is a heavy-tailed distribution, when the right person can, on their paternity leave, increase company revenue of a giant tech company by 0.7% and then much more when they work on that full-time, then severely tamping down on the right side of the curve to improve legibility is very costly and can cost you the majority of your potential returns.
Many people want the world to be simple and apply the algorithm 'charity on GiveWell list = good; not on GiveWill list = bad' because it makes the world simple for them.
posts.key_insight: Treating individuals as interchangeable for organizational legibility and scalability systematically destroys value in systems with heavy-tailed talent distributions, where single exceptional individuals can create orders of magnitude more impact than average performers.

posts.summary: Twitter's CPU-bound services were failing at around 50% container CPU utilization due to the Linux CFS scheduler's throttling mechanism. When services with large thread pools exceeded their reserved cores, the scheduler would let them burst above quota then throttle them to maintain average limits, causing severe tail latency degradation and forcing massive overprovisioning. Solutions included better thread pool defaults, the Finagle Offload Filter for separating IO and application work, and a kernel patch preventing containers from exceeding quotas instantaneously rather than on an amortized basis, yielding 15-60% cost reductions and eliminating throttling death spirals.
key_points: Twitter services were falling over at 50% CPU utilization despite being CPU-bound, due to CFS bandwidth control throttling
Services commonly had 10-100+ runnable threads per reserved core, causing quota exhaustion and severe throttling
The throttling problem created metastable death spirals where throttled shards accumulated work and throttled even harder
Thread pool tuning for individual services like service-1 enabled 1.5-2x capacity increases worth millions annually
Cross-fleet solutions included Finagle Offload Filter for proper thread separation and a kernel patch preventing instantaneous quota violations
money_quotes: Because load isn't, in general, evenly balanced across shards and the shard-level degradation in performance is so severe when we exceed 50% CPU utilization, this makes the practical limit much lower than 50% even during peak load events.
A subsecond stop-the-world GC pause could take many seconds of wallclock time to complete.
In the worst case, a highly parallel application could exhaust all of its quota in the first bandwidth slice leaving 95ms of throttled time before any thread could be scheduled again.

What happens is that, when a service is under high load, it will throttle. Throttling doesn't prevent requests from coming into the shard that's throttled, so when the shard wakes up from being throttled, it has even more work to do than it had before it throttled, causing it to use even more CPU and throttle more quickly.

A service that's highly loaded and throttling toggles between attempting to use all of the cores on the box and then using none of the cores on the box, causing an extremely large amount of interference during the periods where it's attempting to use all of the cores on the box.
posts.key_insight: Container CPU throttling based on amortized quotas creates a fundamental mismatch between how developers reason about parallelism and how the scheduler enforces limits, resulting in catastrophic underutilization and metastable failure modes that can only be fixed by preventing instantaneous quota violations rather than amortized ones.

posts.summary: This post examines systematic measurement problems in Twitter's latency monitoring infrastructure that cause service owners to drastically underestimate real user-experienced latency. Luu identifies three critical issues: opaque latency hidden in uninstrumented parts of the stack (netty, kernel) that can be 15x higher than reported metrics, lack of proper cluster-wide aggregation leading to misleading averages of per-shard tail latencies, and minutely-resolution metrics that miss sub-second latency spikes. These measurement gaps force Twitter to overprovision servers and maintain stricter-than-necessary SLOs to compensate for the uncertainty, directly increasing operating costs.
key_points: Server-side latency measurements miss significant queuing delays in netty and the Linux kernel, causing 99th percentile latency to appear 15x lower than actual client-observed latency
Taking averages of per-shard tail latencies defeats the purpose of monitoring tail latency and fails to detect when individual bad nodes impact overall cluster performance
Minutely metrics resolution misses sub-second latency spikes that cause real incidents, requiring specialized tools to debug
These measurement problems force companies to overprovision infrastructure with large safety margins, significantly increasing operating costs
money_quotes: If we look at 99%-ile latency, we can see that it's ~16ms when measured at the server and ~240ms when measured at the client, a factor of 15 difference.
Taking the average of per-shard tail latency defeats the purpose of monitoring tail latency.
Reported p99 latency is 0.37ms, but actual p99 latency is ~580ms, an over three order of magnitude difference.

Due to large margins of error in our estimates of cluster-wide latencies, we have to provision a very large amount of slack and keep latency SLOs that are much stricter than the actual latencies we want to achieve to avoid user-visible incidents.
There is, in general, no per-shard percentile or other aggregation of per-shard tail latencies that can reconstruct the cluster-level tail latency.
posts.key_insight: Common latency measurement practices—sampling at the wrong layer, averaging tail metrics, and low-resolution sampling—can underestimate real latency by orders of magnitude, forcing expensive overprovisioning to compensate for fundamentally broken observability.

posts.summary: Dan Luu argues that writing advice framed as "how to write" is typically just "write how I write" in disguise, which fails because successful writing styles vary enormously across dimensions like length, tone, detail level, and structure. He demonstrates this by comparing Joel Spolsky, Paul Graham, Steve Yegge, and Julia Evans—all highly successful but with fundamentally different approaches. Rather than copying others' superficial techniques (which usually backfires), writers should start from their own goals and aptitudes, then develop processes and styles that serve those goals.
