key_points: Successful programming bloggers have vastly different styles across topic selection, length, prose style, humor, technical detail, and evidence presentation
Most writing advice fails to capture what actually makes someone's writing work, similar to how dance instructors' verbal cues often don't match what they actually do
Copying superficial aspects without understanding underlying principles leads to poor results—people copy the wrong things
Writing style should emerge from your goals and what you're good at, not from imitating others
What's popular isn't necessarily what you should optimize for—different goals require different trade-offs
money_quotes: Copying what's worked for someone else is unlikely to work for you, making 'write how I write' bad advice.
Most writing advice similarly fails to capture what's important... most people copy entirely the wrong thing.
A wide variety of things can work decently well. Though, if popularity is the goal, then I've probably made a sub-optimal choice on length compared to Julia and on prose style when compared to Paul.

The more general issue is that a person who doesn't understand the thing they're trying to copy will end up copying unimportant superficial aspects of what somebody else is doing and miss the fundamentals that drive the superficial aspects.
You should start from your goals and what you're good at and go from there, not listen to somebody who's going to tell you to write like them.
posts.key_insight: Effective writing emerges from aligning your process and style with your specific goals and aptitudes rather than copying successful writers' superficial techniques, which inevitably fails because it misses the underlying principles that make their approach work for them.

posts.summary: This post documents a decade of severe cache-related incidents at Twitter (2012-2022), revealing that cache was historically the #1 source of site outages. The incidents demonstrate how cache failures cascade through distributed systems due to positive feedback loops, client misbehavior, and infrastructure issues. Most major outages weren't caused by cache logic bugs, but rather by poorly mitigated interactions between cache and surrounding infrastructure—kernel networking issues, hardware problems, aggressive client retry policies, and resource exhaustion under load.
key_points: Cache was Twitter's #1 source of major outages during the early years, with restarting a single cache server causing SEV-0 incidents
Most cache incidents resulted from positive feedback loops and cascade failures rather than cache software bugs
Client retry policies and connection management frequently overwhelmed cache servers, causing site-wide outages
Low-level systems issues (IRQ affinity, firmware bugs, kernel networking, memory fragmentation) repeatedly caused or exacerbated major incidents
Many incidents occurred because fixes from previous incidents were incompletely applied or operational concerns weren't prioritized
Knowledge about past incidents decays rapidly at tech companies, with incorrect viral stories often replacing accurate accounts
money_quotes: Every single incident so far has at least mentioned cache. In fact, for a long time, cache was probably the #1 source of bringing the site down for a while. In my first six months, every time I restarted a cache server, it was a SEV-0 by today's standards.
There's a kind of Gresham's law of stories in play, where incorrect stories tend to win out over correct stories.

Cache failure modes are also interesting because, when cache is used to serve a significant fraction of requests or fraction of data, cache outages or even degradation can easily cause a total outage because an architecture designed with cache performance in mind will not (and should not) have backing DB store performance that's sufficient to keep the site up.

When reading the incidents below, it may be helpful to read them with an eye towards how cache interacts with things above cache in the stack that call caches and things below cache in the stack that cache interacts with.

Clients should 'do the math' when setting retry policies to avoid using retry policies that can completely overwhelm cache servers when 100% of responses fail and maximal backpressure is being applied.
posts.key_insight: Most distributed systems failures in production aren't caused by logic bugs but by insufficiently mitigated positive feedback loops triggered by performance anomalies in the infrastructure stack, compounded by institutional knowledge loss that causes teams to repeatedly encounter similar failure modes.

posts.summary: Luu argues that people with superficial knowledge of complex fields routinely propose simplistic solutions to hard problems, failing to recognize the hidden complexity they don't understand. He demonstrates this with examples from civil engineering, computer architecture, and physics, showing how fields contain orders of magnitude more complexity than outsiders (or even insiders taking a high-level view) typically assume. The root cause is people assuming a topic's full complexity matches their limited understanding of it, rather than recognizing their knowledge gaps.
key_points: People with cocktail-party level knowledge regularly propose solutions to complex problems without understanding the sub-problems that need solving
Programmers frequently make incorrect assumptions about 'traditional' engineering fields, claiming their own field is uniquely complex while others are simple and predictable
Fields like geotechnical engineering contain more knowledge than existed in all of Roman engineering combined, yet outsiders assume modern construction is just 'building bigger things'
Even experts within a field can fall into this trap when taking too abstract a view that elides critical details
The problem stems from people assuming a field's complexity matches their understanding of it, rather than recognizing hidden depth
money_quotes: People who have a superficial understanding of topic assume that the topic is as complex as their understanding of the topic instead of realizing that only knowing a bit about a topic means that they're missing an understanding of the full complexity of a topic.

It's easy to come up with cocktail party solutions to problems by not understanding the problem, assuming the problem is artificially simple, and then coming up with a solution to the imagined problem.

Only a lay person could look at Roman engineering and modern civil engineering and think 'Romans built things and we build things that are just bigger and more varied; a Roman engineer should be able to understand how we build things today because the things are just bigger.'

Someone with a math degree will have a bunch of explicit knowledge in their head, but the important stuff for being able to understand the book isn't the explicit knowledge, but the general way one thinks about math.
The first principle is that you must not fool yourself—and you are the easiest person to fool.
posts.key_insight: The Dunning-Kruger effect manifests not just as overconfidence in one's abilities, but as a failure to recognize that fields contain hidden layers of complexity that become visible only through deep engagement—expertise reveals not just answers but previously invisible questions.

posts.summary: Dan Luu argues that talent identification in tech (and other fields) is deeply flawed, relying on irrelevant surface characteristics like height, confidence, and social class markers rather than actual performance. Drawing parallels to baseball's decades-long resistance to data-driven scouting despite measurable performance metrics, he demonstrates that tech companies perform even worse at talent assessment since individual performance is much harder to measure. Through examples ranging from baseball scouts to university admissions to corporate promotions, he shows how gatekeepers consistently pattern-match on appearance and background rather than capability, creating systemic bias that filters out talented people who don't "look the part." Despite this pessimistic systemic view, he argues individuals and small teams can gain significant advantages by ignoring these irrelevant criteria and focusing on actual output.
key_points: Baseball teams spent a century using irrelevant criteria like height and race for talent scouting despite having easily measurable performance data
Tech companies have even worse talent assessment than sports because individual performance is much harder to measure and has less direct impact on company success
Multiple studies show bias in hiring based on names, appearance, and background rather than qualifications or performance
Height appears more correlated with success in tech/business than in baseball or chess, suggesting halo effects outweigh any actual cognitive correlation
Gatekeepers often pattern-match on confidence, social class markers, and appearance rather than actual demonstrated ability
Individual companies and teams can gain significant advantages by deliberately ignoring these biases and focusing on actual output
money_quotes: There's something a bit odd about height seeming more correlated to success as a consultant or a programmer than in baseball, where height directly conveys an advantage.

I suspect this is not so different from the CMU professor who admitted a promising student on the spot, it just happens that a lot of people pattern match 'smart teenage boy with a story about why their grades and SAT scores are bad' to 'promising potential prodigy' and 'girl from rural Canada with the top grade in her high school class who hasn't really used a computer before and dresses like a poor person from rural Canada because she's paying for college while raising her younger brother because their parents basically abandoned both of them' to 'homeless person who doesn't belong in engineering'.

If we then look at programming, where it's somewhere between extremely difficult and impossible to accurately measure individual performance and the impact of individual performance on company success is much less direct than in sports, what should our estimate of how accurate talent assessment be?
It's pretty easy to do what Bill Wight (the scout known for recommending 'funny looking' baseball players) did and ignore what other people incorrectly think is important.

The ROI would go way down if the industry as a whole ever started using effective signals when hiring but, for the reasons discussed in the body of this post, I expect progress to be slow enough that we don't really see the amount of change that would make this kind of work low ROI in my lifetime.
posts.key_insight: Tech industry talent assessment is systematically biased toward irrelevant surface characteristics (height, confidence, social class) rather than actual performance, creating both massive inefficiency and opportunity for those willing to ignore conventional proxies and focus on demonstrated output.

posts.summary: This post argues that market efficiency assumptions are often wrong in practice - both for products and services. Through numerous examples (from hiring to data infrastructure to shipping to car safety), Luu demonstrates that companies frequently fail to produce working products, informed consumers struggle to identify quality, and 'buy vs build' decisions are often driven by cultural assumptions rather than rational analysis. The persistence of inefficiency occurs because there's weak selection pressure for quality when buyers can't distinguish good products from bad ones, and because cultural expectations (like 'focus on core competencies') override technical reasoning.
key_points: Market efficiency assumptions fail in practice because buyers often can't distinguish quality products from poor ones, creating 'markets for lemons' across most industries

The 'focus on core competencies' advice often fails because outsourced products and services are frequently broken by design, making in-house solutions cheaper and more reliable despite appearing inefficient
Cultural expectations within and between organizations create enormous inefficiencies - lack of trust drives expensive workarounds like empire-building and unnecessary litigation
Success in markets often comes from marketing and PR rather than quality, as seen with MongoDB's false correctness claims outcompeting technically superior databases
Unusually unreasonable individuals (like Kyle Kingsbury with Jepsen) can shift entire industries toward quality, but this is rare because the system punishes such behavior
money_quotes: Whatever abstract arguments people make, inefficiencies persisted for decades.
I valued men and women equally, and found that because other employers did not, good women economists were less expensive than men.
Despite being widely recommended and the leading product in the space, the product has a number of major design flaws that mean that it literally cannot work.
Without that pressure, most software products basically don't work.

It's rare that people are willing to expend a significant amount of personal capital to do the right thing, whatever that means to someone, but it's even rarer that the leadership of a firm will make that choice and spend down the firm's capital to do the right thing.
posts.key_insight: Markets fail to enforce efficiency when information asymmetry prevents buyers from distinguishing quality, allowing broken products to persist and thrive through marketing while cultural assumptions override rational decision-making at both organizational and individual levels.

posts.summary: Wave, a $1.7B fintech company with 70 engineers, deliberately uses a simple Python monolith architecture instead of trendy microservices. The author argues that for most applications, even at massive scale, simple architectures are more cost-effective and allow engineers to focus on user value rather than managing architectural complexity. Despite the tech industry's obsession with sophisticated architectures, companies like Wave and Stack Overflow have scaled to billions in value using boring, monolithic approaches.
key_points: Wave serves billions of requests monthly with a simple Python/Postgres monolith, proving simple architectures work at scale
The cost of engineering teams dominates system costs for most businesses, making expensive but complex optimizations unnecessary
Tech conferences heavily favor complex microservice architectures despite monoliths being effective for most use cases
Complexity should only be added where it provides clear business value, not because it's fashionable
Some complexity is unavoidable (like data residency requirements and telecom integrations in Africa), so simplicity elsewhere is crucial
money_quotes: Wave is a $1.7B company with 70 engineers whose product is a CRUD app that adds and subtracts numbers.
The cost of our engineering team completely dominates the cost of the systems we operate.
There were more talks on quantum computing (one) than talks on monoliths (zero).
Computers are fast enough that high-traffic apps can be served with simple architectures, which can generally be created more cheaply and easily than complex architectures.
By keeping our application architecture as simple as possible, we can spend our complexity (and headcount) budget in places where there's complexity that it benefits our business to take on.
posts.key_insight: Simple architectures remain undervalued in an industry that fetishizes complexity, even though they enable most companies to scale profitably while keeping engineering focused on delivering user value rather than managing self-imposed architectural overhead.

posts.summary: This post systematically evaluates the prediction accuracy of well-known futurists (Kurzweil, Kaku, Toffler, etc.) by grading their concrete predictions, finding accuracy rates of 3-10% for most futurists. The author contrasts these poor results with more accurate predictors who had deep domain expertise (like Steve Yegge and Bill Gates) and identifies common failure modes: cocktail-party level understanding, blindly extrapolating exponential trends, panacea thinking, and failure to learn from past errors. The key finding is that accurate prediction requires genuine domain expertise and probabilistic thinking, not broad theories or 'big ideas.'
key_points: Most prominent futurists had terrible accuracy rates (3-10%) despite claims of 86%+ accuracy through cherry-picking successful predictions

Common failure modes include: relying on exponential extrapolation without understanding mechanisms, 'rounding up' evidence optimistically, panacea thinking about technologies, and never learning from past errors
Accurate predictors like Steve Yegge (50% accuracy) and Bill Gates had deep domain expertise, access to real data flows, and critically evaluated their own past mistakes
The 'big ideas' approach (using a few theoretical hammers on everything) consistently failed across multiple futurists
High certainty about exponential scaling curves is particularly dangerous - small errors in growth rates compound massively over time
Superficial application of forecasting techniques (like adding probabilities after the fact) doesn't make predictions accurate without deep understanding
money_quotes: Fundamentally, the thing that derailed so many of Kurzweil's predictions is that he relied on the idea of exponential and accelerating growth in basically every area he can imagine
There's a sense in which it isn't too difficult to make correct predictions if you understand the topic and have access to the right data
Instead of doing what most futurists do and focusing on the predictions that worked out without looking at what went wrong isn't such a great way to improve
People who had (relatively) accurate predictions had a deep understanding of the problem and also tended to have a record of learning lessons from past predictive errors

If you take a pile of data, incorrectly interpret it and then round up the interpretation further to support a particular conclusion, throwing a probability on it at the end is not likely to make it accurate
posts.key_insight: Accurate futurism requires deep domain expertise combined with rigorous self-criticism rather than broad theoretical frameworks - most celebrated futurists fail because they apply cocktail-party level understanding with unjustified certainty about exponential trends while never seriously evaluating their past errors.

posts.summary: Through systematic testing of naive queries across Google, Bing, Kagi, Marginalia, Mwmbl, and ChatGPT, this post empirically demonstrates that major search engines now return significantly worse results than a decade ago, dominated by SEO spam, scams, and deliberately fraudulent content. Marginalia, a single-person search engine, often outperforms trillion-dollar companies by returning fewer scams despite having less comprehensive coverage. The findings validate complaints that search quality has degraded substantially, contradicting defenders who claim concerns are overblown.
key_points: Major search engines (Google, Bing, Kagi) consistently returned scam sites, malware, and SEO spam for straightforward queries like 'download youtube videos' and 'ad blocker'
Google's ad-driven business model creates fundamental misalignment with user interests, exactly as Brin and Page warned in their original PageRank paper
Marginalia, built by one person, often provided better results than Google by having fewer scams and more honest 'no result' responses
The incentive structure means scam ad blockers that charge money will always outbid free, superior options like ublock origin for top ad slots
Expert users have developed workarounds for bad search that they don't even notice, similar to reflexively hitting ctrl+s, masking how broken search has become for normal users
money_quotes: In their original Page Rank paper, Sergei Brin and Larry Page noted that ad-based search is inherently not incentive aligned with providing good results: 'we expect that advertising funded search engines will be inherently biased towards the advertisers and away from the needs of the Consumers.'

I suspect what's going on here is that some people have gotten so used working around bad software that they don't even know they're doing it, reflexively doing the modern equivalent of hitting ctrl+s all the time in editors.
Google has also gotten into the action by buying ads that trick users, such as paying for an installer to try to trick users into installing Chrome over Firefox.

the traditional ecosystem has the problem that the system highly incentivizes putting whatever is most profitable for the software supply chain in front of the user which is, in general, quite different from the best result.

Pre-LLM, Google certainly had the resources to index every tweet as well as every human generated utterance on every public website, but they seem to have chosen to devote their resources elsewhere and, relative to its size, the public web appears less indexed than ever.
posts.key_insight: The degradation of search quality is not subjective complaining but empirically demonstrable through simple queries, revealing how profit incentives have corrupted what was once Google's core competency while demonstrating that small, independent alternatives can outperform billion-dollar companies when properly incentivized.

posts.summary: This post analyzes the Quinn Emanuel report on Cruise's handling of a 2023 pedestrian accident where their autonomous vehicle dragged a pedestrian 20 feet after the initial collision. Luu argues that while media coverage portrayed Cruise as either transparently cooperative or deliberately deceptive, the actual 200-page report reveals both better and worse behavior than reported. The core issue wasn't intentional deception but catastrophic leadership failures, poor regulatory understanding, and an adversarial mindset toward regulators—behavior that, ironically, would be considered normal or even above-board at most tech companies, highlighting a dangerous culture clash between "move fast" tech culture and safety-critical systems.
key_points: Cruise employees knew about the 20-foot pedestrian dragging by the morning after the accident but failed to explicitly communicate this to regulators
Leadership fixated on correcting media narrative that the AV caused the accident, leading them to emphasize the hit-and-run driver while omitting discussion of the dragging
Technical issues during video presentations to regulators likely prevented them from seeing the full accident, but Cruise employees didn't verbally explain what happened

The incident revealed fundamental organizational failures: no single person took responsibility, senior leadership missed key meetings, and there was no coordinated crisis response despite 200+ employees involved
Cruise's behavior—passive disclosure, assuming regulators would ask questions, lack of transparency—reflects normal tech company culture but is unconscionable in safety-critical industries
money_quotes: As we previously discussed, it's common for summaries to be wildly wrong, even when they're summarizing a short paper that's easily read by laypeople, so of course summaries of a 200-page report are likely to be misleading at best.
The behavior that's described as unconscionable in the report is not only normal in tech, but probably more transparent and above board than you'd see at most major tech companies

it was a fundamentally flawed approach for Cruise or any other business to take the position that a video of an accident causing serious injury provides all necessary information to regulators and otherwise relieves them of the need to affirmatively and fully inform these regulators of all relevant facts
There was no captain of the ship. No single person or team within Cruise appears to have taken responsibility to ensure a coordinated and fully transparent disclosure of all material facts

You get good at incidents given a steady stream of incidents of varying severity if you have to handle the many small ones. You get terrible at incidents if you can cover up the small ones until a big one happens.
posts.key_insight: The Cruise incident reveals a fundamental incompatibility between standard tech company culture—where passive disclosure and assuming others will ask questions is normal—and the proactive transparency requirements of safety-critical systems, demonstrating that tech companies entering regulated industries need cultural transformation, not just compliance checklists.

posts.summary: Dan Luu examines why people post content on platforms like Twitter/Mastodon ("bad platforms") instead of traditional blogs, despite frequent complaints from readers. He identifies four main reasons: higher engagement (short-form platforms vastly dominate long-form in usage and valuation), community presence (people post where their friends are), lower emotional friction (many creators find it mentally easier to write in short chunks than complete blog posts), and better monetization (especially for video, which generates far more revenue than text). While Luu personally prefers long-form text content, he recognizes that much of the content people complain about wouldn't exist at all if creators were forced to use "better" platforms.
key_points: Short-form platforms like Twitter completely dominate long-form platforms in engagement and valuation (Substack valued at ~$600M vs. multi-billion dollar valuations for short-form platforms)
The friction issue is about emotional barriers to writing, not technical difficulty - many people (including those with ADHD) can produce content in short chunks but struggle with long-form posts
Video monetizes dramatically better than text, enabling in-depth content that wouldn't be financially viable as written articles (example: YouTuber with 50k subscribers earning more than AWS salary)
People unconsciously optimize for engagement over time, posting more high-engagement content and less low-engagement content based on platform feedback
Complaints about platform choice are often just expressions of unhappiness rather than rational critiques of the medium
money_quotes: If I were ten years younger and started doing something online in 2023 instead of 2013, more likely than not, I would've tried streaming before I tried blogging.
CAUSE I CAN'T MAKE A BLOG POST, GOD DAMN IT. I have ADHD... if I try to make a blog post, it'll end up being abandoned and unfinished, as I am unable to edit it into something readable and postable.
The reality is that most of that content wouldn't exist at all if it had to be written up as long-form text instead of as chunked up short-form text or video.

From an engagement or revenue standpoint, posting to short-form platforms totally dominates the kind of writing I'm doing and anyone who care almost at all about engagement or revenue would do the short-form posting instead.
I always just interpret statements like 'IQ has gone down' as 'I am unhappy and have difficulty expressing that' and everybody else going 'Yes so am I' when they concur.
posts.key_insight: Complaints that content should be on a "better" platform fundamentally misunderstand that platform constraints enable rather than prevent most online content creation, and the content wouldn't exist at all if forced into the critic's preferred format.

posts.summary: Luu systematically dismantles the claim that only large tech companies can effectively handle moderation, spam filtering, and fraud detection. Through extensive empirical evidence, he demonstrates that larger platforms consistently perform worse than smaller ones in these areas, despite having vastly more resources. He argues this stems from three core issues: 1) larger platforms attract more sophisticated attacks that overwhelm even advanced ML systems, 2) support quality degrades as companies scale up and optimize for profit over user experience, and 3) scaling makes consensus on content policies impossible. The post includes hundreds of documented examples of failures at companies like Meta, Google, Amazon, and others, showing that theoretical economies of scale don't translate to better outcomes when companies choose not to deploy adequate resources.
key_points: Smaller platforms consistently outperform larger ones on spam/fraud/moderation - from Signal vs WhatsApp to HN vs Reddit vs Facebook
Large companies have resources but choose not to deploy them adequately - Meta could afford 1.6M moderators with one year's profit but doesn't
Support becomes kafkaesque at scale - the only reliable way to get help is going viral or knowing employees internally
Big tech's ML systems miss 95-98% of violating content while also generating significant false positives
Companies use 'economies of scale' rhetoric to argue against antitrust while simultaneously providing worse service than smaller competitors
The problem isn't technical capability but business choices - growth and engagement are prioritized over safety and quality
money_quotes: I don't think it's controversial to say that in general, a lot of things get worse as platforms get bigger. For example, when I ran a Twitter poll to see what people I'm loosely connected to think, only 2.6% thought that huge company platforms have the best moderation and spam/fraud filtering.

If Meta had a version of the internal vision statement of a power company a friend mine worked for ('Reliable energy, at low cost, for generations.') and operated like that power company did, trying to create a good experience for the user instead of maximizing profit plus creating the metaverse, they could've spent the $50B they spent on the metaverse on moderation.

What's really being said is more along the lines of 'we would not be able to generate as many billions a year in profit if we hired enough competent people to manually review cases our system should flag as ambiguous, so we settle for what we can get without compromising profits.'

The most naive and simplistic tricks you might use to put content on the front page work on reddit and don't work on HN. I'm not saying you can't defeat HN's system, but doing so would take a little bit of thought, which is not the case for reddit and Facebook.
At least at the level of resources that big companies choose to devote to moderation, spam, etc., having the larger target and other problems associated with scale dominate.
posts.key_insight: The problem with moderation, spam, and support at large tech companies isn't a technical limitation or lack of resources, but a deliberate business choice to prioritize growth and profit over user safety, compounded by organizational complexity that makes systems opaque even to employees working on them.

posts.summary: Using a simple thought experiment about what vehicles should be allowed in a park, this post demonstrates that it's impossible to get people to agree on moderation policies even in trivial cases. Analysis of survey data reveals that even among homogeneous groups like Hacker News readers, only 11.7% agreed on the 'obvious' answer, with 9,432 distinct opinions expressed by 36,902 respondents. The problem becomes exponentially worse with emotionally-charged issues, where opinion spaces are 'fractally contentious' - people with nearly identical views will fight bitterly over minute differences, making platform-wide moderation policies that satisfy users fundamentally impossible at scale.
key_points: A simple survey about 'no vehicles in the park' produced 9,432 distinct opinions from 36,902 respondents, averaging only 3.9 people per opinion
The most 'obvious' answer was only chosen by 11.7% of respondents, despite being from a homogeneous group (HN readers)
People vastly overestimate how much others will agree with them on what seems 'obvious'
Emotionally-charged issues are fractally contentious - agreement becomes impossible even among people with nearly identical positions
Proposed solutions like 'no moderation' or 'federation' don't solve the fundamental disagreement problem
money_quotes: 36902 people expressed an opinion on what constitutes a vehicle in the park and they came up with 9432 distinct opinions, for an average of ~3.9 people, per distinct expressed opinion.

Many (and probably actually most) people are overconfident when predicting what other people think is obvious and often incorrectly assume that other people will think the same thoughts and find the same things obvious.

Charged issues are fractally contentious, causing disagreement among people who hold all but identical opinions, making them significantly more difficult to agree on than our 'no vehicles in the park' example.
No large platform can satisfy user preferences because users will disagree over what content should be moderated off the platform and what content should be allowed.
The fundamental problem is that users on different servers want different things to be allowed, which then results in no server giving you access to everything you want to see.
posts.key_insight: People dramatically underestimate how much disagreement exists even on seemingly simple rules, and this disagreement becomes fractally worse with emotionally-charged topics, making universal agreement on platform moderation policies mathematically impossible at scale.

posts.summary: Dan Luu demonstrates that while web bloat was previously constrained by slow internet connections, CPU performance for web apps hasn't scaled as quickly as bandwidth, making modern websites increasingly inaccessible to users with low-end devices even with fast internet. Testing popular platforms on devices like the Tecno Spark 8C and Itel P32 reveals that newer platforms (Discourse, Medium, Substack) can be 20-30x slower than older alternatives (MyBB, old WordPress), with many modern sites being completely unusable or crashing browsers on budget devices. The article challenges the widespread developer attitude that CPU speed is "infinite" and that only wealthy users with premium devices matter, noting that globally accessible design remains achievable as demonstrated by older, simpler platforms.
key_points: Modern websites are becoming inaccessible to people with low-end devices despite having high-speed connections, as CPU performance hasn't scaled as quickly as bandwidth
Testing shows older platforms (MyBB, phpBB, old WordPress) are 3-33x faster than modern alternatives (Discourse, Medium, Substack) on low-end devices
Many developers assume CPU speed is infinite and optimize for metrics like LCP rather than actual usability, often gaming metrics without improving user experience
There's a prevalent attitude among developers that non-wealthy users don't matter, despite billions of people worldwide using low-end devices
Simple, static pages with full content loading work far better on low-end devices than modern dynamic loading approaches
money_quotes: In the case of Discourse, a hardware engineer is an embarrassment not deserving of a job if they can't hit 90% of the performance of an all-time-great performance team but, as a software engineer, delivering 3% the performance of a non-highly-optimized application like MyBB is no problem.

It's fairly common for programmers to expect that hardware will solve all their problems, and then, when that doesn't happen, pass the issue onto the user, explaining why the programmer needn't do anything to help the user.
When asked if 100% of users are on iOS, the founder of Discourse says 'The influential users who spend money tend to be, I'll tell you that'.
Many sites that use modern techniques like partially loading the page and then dynamically loading the rest of it tend to be less usable than the scores in the table indicate.

It's still the case that many users don't have broadband speeds, both inside and outside of the U.S. and that much of the modern web isn't usable for people with slow internet, but the exponential increase in bandwidth has outpaced web bloat for typical sites.
posts.key_insight: Developer assumptions that CPU speed is infinite and that only wealthy users matter have created a web where modern platforms can be orders of magnitude slower than decade-old alternatives, making much of the internet unusable for billions of people with low-end devices despite these devices being powerful enough to run 3D games and compile complex codebases.

posts.summary: Dan Luu analyzes leaked internal FTC memos from the 2011-2012 Google antitrust investigation, revealing how the Bureau of Economics (BE) made fundamental errors about the tech industry that contradicted the Bureau of Competition's (BC) case. BE staff argued mobile was unimportant, switching costs were trivial, and Google's anticompetitive practices had minimal impact—positions that were demonstrably wrong at the time and proven incorrect by subsequent events. The investigation was dropped despite BC making a defensible case, highlighting a systemic problem: tech policy decisions are made by people who lack basic understanding of the industry they're regulating.
key_points: The BE memo repeatedly made claims that were obviously false to anyone in tech, such as mobile being unimportant (8% of market) when it was already understood to be the future
BE staff used selective evidence and flawed models, like claiming defaults don't matter because switching takes 'a few taps,' while ignoring that Google paid billions for default status
The FTC gave equal weight to both memos despite BE's fundamental errors, suggesting tech expertise gaps at leadership levels
BE staff's predictions were systematically wrong—they predicted robust competition would continue, but Google's search share grew from ~67% to 86-90%
The lack of tech practitioners involved in crafting remedies and regulation allows companies to easily 'hack' around poorly-designed consent decrees
money_quotes: In order for the BE memo to seem credible, the reader must have large and significant gaps in their understanding of the tech industry.
When the authors of the BE memo make a prediction, they seem to have a facility for predicting the opposite of what will happen.
It's curious that someone could have access to all of these internal documents from these companies as well as interviews and then make the argument that mobile was, at the time, not very important.

We can see, from the history of antitrust in tech going back at least two decades, that courts, regulators, and legislators have not been prepared for the vigor, speed, and delight with which tech companies hack the system.

The BE memo argues that defaults are essentially worthless and have little to no impact, saying multiple times that users can switch with just 'a few taps'... Google paid Apple $26.3B to retain its default status in 2021.
posts.key_insight: Effective tech regulation requires practitioners with hands-on industry expertise who understand how these systems actually work, not just economists and lawyers applying abstract models that contradict observable market behavior and incentives.

posts.summary: Luu examines how discussions about AI/ML bias reveal a double standard compared to 'classical' software bugs, where people frequently deny that outputs opposite to user intent (like turning Asian faces white in professional photos) constitute bugs at all. He argues that encoding biases into automated systems isn't new to ML—it's been happening throughout computing history—but ML has made these biases more legible to lay people. The fundamental problem is that consumer markets reward velocity over quality, making it structurally difficult to address bias even when technical solutions might exist.
key_points: When generative AI produces output opposite to user intent (e.g., converting Asian faces to white faces for 'professional' photos), commenters deny this is even a bug—a response that wouldn't happen with classical software bugs

Bias in automated systems predates ML and exists throughout computing (compression algorithms favoring English, forms rejecting Vietnamese names, etc.) but ML makes it more visible to the general public

Common proposed solutions like 'hire more diverse teams' have failed for decades because: executives don't prioritize it, there are too many dimensions of diversity, and diverse hiring doesn't automatically cause issues to be prioritized and fixed

Consumer markets structurally favor velocity over quality (see: Volvo's safety advantage becoming a market disadvantage), making it difficult for any quality-focused solution to succeed without regulatory changes

The author genuinely doesn't know if the technical problem is solvable, drawing parallels to both impossible-seeming problems (1000x faster CPUs) and problems wrongly assumed impossible (efficient software testing)
money_quotes: AI apologists insist this is fine when it comes to things like changing someone's race or ethnicity...it would be absurd to argue that it's fine for my tire change appointment to have been converted to an oil change appointment because other companies have schedulers that convert oil change appointments to tire change appointments

My feeling is that the 'natural', as in lowest energy and most straightforward state for institutions and products is that they don't work very well. If someone hasn't previously instilled a culture or instituted processes that foster quality in a particular dimension, quality is likely to be poor

We've encoded biases into automation for as long as we've had automation and the increased scope and scale of automation has been and will continue to increase the scope and scale of automated bias. It's just that now, many uses of ML make these kinds of biases a lot more legible to lay people

Companies have overwhelmingly chosen velocity over quality...consumers overwhelmingly choose the lower cost or more featureful option over the higher quality option. Despite vehicular accidents being one of the leading causes of death for people under 50, paying for safety is such a low priority for consumers that Volvo has become a niche brand

Even if execs and capital were bought into the idea, it still wouldn't work because there are too many dimensions. You're lucky if the organization is capable of seriously prioritizing diversity in two or three dimensions while dropping the ball on hundreds or thousands of other dimensions
posts.tone: analytical and methodical with underlying frustration
posts.key_insight: AI bias isn't a new problem but rather the latest manifestation of how automated systems have always encoded societal biases—the real challenge is that market incentives systematically favor shipping fast over fixing these issues, making proposed solutions based on 'caring more' or 'diverse hiring' structurally doomed to fail without addressing the underlying economic forces.

posts.summary: This post explores how a Codenames player could dominate the game by memorizing the 40 setup cards that come with the game, rather than relying on word associations. The author built a simple bot that uses only positional information from these cards and found it beats most human players, demonstrating that the game has an exploitable side-channel vulnerability. Despite this flaw, the author argues Codenames still has better replayability than most word games, which typically break down after just a few plays when players memorize common word associations.
key_points: A teammate once won a Codenames game by guessing based on physical layout patterns rather than word clues, sparking curiosity about how good someone could be using only position information

A bot that simply memorizes the 40 card configurations and doesn't guess until it can win outright beats most human players, even when humans get unrealistic advantages like 3 perfect guesses per turn
Players subconsciously develop intuitions about valid/invalid patterns after playing enough games, creating a side-channel attack even without deliberate memorization
Most word guessing games break down quickly when played seriously - Codenames is better than most due to combinatorial word associations, though still exploitable
The author used AI assistants (Storytell and Cursor) to write the code for this post, finding them surprisingly capable despite frequent bugs that required iteration
money_quotes: This very silly bot that doesn't guess until it can guess everything is much stronger than most Codenames players.

The relevant question isn't 'when will AI allow laypeople to create better software than programmers?' but 'when will AI allow laypeople to create software that's as good as phone trees and crappy voice recognition are for customer support?'

Most software that's in production today has fairly weak performance and correctness constraints. People basically just accept that software has a ton of bugs and that it's normal to run into hundreds or thousands of software bugs in any given week.
From discussions with founders and VCs in this space, almost everyone has taken this to heart [The Bitter Lesson] - they assume huge improvements are coming and avoid mundane engineering work.
I think people should play games however they find it fun and should find a group that likes playing games in the same way.
posts.key_insight: Even seemingly simple games can have exploitable side-channels that reveal the gap between designed and actual gameplay, much like how AI tools are already 'good enough' for many software tasks despite not being anywhere near perfect.

posts.summary: The dominant narrative that Steve Ballmer was a failed CEO who held Microsoft back until Satya Nadella saved the company is fundamentally wrong. While Ballmer faced intense criticism for missing mobile and other trends, he actually oversaw massive revenue growth (from $14-22B to $83B), made crucial long-term bets that define Microsoft today (Azure, Office 365, Bing, Xbox), and built the enterprise sales machine that powers the company. He inherited a company hamstrung by antitrust constraints and brutal internal politics, then cleared both obstacles while subsidizing multi-billion dollar moonshots that critics derided but which positioned Microsoft for decades of success.
key_points: Revenue grew from $14-22B to $83B under Ballmer, with $27B in profit when he left
Ballmer launched Azure (worth ~$1T), Office 365, and built world's most effective enterprise sales org
Even Ballmer's 'failures' like Bing would be worth $240B as standalone companies
He inherited a company constrained by antitrust and internal politics, cleared both for his successor
Microsoft made successful transitions from boxed software to cloud and from software to hardware (Xbox, Surface) that most companies fail at
money_quotes: Even Bing, widely considered a failure, on last reported revenue and current P/E ratio, would be 12th most valuable tech company in the world, between Tencent and ASML.
Microsoft salespeople noted that they would still usually be able to close the sale of Microsoft's paid product even when competing against a Google that was giving their product away.

Much like Gary Bernhardt's talk, which was panned because he made the problem statement and solution so obvious that people didn't realize they'd learned something non-trivial, Ballmer set up Microsoft for future success so effectively that it's easy to criticize him for being a bum because his successor is so successful.

If we look at the broader history of the tech industry, Microsoft has had sustained strong execution from its founding in 1975 until today, a nearly fifty year run, a run that's arguably been unmatched in the tech industry.
Basically, no matter what people think Ballmer is doing, they say it's wrong and that he should do the opposite.
posts.key_insight: Ballmer's real achievement was making massive, heavily-criticized long-term bets that set up trillion-dollar business units and clearing political obstacles so thoroughly that his successor's success made him look incompetent by comparison.
